title: Binary Tree Preorder Traversal Visits Parent Before Children
verdict_evidence: Knuth, TAOCP Vol 1, Section 2.3.1 'Traversing Binary Trees' defines preorder as: visit the root, traverse the left subtree in preorder, traverse the right subtree in preorder. By that definition the root is emitted before any node of either subtree, which is the stated positional inequality. Falsifiable: the sequence produced by inorder on a root with a left child places the child before the root, violating pos(root) < min pos(children) - so the equation genuinely discriminates preorder from the other two orders Knuth defines in the same section.
verification: Build a tree with known structure. Run preorder traversal and record positions. For each non-root node, assert that its parent's position in the output is strictly less than its own. Verify the relationship holds for all three depth-first traversals: preorder (parent first), inorder (parent between left and right), postorder (parent last).

prompt_injection: Postorder traversal (L-R-N) requires that both subtrees are fully processed before the parent node is visited. This invariant is essential for safe deallocation (free children before parent) and for evaluating expression trees to postfix notation. Verify that for every node with children, its output position is strictly greater than all its children's positions.
tensor_equation: ∀ node n ∈ T: pos(postorder, n) > max(pos(postorder, c) | c ∈ children(n))
title: Binary Tree Postorder Traversal Visits Children Before Parent
verdict_evidence: TAOCP Vol. 1 Sec. 2.3.1 ('Traversing Binary Trees') defines the three orders; postorder is 'traverse the left subtree, traverse the right subtree, visit the root', so the root is visited strictly after every node of both subtrees, which is exactly pos(n) > max over children (vacuously true at leaves, where the max is over the empty set). Counterexample showing teeth: preorder on the same tree gives pos(root) = 1 < pos(child), so the equation discriminates between traversal orders rather than holding for all of them; an iterative 'postorder' that pops a parent before its right child is rejected by it.
verification: Build a tree with known structure. Run postorder traversal and record positions. For each internal node, assert that its position is greater than all children's positions. As a stronger check, generate all three traversals from the same tree and verify no node is missed: |preorder| = |inorder| = |postorder| = |nodes(T)|.

prompt_injection: An AVL tree requires that the balance factor of every node — defined as height(right_subtree) - height(left_subtree) — lies in {-1, 0, 1}. After every insertion or deletion, recompute balance factors along the path to the root. If any node's |BF| ≥ 2, trigger the appropriate rotation (LL, RR, LR, RL). The tree is not an AVL tree until this condition holds globally. The height bound h ≤ 1.44 log₂(n+2) must also hold after every operation.
tensor_equation: ∀ node n ∈ T: BF(n) = height(right(n)) - height(left(n)) ∈ {-1, 0, 1}
title: AVL Tree Balance Factor Invariant
verdict_evidence: Knuth, TAOCP Vol. 3 Sec. 6.2.3 'Balanced Trees' (pp. 458ff in the 2nd ed) defines, for each node of an AVL (Adelson-Velsky/Landis) tree, the balance factor B(N) = (height of right subtree) - (height of left subtree), and requires B(N) in {-1, 0, +1} for every node. The axiom's sign convention (right minus left) matches Knuth's exactly. Has teeth: inserting 1, 2, 3 in order without rebalancing gives the root BF = +2, which is precisely the case Knuth's Algorithm A handles by a single left rotation; the bound is what yields height <= 1.4404 lg(n+2).
verification: Insert and delete sequences of random keys. After each operation, walk every node, compute height of left and right subtrees recursively, compute BF = right_height - left_height, and assert BF ∈ {-1, 0, 1}. Also assert that tree height h satisfies h ≤ 1.44 × log₂(n+2). Test with sorted insertion (worst case for unbalanced trees) to verify rotations prevent degeneration.

prompt_injection: Knuth proved that an AVL tree of height h contains at least F_{h+2} - 1 nodes. This gives the worst-case height bound of approximately 1.44 log₂(n+2). If your AVL tree has height exceeding this bound for its node count, the balance factor invariant has been violated somewhere. Use this as a fast sanity check before the more expensive per-node balance factor walk.
tensor_equation: ∀ AVL tree T of height h with n nodes: n ≥ F_{h+2} - 1 ∧ h ≤ 1.44 log₂(n+2) + O(1), where F_k is the Fibonacci sequence with F_1 = F_2 = 1
title: AVL Tree Height Bound via Fibonacci Numbers
verdict_evidence: TAOCP Vol 3 Section 6.2.3 (Balanced Trees) proves that a balanced (AVL) tree of height h has at least F_{h+2} - 1 internal nodes, and derives the height bound h <= 1.4404 lg(N+2) - 0.328, i.e. h <= 1.44 log2(n+2) + O(1). The axiom reproduces both the Fibonacci node-count lemma and the constant 1.44. Falsifiable: a 4-node tree of height 4 (a right spine) has n = 4 < F_6 - 1 = 7, violating it - which is exactly the unbalanced case AVL rotations exclude.
verification: After any sequence of insertions/deletions, measure tree height h and node count n. Compute the minimum node count for height h: min_nodes = fib(h+2) - 1. Assert n ≥ min_nodes. Equivalently, assert h ≤ ceil(log_φ((n+2)/√5 + 1/2) - 1) where φ ≈ 1.618.

prompt_injection: Linear probing places each key in the first empty slot starting from its hash position. The invariant: for any occupied slot i containing key k, every slot between h(k) and i-1 must also be occupied. If a search encounters an empty slot before finding the key, the key is not in the table. When deleting, you must either use tombstones (lazy deletion) or rehash the run after the deleted slot to close the gap. A gap in an occupancy chain silently makes some keys unreachable — this is a data corruption bug.
source: TAOCP Vol 3, Section 6.4 (Hashing), Knuth's 1963 linear probing analysis
tensor_equation: ∀ key k in table T at index i: ∀j ∈ [h(k), i-1]: T[j] is occupied (not empty and not tombstone)
title: Linear Probing Occupancy Chain Invariant (No Gaps)
verdict_evidence: Knuth, TAOCP Vol. 3 Sec. 6.4 (Hashing), Algorithm L (linear probing) and its analysis — Knuth's 1963 note analyzing linear probing is the origin of the load-factor formulas quoted there. Algorithm L's correctness rests exactly on this invariant: the search for k stops at the first empty slot, so if k is stored at i, every slot from h(k) up to i-1 (modulo M) must be occupied. Has teeth: deleting a record by simply blanking its slot creates a gap and makes any key whose probe chain crossed that slot unfindable — which is why Knuth gives Algorithm R, which repairs the invariant by moving records back rather than leaving a hole. The axiom is faithful to Knuth's no-tombstone model; note that lazy-deletion implementations weaken it (a tombstone in the chain is permissible there), and the index interval must be read modulo the table size.
verification: After every operation, for each occupied slot i: let h = hash(key_at_i) % table_size. Walk from h to i-1 (wrapping if needed). Assert no empty slot is encountered. Also verify the converse: if a search starting at h(key) encounters an empty slot before finding the key, assert the key is not in the table. Test with many random insert/delete sequences.

prompt_injection: Linear probing performance degrades as O(1/(1-α)²) when the load factor α approaches 1. Maintain α below a fixed threshold (commonly 0.5). When α exceeds the threshold, allocate a larger table (typically 2×) and rehash all entries. Tombstones count toward α for load-factor purposes — failing to account for tombstones causes silent performance degradation. Knuth's analysis shows that primary clustering causes runs of occupied slots to grow as Θ(1/(1-α)²).
tensor_equation: ∀t: α(t) = occupied_slots(t) / table_size(t) < threshold, where threshold ∈ (0, 1); Knuth showed expected probe count = O(1/(1-α)²) for unsuccessful search
title: Linear Probing Load Factor Bound
verdict_discrepancy: ATTRIBUTION/FIDELITY of the first clause: 'alpha(t) < threshold, where threshold in (0,1)' is an unbound existential - for any alpha < 1 some threshold in (0,1) satisfies it, so the clause carries almost no discriminating power, and Knuth states no normative load-factor limit anywhere in 6.4; he derives asymptotics and leaves the operating point to the implementer. The axiom fuses a genuine Knuth result with an unattributed engineering rule and cites both to Section 6.4.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error
verification: Track occupied slots (including tombstones) and table size. After every insertion, assert load_factor < threshold. Measure the average probe length for successful and unsuccessful searches; assert it does not exceed the theoretical bound (approximately 0.5 × (1 + 1/(1-α)²) for unsuccessful searches). Trigger a rehash when the threshold is crossed and verify the new table satisfies all invariants.

prompt_injection: Knuth defined a B-tree of order m with five properties: (1) every node has at most m children, (2) every non-root non-leaf node has at least ⌈m/2⌉ children, (3) the root has at least 2 children unless it is a leaf, (4) all leaves appear on the same level, (5) a non-leaf node with k children contains k-1 keys. The keys in each node are ordered and serve as separation values: for keys a₁...a_{k-1}, all values in child i are less than aᵢ, all values in child i+1 are greater than aᵢ. Insertion and deletion must maintain all five properties — split when a node overflows (hits m children), merge or redistribute when a node underflows (below ⌈m/2⌉ children).
tensor_equation: ∀ internal node N with k children (k ∈ [⌈m/2⌉, m] for non-root, k ∈ [2, m] for root): N has k-1 keys a₁ < a₂ < ... < a_{k-1} ∧ ∀ subtree Tᵢ rooted at child i: ∀x ∈ T₁: x < a₁, ∀x ∈ Tᵢ (1 < i < k): a_{i-1} < x < aᵢ, ∀x ∈ T_k: x > a_{k-1}
title: B-Tree (Multiway Tree) Ordering and Occupancy Invariant
verdict_evidence: TAOCP Vol 3 Section 6.2.4 is 'Multiway Trees', where Knuth presents Bayer and McCreight's B-tree of order m: every node has at most m children, every node except the root and leaves has at least ceil(m/2) children, the root has at least 2 children unless it is a leaf, and a node with k children holds k-1 keys separating the subtrees. The axiom transcribes both the occupancy bounds and the ordering. Falsifiable on both halves: an order-5 non-root internal node with 2 children violates the ceil(m/2)=3 floor; a node with keys [10,20] whose middle subtree contains 25 violates a_{i-1} < x < a_i.
verification: After every operation, walk the entire tree. For each node: (1) assert children count ∈ [⌈m/2⌉, m] (or [2, m] for root), (2) assert keys count = children_count - 1 (or 0 for leaves), (3) assert keys are strictly increasing, (4) for each key aᵢ, assert all values in left child are < aᵢ and all values in right child are > aᵢ, (5) assert all leaves are at the same depth. Insert sorted keys to test splitting, delete keys to test merging.

prompt_injection: All leaves in a B-tree must be at the exact same depth. This is the invariant that guarantees O(log n) worst-case access time. Insertion grows the tree upward (by splitting the root) rather than downward. Deletion may shrink the tree upward (by merging into a new root). If you ever find leaves at different depths, the tree structure is corrupted. This invariant is tested independently because violations are catastrophic — some keys become unreachable or search terminates prematurely.
tensor_equation: ∀ leaves L₁, L₂ in B-tree T: depth(L₁) = depth(L₂)
title: B-Tree Leaf-Level Uniform Depth Invariant
verdict_evidence: TAOCP Vol. 3 Sec. 6.2.4 is 'Multiway Trees', the section in which Knuth presents Bayer & McCreight's B-trees, and the defining property list includes that all leaves appear on the same level (carrying no information), alongside the at-most-m-children, at-least-ceil(m/2)-children-for-non-root, root-has->=2-children, and k-children-implies-k-1-keys conditions. Counterexample showing teeth: growing a B-tree by attaching a new child below one leaf (as an unbalanced multiway search tree would) makes depth(L1) != depth(L2) and destroys the uniform log_m N probe bound; the property is precisely what forces B-trees to grow at the root rather than at the leaves.
verification: Walk the tree and record the depth of every leaf. Assert all recorded depths are identical. Also verify that the path length from root to any leaf is the same (this is equivalent). After bulk insertions and deletions, recheck. If using on-disk B-trees, check after crash recovery.

prompt_injection: A binary heap must be a complete binary tree: all levels except possibly the last are completely full, and nodes on the last level are filled from left to right. When stored in an array starting at index 1 (Knuth's convention), this gives the parent/child index formulas: parent = ⌊i/2⌋, left_child = 2i, right_child = 2i+1. An array with gaps (null entries between valid elements) violates the shape property. Insertion always appends at the end and sifts up; deletion removes the root, replaces it with the last element, and sifts down.
source: TAOCP Vol 3, Section 5.2.3 (Sorting by Selection), Heapsort
tensor_equation: ∀ levels ℓ ∈ [0, h-1] of heap H: |nodes_at_level(ℓ)| = 2^ℓ ∧ |nodes_at_level(h)| ≤ 2^h ∧ all nodes at level h are as far left as possible
title: Binary Heap Shape Property (Complete Binary Tree)
verdict_evidence: TAOCP Vol 3, Section 5.2.3 'Sorting by Selection' introduces heapsort over an array with the implicit tree structure in which node j has children 2j and 2j+1; that array embedding is exactly the complete-binary-tree shape the axiom states (every level but the last full, last level filled left to right). Falsifiable: an array-backed heap with a hole at index 4 while index 5 is occupied breaks 'as far left as possible' and breaks the 2j/2j+1 addressing that heapsort's siftup depends on. Caveat: Knuth states the shape implicitly through the array indexing rather than as a named property, and his heap is a max-heap (K_{floor(j/2)} >= K_j); the axiom asserts only shape, which is orientation-independent.
verification: For a heap stored at indices [1..n]: verify that for every index i ≤ n/2, child indices 2i and 2i+1 (if ≤ n) contain valid elements. Verify there are no gaps: for each i from 1 to n, heap[i] exists. Verify the last level's fill: the number of nodes at level h must be between 1 and 2^h, and they occupy the leftmost positions.

prompt_injection: In a max-heap, every node's key is greater than or equal to its children's keys. This means the root holds the maximum element. For a min-heap, the inequality is reversed. This property must hold for every non-root node: key[parent(i)] ≥ key[i] (max-heap) or key[parent(i)] ≤ key[i] (min-heap). After insert (sift-up) or delete-min/max (sift-down), verify the property along the modified path, then verify globally. A single violation means extract-min/max would return the wrong element.
source: TAOCP Vol 3, Section 5.2.3 (Sorting by Selection), Heapsort
tensor_equation: ∀ node i ∈ heap H (array representation, 1-indexed): H[i] ≥ H[⌊i/2⌋] for min-heap, or H[i] ≤ H[⌊i/2⌋] for max-heap
title: Binary Heap Order Property (Heap Condition)
verdict_evidence: TAOCP Vol. 3 Sec. 5.2.3 ('Sorting by Selection') introduces heapsort and defines a heap on the array representation by the condition K_floor(j/2) >= K_j for 1 < floor(j/2) < j <= N, i.e. the parent key dominates the child key, which is the axiom's max-heap form H[i] <= H[floor(i/2)]; the largest key therefore sits at K_1. Counterexample showing teeth: array [3, 9, 5] (1-indexed) has H[2] = 9 > H[1] = 3, violating the max-heap direction, and siftup/siftdown must repair it before the selection phase is correct.
verification: For every index i from 2 to n: assert heap[parent(i)] ≥ heap[i] for a max-heap (or ≤ for min-heap). After insert, verify the sift-up path. After extract, verify the sift-down path. Use random sequences of insert and extract operations, verifying the heap property after each operation. Also assert that extract-min/max always returns the root value.

prompt_injection: A sorting algorithm is stable if, whenever two records R and S have equal keys and R appears before S in the input, R also appears before S in the sorted output. Knuth defined this as relative order preservation among equal-keyed elements. Stability is a binary property: an algorithm is either stable (preserves relative order for all equal-key pairs) or unstable (may reorder some equal-key pairs). Verify stability by sorting records where keys are deliberately non-unique and observing whether the original order is preserved within equal-key groups.
source: TAOCP Vol 3, Section 5 (Sorting), Knuth's definition of stable sorting
tensor_equation: ∀ records R, S in input sequence: if key(R) = key(S) ∧ index_input(R) < index_input(S) ⟹ index_output(R) < index_output(S)
title: Sorting Stability Invariant (Equal-Key Relative Order Preservation)
verdict_evidence: Knuth, TAOCP Vol. 3 Sec. 5 (Sorting, Introduction) defines stability: a sorting method is stable if it preserves the relative order of records with equal keys — i.e. records with equal keys appear in the output in the same order as in the input. The axiom's formalization (equal keys, input index order implies output index order) is exactly that definition. Has teeth: quicksort with the standard Hoare/Lomuto partition sorts [(1,a),(1,b),(0,c)] to an output where (1,b) precedes (1,a), a concrete violation; merge sort with '<=' on the merge comparison does not violate it, and flipping that comparison to '<' does.
verification: Create an input array of records with non-unique keys and a secondary payload that encodes the original position (e.g., (key=5, seq=0), (key=3, seq=1), (key=5, seq=2)). Sort by key only. For each group of equal keys, assert that seq values are in increasing order. Repeat for edge cases: all keys equal, no keys equal, array of size 1, array of size 2 with equal keys. This test catches any non-stable sort implementation.

prompt_injection: When implementing a banker's queue (lazy amortized queue), maintain the invariant that the front stream length is always at least as large as the rear stream length. Each snoc increments |R| and each tail decrements |F|. When |R| would exceed |F|, perform a rotation: replace F with F ++ reverse(R) and reset R to empty. Use lazy evaluation (streams) so that the ++ and reverse operations are incremental and their cost is amortized across future tail operations. Allocate one debit per rear element when the rotation suspension is created, and discharge one debit per tail operation to guarantee O(1) amortized time even under persistent usage.
tensor_equation: ∀q ∈ BankersQueue: |q.F| ≥ |q.R| ∧ (|q.F| = 0 ⟹ |q.R| = 0)
title: Banker's Queue Scheduling Invariant (|F| >= |R|)
verdict_evidence: Verified verbatim against Okasaki's thesis (CMU-CS-96-177), Section 3.4.2 'Example: Queues', p.25-26: 'we will rotate the queue whenever R becomes one longer than F, thereby maintaining the invariant that |F| >= |R|. Incidentally, this guarantees that F is empty only if R is also empty.' The BankersQueue structure in Figure 3.3 carries the comment '( Invariants: |F| >= |R|, LenF = |F|, LenR = |R| )'. Both conjuncts of the axiom are stated by the source, at the exact cited thesis section. Falsifiable: a snoc implementation that appends to R without invoking the 'queue' pseudo-constructor produces |R| = |F|+1 and violates the invariant.
verification: In a test harness: (1) For every queue state reachable via public API (snoc, tail), assert len(F) >= len(R). (2) Invariant check that len(F) == 0 iff len(R) == 0. (3) Amortized analysis: execute a sequence of n mixed snoc/tail operations, assert each operation completes in O(1) amortized work. (4) Persistence test: fork from a queue state and execute independent tail sequences; verify memoization prevents re-execution of already-forced suspensions.

prompt_injection: When implementing a real-time queue with worst-case O(1) bounds, maintain the scheduling invariant |S| = |F| − |R| where S is a suffix of the front stream F. Each operation forces the first node of S (executing one incremental step of the suspended rotation), then advances S by one. When S becomes empty (i.e., |R| = |F| + 1), trigger a new rotation by calling rotate(f, r, $Nil) and set both the new F' and new S' to the result. The rotate function interleaves one step of ++ with one step of reverse via an accumulating parameter: rotate($Nil, $Cons(y, $Nil), a) = $Cons(y, a); rotate($Cons(x, f), $Cons(y, r), a) = $Cons(x, rotate(f, r, $Cons(y, a))). This guarantees each suspension has O(1) intrinsic cost and no suspension is forced before its dependencies are memoized.
tensor_equation: ∀q ∈ RealTimeQueue: |q.S| = |q.F| − |q.R| ∧ (|q.S| = 0 ⟺ |q.F| = |q.R|) ∧ rotate(f, r, $Nil) = f ++ reverse(r)
title: Real-Time Queue Scheduling Invariant (|S| = |F| - |R|)
verdict_evidence: Okasaki's real-time (scheduled) queue maintains front f, rear r and schedule s with the invariant |s| = |f| - |r|, which forces |f| >= |r| and makes |s| = 0 exactly when |f| = |r| (the moment a rotation is started). The rotation specification rotate(f, r, a) = f ++ reverse(r) ++ a with a = $Nil gives rotate(f, r, $Nil) = f ++ reverse(r), exactly as stated. Has teeth: dropping the schedule decrement in tail() makes |s| = |f| - |r| + 1, the rotation is not forced far enough ahead, and a single tail() call costs O(n) instead of O(1) worst case.
verification: In a test harness: (1) After every snoc or tail, assert len(S) == len(F) - len(R). (2) Assert invariant that S is a suffix of F (verify by traversing F and checking S appears as a tail). (3) Timing test: execute n operations, assert each individual operation completes in O(1) worst-case (not amortized) time. Measure wall-clock time per operation and check max is bounded by a constant. (4) Rotation trigger test: verify rotation is triggered exactly when |R| = |F| + 1 (S becomes empty), not before.

prompt_injection: When implementing a functional red-black tree, enforce three invariants after every insert: (1) No red node has a red parent — this is the 'red-violation' that must be eliminated. (2) Every path from a node to any descendant leaf contains exactly the same number of black nodes — the black-height invariant. (3) The root is always black. Okasaki's functional insert uses only 4 balance cases (vs 8 in imperative) because pattern matching on the return path naturally covers symmetric cases. The balance function handles these four configurations of a black node whose left or right child is red and that red child's left or right child is also red: (a) left-left: B(T R(T1,a,T2), b, T3) → R(T1, a, T2, b, T3), (b) left-right: B(T1, a, R(T2,b,T3)) → B(T1, a, R(T2,b,T3)) followed by rebalancing, (c) right-left, (d) right-right — each resolved by a single pattern-match clause that produces a balanced tree with a black root and two red children. The insert function returns a tree with a possibly-red root; the caller re-colors the root black.
source: Okasaki, Chapter 3 (book, 1998); Okasaki, JFP 1999 'Red-black trees in a functional setting'
tensor_equation: ∀ node x: color(x) ∈ {Red, Black} ∧ (color(x) = Red ⟹ color(parent(x)) ≠ Red) ∧ ∀ leaves L1, L2 on paths from x: black_height(x→L1) = black_height(x→L2) ∧ color(root) = Black
title: Functional Red-Black Tree Color Invariants
verdict_evidence: Okasaki, 'Purely Functional Data Structures' Section 3.3 (and JFP 9(4):471-477, 1999, 'Red-black trees in a functional setting') states the two invariants as: no red node has a red child, and every path from the root to an empty node contains the same number of black nodes. His insert wraps the recursive ins with a makeBlack that repaints the root, which is where the root-is-black clause comes from. Falsifiable: T(R, T(R, E,1,E), 2, E) has a red node with a red child and is rejected by the balance function's four rotation cases.
verification: In a test harness: (1) After every insert and delete, perform a full tree traversal verifying no red node has a red child. (2) Compute black-height at every leaf; assert all leaves from any given node have identical black-height. (3) Assert root is black. (4) Property-based test: generate random sequences of inserts and deletes, verify invariants hold at every intermediate state. (5) Structural: verify the tree remains a valid BST (in-order traversal yields sorted elements). (6) Height bound: assert tree height h satisfies log₂(n+1) ≤ h ≤ 2·log₂(n+2) − 2.

prompt_injection: When implementing a binomial heap, enforce three invariants: (1) Every binomial tree is heap-ordered: the key of a node is ≤ keys of all children. (2) A binomial tree of rank r is a root node with r children c₁…cᵣ where each cᵢ is a binomial tree of rank r−i. Equivalently, a rank-r tree is formed by linking two rank-(r−1) trees, making the larger root a child of the smaller root. A rank-r tree contains exactly 2ʳ nodes and has height r. (3) The heap contains at most one tree of each rank — corresponding to the binary representation of n. After insertion, trees of equal rank are repeatedly linked (carry propagation). Maintain the list of trees in increasing order of rank; maintain children lists in decreasing order of rank. The link operation compares roots: fun link(t1 as Node(r, x1, c1), t2 as Node(_, x2, c2)) = if x1 ≤ x2 then Node(r+1, x1, t2::c1) else Node(r+1, x2, t1::c2).
tensor_equation: ∀h ∈ BinomialHeap: (∀t ∈ h: heap_ordered(t) ∧ rank(t) = |children(t)|) ∧ (∀i ≠ j, ti,tj ∈ h: rank(ti) ≠ rank(tj)) ∧ (∀t ∈ h, child ci of t: rank(ci) = rank(t) − i for i ∈ {1,…,r}) ∧ (∀p ∈ ancestors(x): key(p) ≤ key(x))
title: Binomial Heap Rank Invariant and Heap Order
verdict_evidence: Verified directly against Okasaki's 1996 thesis (CMU-CS-96-177). Sec. 6.2.2 is titled 'Binomial Heaps' (thesis Ch. 6 = book Ch. 9, Numerical Representations), and Definition 6.2 reads verbatim: 'A binomial tree of rank r is a node with r children c1...cr, where ci is a binomial tree of rank r - i' — exactly the axiom's child-rank clause, and it justifies rank(t) = |children(t)| and |t| = 2^r. Binomial heaps are a collection of heap-ordered binomial trees with at most one tree per rank (the binary-number correspondence that the whole chapter is built on: a heap of size n has a tree of rank i iff bit i of n is set, which forces rank uniqueness). Counterexample showing teeth: a heap holding two separate rank-2 trees, or a rank-3 node with children of ranks 2,1,1, is rejected; so is a node whose key exceeds a child's.
verification: In a test harness: (1) For every tree in the heap, verify heap order: parent key ≤ all child keys recursively. (2) Verify rank uniqueness: no two trees share the same rank. (3) Verify binomial tree structure: for each tree of rank r, its children have ranks r−1, r−2, …, 0 in decreasing order. (4) Size consistency: sum of 2^rank_i over all trees = total node count. (5) After each insert/merge/deleteMin, re-check all invariants. (6) findMin must return the global minimum; deleteMin must produce a heap with the correct remaining elements.

prompt_injection: When implementing a leftist heap, enforce the leftist property: for every node, the s-value (shortest distance to a leaf) of the left child is ≥ the s-value of the right child. Null nodes have s-value 0. A node's s-value = right_child.s_value + 1. During merge(A, B): (1) If A.key > B.key, swap A and B to maintain heap order. (2) Recursively merge B with A.right. (3) After the recursive merge returns, if A.right.s_value > A.left.s_value, swap A.left and A.right to restore the leftist property. (4) Set A.s_value = A.right.s_value + 1. The right spine (path following right children) is always the shortest path to a leaf. This guarantees O(log n) merges since the right-spine length is bounded by log₂(n+1). The merge algorithm recurses only along right spines.
source: Okasaki, Chapter 3 (book, 1998); Crane, 1972 'Linear lists and priority queues as balanced binary trees'
tensor_equation: ∀ node x: s(x) = min{distance to nearest leaf} ∧ s(null) = 0 ∧ (∀ internal x: s(x.left) ≥ s(x.right)) ∧ s(x) = s(x.right) + 1 ∧ s(x) ≤ ⌊log₂(size(x) + 1)⌋
title: Leftist Heap Right-Spine Length Invariant
verdict_evidence: Okasaki, 'Purely Functional Data Structures' (1998) Ch. 3.1 defines the rank of a leftist heap node as the length of its right spine, with rank(E) = 0 and rank(T r a b) = r, and states the leftist property: 'the rank of any left child is at least as large as the rank of its right sibling.' The rank recurrence s(x) = s(x.right) + 1 is exactly how rank is maintained in makeT/merge, and Exercise 3.1 asks the reader to prove the right spine of a leftist heap of size n contains at most floor(log(n+1)) elements — the last conjunct. Okasaki attributes leftist heaps to Crane (1972), matching claimed_source. Has teeth: a node whose left child has rank 1 and right child rank 2 violates the axiom, and merge on it degrades from O(log n) to O(n). Minor definitional wobble: 's(x) = min distance to nearest leaf' coincides with right-spine length only when 'leaf' denotes the empty node E (null path length), as in Okasaki's representation.
verification: In a test harness: (1) For every node, assert s(left) ≥ s(right). (2) For every node, assert s(node) = s(right) + 1 (with s(null) = 0). (3) Verify heap order: every parent key ≤ child keys. (4) Verify s-value bound: for each node with subtree size m, assert s(x) ≤ ⌊log₂(m+1)⌋. (5) Right-spine length test: after merging two heaps of size n, verify right-spine depth is ≤ log₂(n+1). (6) Property-based test: generate random merge sequences, verify all invariants hold.

prompt_injection: When implementing implicit recursive slowdown, maintain the digit invariant that the first 2 is preceded by at least one 0 (and any number of 1s), and any pair of 2s is separated by at least one 0 (and any number of 1s). In the color interpretation: green=0, yellow=1, red=2. The first red level is preceded by at least one green level, and any two red levels are separated by at least one green level. No operation degrades a level from green directly to red — only green→yellow or yellow→red. The fixup procedure finds the first non-yellow level; if it is red, converts it to green and degrades the following level by one step. Consecutive yellow levels are grouped in blocks (Ones of int) for O(1) access. For the implicit (lazy) variant using streams, the digit type becomes simply Zero | One (binary), and laziness handles the carry propagation incrementally: inc($Cons(One, ds)) = $Cons(Zero, inc ds). For queues based on implicit recursive slowdown: a deep queue has F ∈ {One, Two}, R ∈ {Zero, One}, and M is a suspended queue of pairs; snoc on a One rear pairs the elements and recurses into M.
title: Implicit Recursive Slowdown Digit Invariant
verdict_discrepancy: ATTRIBUTION: the invariant belongs to RECURSIVE SLOWDOWN (Kaplan & Tarjan's technique, reviewed in thesis 8.1 / book 11.1 with the green-yellow-red level colouring), not to IMPLICIT recursive slowdown, which is the point of Section 8.2 and which is presented over digits {Zero, One} using lazy evaluation and debit passing - it deliberately drops the segmented 0-between-2s bookkeeping ('significantly simpler than the original'). The title and claimed scope are therefore misassigned. The book citation is also wrong: 'Implicit Recursive Slowdown' is Chapter 11 of the 1998 book; Chapter 10 is 'Data-Structural Bootstrapping'. The third conjunct is redundant (implied by the second).
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error
verification: In a test harness: (1) After every inc/dec operation, scan the digit list and verify: the first 2 (if any) is preceded by at least one 0. (2) Verify no two consecutive 2s without an intervening 0. (3) For the color formulation: no red-red adjacency without a green between them. (4) For queue implementation: verify that F has 1 or 2 elements and R has 0 or 1 elements for all deep queues. (5) Invariant over sequences: execute random sequences of snoc/tail, at each step verify the digit/color invariant holds. (6) Complexity: verify that inc/dec each run in O(1) amortized time — amortized cost bounded by a small constant over any operation sequence.

prompt_injection: When implementing a persistent functional data structure, ensure that every update operation (insert, delete, modify) produces a new version by copying only the nodes on the path from the root to the modification point. All subtrees not on this path are structurally shared between the old and new versions. This is called 'path copying' and is the fundamental mechanism that makes functional data structures persistent without excessive memory use. For a BST: inserting key k into tree T copies O(log n) nodes along the search path; all off-path subtrees are shared. Verify that T remains unchanged and accessible — operations on the old version must produce the same results as before the update. In Standard ML (the language used in Okasaki), this is guaranteed automatically because all values are immutable; there is no assignment operator that can modify an existing node.
source: Okasaki, Section 6.2.1 (thesis); Sarnak and Tarjan, 1986 'Planar point location using persistent search trees'
tensor_equation: ∀ persistent tree T, update op u: let T' = u(T, k, v). ∀ node n ∈ T: (n ∉ path(root_T, insertion_point(k))) ⟹ (n ∈ T' ∧ n shared, not copied). For BST insert: |nodes_copied(T, T')| = depth(insertion_point) + 1.
title: Persistent Structural Sharing (Path Copying) Invariant
verdict_discrepancy: MISATTRIBUTION OF LOCATION: Okasaki's 1996 CMU thesis (CMU-CS-96-177) Chapter 6 is 'Numerical Representations'; Section 6.2.x is about segmented/skew binary number representations, not persistence or path copying. Structural sharing and the copied-path picture appear in Chapter 2 ('Persistence', Sections 2.1-2.2) of the 1998 book, which has no thesis counterpart. Path copying also predates Sarnak & Tarjan - it is due to Myers (1982/1984) and Reps/Teitelbaum/Demers (1983), whom Sarnak & Tarjan cite.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error
verification: In a test harness: (1) After update(v_old) → v_new, verify v_old still accessible and unchanged (all operations on v_old return identical results as before). (2) For tree structures: count nodes in v_new that are reference-equal (pointer-equal, or identity-equal) to nodes in v_old. This count should equal total_nodes(v_old) minus depth(modified_path). (3) Memory test: insert n elements sequentially, keeping all versions; verify total memory is O(n log n), not O(n²). (4) Concurrent access test: from a shared ancestor version, perform different update sequences on different branches; verify each branch sees only its own modifications. (5) For path-copying specifically: after insert(T, k, v), verify that the new root is a new node, the nodes along the search path are new, and all other nodes are identity-equal to nodes in T.

prompt_injection: Verify there are no right-leaning red links in the tree. Traverse every node. If any node has a red right child, the LLRB invariant is violated. A red right child means a 3-node is encoded with the red link leaning right instead of left, breaking the 2-3 tree correspondence that makes operations O(log n).
tensor_equation: ∀n ∈ T: isRed(n.right) → false
title: LLRB: No right-leaning red links
verdict_evidence: Sedgewick & Wayne 4th ed Section 3.3 and algs4.cs.princeton.edu/33balanced define left-leaning red-black BSTs by exactly three restrictions, the first of which is 'red links lean left', i.e. no right-leaning red link. RedBlackBST.java's rotateLeft is invoked precisely when isRed(h.right) && !isRed(h.left). Falsifiable: after inserting a key larger than the current node into a 2-node, the new red link leans right; the invariant is restored only by the subsequent rotateLeft, and the intermediate state is a genuine violation.
verification: Traverse all nodes recursively. For each node, assert: if node.right is not null, node.right.color != RED. This is the first check in Sedgewick's is23() method. A single violation means the tree is no longer a valid LLRB.

prompt_injection: Verify that no node has two red children. In the 2-3 tree correspondence, a node with two red children represents a temporary 4-node. The insertion algorithm creates 4-nodes and then splits them via color flips. If a 4-node persists after all operations complete, the tree no longer corresponds to a valid 2-3 tree.
source: Sedgewick & Wayne 4th ed, Section 3.3; is23() check in RedBlackBST.java
tensor_equation: ∀n ∈ T: ¬(isRed(n.left) ∧ isRed(n.right))
title: LLRB: No node has two red children (no 4-nodes in 2-3 correspondence)
verdict_evidence: Sedgewick & Wayne 4th ed. Sec. 3.3 defines left-leaning red-black BSTs in one-to-one correspondence with 2-3 trees; a node with two red children is a temporary 4-node, which is disallowed in the 2-3 correspondence and is eliminated by flipColors() on the way up from an insertion. algs4's RedBlackBST.java carries an is23() check that rejects any red right link (and two reds in a row), which strictly implies the axiom's condition. Counterexample showing teeth: the intermediate tree produced inside put() after inserting into a 3-node, before flipColors runs, has both children red — so the invariant is a between-operations invariant, and any implementation that fails to flip colors leaves a persistent 4-node that violates it.
verification: Traverse all nodes. For each node, assert: not (node.left != null and node.left.color == RED and node.right != null and node.right.color == RED). This is the second condition in Sedgewick's is23() method.

prompt_injection: Verify perfect black balance: every path from the root to a null link must traverse exactly the same number of black links. This is the fundamental invariant that guarantees O(log n) height. Compute the black-height of the leftmost null path, then verify every other null path has the same black-height. Reject the tree if any path differs.
source: Sedgewick & Wayne 4th ed, Section 3.3; isBalanced() check in RedBlackBST.java
tensor_equation: ∀p₁,p₂ ∈ Paths(root, null): blackCount(p₁) = blackCount(p₂)
title: LLRB: Perfect black balance (all root-to-null paths have equal black-link count)
verdict_evidence: Sedgewick & Wayne 4th ed Section 3.3 defines a left-leaning red-black BST as perfectly black balanced: every path from the root to a null link has the same number of black links. RedBlackBST.java's check() calls isBalanced(), which counts black links on the leftmost path and then verifies every root-to-null path has that same count. Falsifiable: insert into a tree and skip the rotateLeft/flipColors rebalance in put(); a path with 2 black links alongside one with 3 makes isBalanced() return false.
verification: Perform a DFS from root, tracking black-link count. At each null link encountered, record the count. All counts must be equal. In Sedgewick's isBalanced(), black is counted on edges: increment when traversing to a non-red child, skip for red children. Every null link must see the same accumulated black count plus one (null links are black by convention).

prompt_injection: Verify the root node is black. Every insertion and deletion in LLRB ends by setting root.color = BLACK. A red root would mean the black-height accounting is off by one, since every null link must be at the same black distance and root-to-null paths all include the root.
source: Sedgewick & Wayne 4th ed, Section 3.3; root.color = BLACK enforced after every mutation in RedBlackBST.java
tensor_equation: isRed(root) → false
title: LLRB: Root is always black
verdict_evidence: Sedgewick & Wayne 4th ed Sec. 3.3 states the root of a left-leaning red-black BST is always black, and RedBlackBST.java enforces it by executing root.color = BLACK as the last statement of the public put(); the private isRed() helper also returns false for a null root. Has teeth: deleting that line lets a put() that ends in a color flip leave a red root, which breaks the black-height accounting in the class's own check()/isBalanced() assertion.
verification: Assert root == null or root.color == BLACK. Trivial O(1) check. Run after every mutating operation (put, delete, deleteMin, deleteMax).

prompt_injection: Verify the load factor n/m never exceeds the configured upper bound. In Sedgewick's SeparateChainingHashST, the table doubles capacity when n >= 10*m. After any insertion, check that n/m < 10. If this fails, the resize was not triggered or was applied incorrectly. An unbounded load factor degrades search from expected O(1) to O(n).
source: Sedgewick & Wayne 4th ed, Section 3.4; SeparateChainingHashST.java (resize triggers when n >= 10*m)
tensor_equation: ∀t: n / m < α_max, where α_max = 10 in Sedgewick's implementation
title: Hash table with separate chaining: load factor bounded above
verdict_evidence: algs4 SeparateChainingHashST.java (book Sec. 3.4) resizes on the condition 'if (n >= 10*m) resize(2*m);' inside put(), so at the end of every put the invariant n/m < 10 holds; the class also documents an average list length of n/m. Counterexample showing teeth: removing the resize call and inserting 40 keys into the default m = 4 table yields n/m = 10, violating the strict bound and degrading search from constant-ish to linear-in-alpha (Prop. K: search/insert time proportional to n/m under uniform hashing).
verification: After every put(), assert n < 10 * m (where n = key count, m = table size). The implementation guarantees this by calling resize(2*m) when n >= 10*m. A verification test inserts keys until the threshold is crossed and confirms the table size increased.

prompt_injection: Verify that every key in a chain belongs to that chain's bucket index. Iterate each chain (SequentialSearchST). For every key in chain i, confirm hash(key) mod m == i. If a key is in the wrong chain, lookups for that key will miss and the hash table's correctness is broken.
source: Sedgewick & Wayne 4th ed, Section 3.4; each SequentialSearchST chain corresponds to one hash bucket
tensor_equation: ∀k₁,k₂ ∈ T: chain(k₁) = chain(k₂) ⟹ (hash(k₁) mod m) = (hash(k₂) mod m)
title: Hash table with separate chaining: all keys in same bucket share hash index
verdict_evidence: Sedgewick & Wayne 4th ed Section 3.4, SeparateChainingHashST: the table is an array st[] of M SequentialSearchST objects, and every operation indexes st[hash(key)] where hash() returns (key.hashCode() & 0x7fffffff) % M. Hence membership in chain i implies hash index i for every key in that chain. Not vacuous as an implementation invariant: a buggy put() that computes the index before a resize and inserts into the stale bucket, or that appends to st[i+1], places two keys with different hash indices in one chain and falsifies it - this is exactly the class of bug the invariant catches.
verification: For each bucket index i from 0 to m-1, iterate the linked list at st[i]. For each entry's key, assert (key.hashCode() & 0x7fffffff) % m == i. This confirms the structural invariant that chains partition the key space by hash index.

prompt_injection: Verify the trie's prefix property: every key in the subtree rooted at node n must have the prefix spelled by the path from root to n. The path to a node encodes its prefix via the character labels on edges. For each node, pick any key in its subtree and confirm the first depth(n) characters match the concatenation of edge labels from root to that node.
source: Sedgewick & Wayne 4th ed, Section 5.2; TrieST.java (256-way trie)
tensor_equation: ∀n ∈ T: ∀d ∈ descendants(n): prefix(d.key, depth(n)) = pathLabel(root, n)
title: R-way trie prefix property: all descendants share the node's path prefix
verdict_evidence: Sedgewick & Wayne 4th ed. Sec. 5.2 (Tries) / TrieST.java: 'each node has R children, one for each possible character' and 'each node's position in the trie is defined by the characters on the links from the root to it' — a key is stored in the node reached by following its characters, so every key stored in the subtree rooted at n necessarily begins with the path label of n. Counterexample showing teeth: an implementation that on collision stores a key in an arbitrary free child slot (as a linear-probing table would) puts a key whose depth(n)-prefix differs from pathLabel(root, n) into that subtree, and get() would then fail to find it.
verification: Traverse the trie, tracking the accumulated prefix string. At each node, collect all keys in its subtree (by finding all terminal nodes below it). Assert every such key starts with the accumulated prefix. Alternatively, for each stored key, walk the trie from root character by character; assert that at step i, the current node's children contain the key's i-th character, and the terminal node has the correct value.

prompt_injection: Verify that a trie lookup for a key of length L visits exactly L nodes (or L+1 including root), regardless of how many keys are in the trie. Insert N random keys, measure lookup steps for keys of varying lengths, and confirm the step count equals the key length. This distinguishes the trie from a BST where search depth depends on N.
source: Sedgewick & Wayne 4th ed, Section 5.2; TrieST.java class Javadoc
tensor_equation: ∀key: lookupSteps(key) = |key|, independently of N (the number of keys stored)
title: R-way trie: search time is proportional to key length, independent of N
verdict_discrepancy: FIDELITY (equality vs bound, and the independence claim): (a) lookupSteps(key) = |key| holds only for a search hit; a search miss terminates early, so the correct statement is lookupSteps <= |key|. (b) 'independently of N' contradicts the book's own analysis, which gives the typical-case search-miss cost as ~log_R N - explicitly a function of N. The axiom hardens a worst-case upper bound into an equality and then asserts an independence the source denies.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error
verification: Instrument the get() method to count node visits. For each stored key, assert visits == key.length(). For each absent key, assert visits <= key.length() (may stop early at null link). Repeat at scale (N=10, N=1000, N=100000) to confirm step count does not grow with N.

prompt_injection: Verify the skip list level distribution follows the geometric distribution. For each node, its level is the number of successful coin flips before the first failure. Count nodes at each level. Assert that approximately half the nodes at level i also appear at level i+1. The expected count at level i is n / 2^i. Large deviations suggest the random level generator is broken or the promotion invariant is not maintained during insertion.
source: Pugh, 'Skip Lists: A Probabilistic Alternative to Balanced Trees', CACM 1990. NOT in Sedgewick & Wayne 4th ed — skip lists do not appear in the book.
tensor_equation: ∀k: P(node appears in level ℓ+1 | node appears in level ℓ) = p, with p = 1/2 yielding expected height O(log n)
title: Skip list: level promotion probability invariant (1/2 geometric distribution)
verdict_evidence: Pugh, 'Skip Lists: A Probabilistic Alternative to Balanced Trees', CACM 33(6):668-676, 1990, defines a level-i node as one appearing in levels 1..i, with a fraction p of level-i nodes also appearing in level i+1 (p=1/2 in the base analysis), giving expected level L(n)=log_{1/p} n and expected O(log n) search cost. The claimed_source honestly records that this is Pugh, not Sedgewick, and it is correct that skip lists do not appear in Sedgewick & Wayne 4th ed. Falsifiable: a deterministic builder that promotes every node to every level yields height n, not O(log n), and violates the geometric-promotion premise.
verification: After building a skip list with n nodes, count nodes at each level. Run a chi-squared goodness-of-fit test against the geometric distribution with p=0.5. For level i, expected count = n / 2^(i+1). Alternatively, for each node, assert its level equals the number of consecutive heads from a fair coin flip. For a weaker check: assert max_level ≈ log₂(n). This is a probabilistic check — low-probability false positives are acceptable; use a reasonable significance threshold (e.g., p < 0.001).

prompt_injection: Verify that each level of the skip list is a strictly increasing sorted linked list. Walk each level from the head sentinel to null. Assert that each node's key is strictly less than its successor's key. If any inversion exists (a.key >= b.key where b follows a), the skip list's search correctness is broken: a search scanning level ℓ may skip over the target key.
source: Pugh, 'Skip Lists', CACM 1990. NOT in Sedgewick & Wayne 4th ed.
tensor_equation: ∀level ℓ: ∀ consecutive nodes a→b at level ℓ: a.key < b.key (strictly increasing)
title: Skip list: sorted-order invariant (each level is a sorted linked list)
verdict_evidence: Pugh, 'Skip Lists: A Probabilistic Alternative to Balanced Trees', CACM 33(6), 1990: a skip list is built from a sorted linked list at level 1, with each higher level a sorted sublist of the level below; search relies on the strictly-increasing key order at every level to decide whether to drop down. The claimed_source is honest that this is Pugh's structure and not in Sedgewick & Wayne 4th ed (algs4 has no skip-list implementation). Has teeth: swapping two adjacent nodes at level 3 while leaving level 1 sorted causes search to drop down past the target and report 'not found' for a key that is present. Caveat: 'strictly increasing' presumes distinct keys, which is Pugh's setting.
verification: For each level from 0 to maxLevel, traverse the linked list starting from the head's forward pointer at that level. At each step, assert current.key < current.next.key (using the comparison function, e.g., compareTo < 0). Also verify that the bottom level (level 0) contains all nodes in sorted order. This must hold after every insert and delete.

prompt_injection: Verify the B-tree occupancy invariant: every internal node except the root must have at least ceil(M/2) children and at most M children. For Sedgewick's BTree.java where M=4, this means 2-4 children per non-root node. Count children of each node. If any non-root node has fewer than ceil(M/2) children, the tree is underfull — meaning a deletion removed too many keys without rebalancing. If any node has more than M children, a split was missed.
tensor_equation: ∀n ∈ T, n ≠ root: ⌈M/2⌉ ≤ children(n) ≤ M, where M is the order (max children). For Sedgewick's implementation, M=4, giving 2 ≤ children(n) ≤ 4 (a 2-3-4 tree).
title: B-tree: occupancy invariant (every non-root node has between ⌈M/2⌉ and M children)
verdict_discrepancy: The concrete claim contradicts the cited implementation. In algs4's BTree.java, M = 4 means a maximum of M-1 = 3 children per node (2 keys), i.e. 2-3 nodes — not '2 <= children(n) <= 4 (a 2-3-4 tree)'. The axiom's parenthetical is therefore false for the named source file. Secondarily, algs4's BTree implements no delete() and never merges or borrows, so the lower-bound half of the occupancy invariant is not enforced by that code at all; it holds only as a consequence of split() producing halves of size M/2.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error
verification: Traverse all nodes. For each node, count the number of non-null entries in the children array. Assert children_count >= ceil(M/2) for all nodes except root. Assert children_count <= M for all nodes. In Sedgewick's code, M=4 gives bounds [2, 4] for non-root nodes. The root may have as few as 0 children (empty tree) or 1 child (special case where the root is a leaf with keys).

prompt_injection: Verify the B-tree search-tree property: keys within each node are in strictly increasing order, and for each child c_i, all keys in c_i's subtree fall between the surrounding parent keys. Specifically, all keys in child i are greater than parent key i-1 (if i > 0) and less than parent key i (if i < k). This is the generalization of the BST property to multi-way nodes.
tensor_equation: ∀n with k keys [k₀..k_{k-1}] and k+1 children [c₀..c_k]: ∀x ∈ subtree(cᵢ): (i=0 ∨ x.key > k_{i-1}) ∧ (i=k ∨ x.key < k_i). Note: x = k_i occurs (the left child's max key equals the parent's separator).
title: B-tree: key ordering invariant (keys within node are sorted, children's keys lie between parent keys)
verdict_discrepancy: BOUND TIGHTNESS / STRUCTURAL MISMATCH against the cited implementation. Counterexample in Sedgewick & Wayne's own BTree: for child c_i the actual invariant is k_i <= x < k_{i+1}, and x = k_i occurs (the separator key is itself in the subtree, since algs4 uses a B+-tree with all values in external nodes and a sentinel smallest key). The axiom's strict 'x > k_{i-1}' is therefore violated on every internal node of a BTree.java instance. The equation belongs to CLRS Ch.18 / Knuth 6.2.4, not to Sedgewick & Wayne Section 6.2.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error
verification: For each internal node, verify its keys are sorted: k[0] < k[1] < ... < k[m-2] (where m = number of children). Then for each child i (0 to m-1): find the min and max key in child i's subtree. Assert min > parent.key[i-1] when i > 0. Assert max < parent.key[i] when i < m-1. Alternatively, perform an inorder traversal and assert the resulting key sequence is strictly increasing.

prompt_injection: Verify the weighted quick-union invariant: when merging two trees, the root of the smaller tree always becomes a child of the root of the larger tree. If the two trees have equal sizes, either root may become the child (the implementation makes root(v) the child of root(u)). This invariant guarantees that no tree ever exceeds depth lg n, which bounds find() to logarithmic time. To verify, after every union, check that the new parent's size equals the sum of both original trees' sizes, and that the smaller tree's root points to the larger tree's root.
source: Sedgewick & Wayne 4th ed, Section 1.5 (Weighted Quick-Union); algs4.cs.princeton.edu/15uf/
tensor_equation: ∀ union(u,v) where root(u) ≠ root(v): if size[root(u)] < size[root(v)] then parent[root(u)] = root(v), else parent[root(v)] = root(u). Equivalently: after union, size[newRoot] = size[root(u)] + size[root(v)].
title: Union-find: size/rank invariant (smaller tree attached to larger tree root)
verdict_evidence: Sedgewick & Wayne 4th ed Section 1.5, WeightedQuickUnionUF (algs4.cs.princeton.edu/15uf/): union() compares size[rootP] < size[rootQ] and links the smaller tree's root to the larger tree's root, then adds the smaller size into the new root's size entry. The axiom restates the code exactly, including the size accumulation. Falsifiable: plain QuickUnionUF (same file directory, no size array) always sets parent[rootP] = rootQ regardless of size, violating the equation and producing height-N trees - which is precisely the worst case weighting exists to prevent.
verification: For each union operation, record sizes before and after. Assert that after union: (1) the parent of the smaller tree's root points to the larger tree's root, and (2) the new root's size equals size_before_u + size_before_v. In the weighted quick-union implementation, the size array is only meaningful at roots. When a node becomes a non-root, its size entry is stale but never read. For the path-compressed variant, verify the same size invariant holds — path compression does not change tree sizes, only pointer structure.

prompt_injection: Verify that no node in the forest has depth exceeding floor(lg n). This is the key theorem that makes weighted quick-union efficient. The proof is by induction: when tree size at least doubles each time depth increases, so depth <= lg n. After building a forest of n sites, traverse every node, follow parent pointers to the root, count the path length, and assert it does not exceed floor(log2(n)).
source: Sedgewick & Wayne 4th ed, Section 1.5, Proposition: 'The depth of any node in a forest built by weighted quick-union for n sites is at most lg n.'
tensor_equation: ∀ node x: depth(x) ≤ ⌊lg n⌋, where n = total number of sites in the disjoint-set forest
title: Union-find: depth bound from weighted union (any node depth ≤ lg n)
verdict_evidence: Sedgewick & Wayne, 'Algorithms' 4th ed, Sec. 1.5, Proposition H: 'The depth of any node in a forest built by weighted quick-union for N sites is at most lg N.' Since depth is an integer, depth <= lg n is equivalent to depth <= floor(lg n), so the floor in the axiom is sound. Has teeth: plain (unweighted) quick-union on the same union sequence 1-2, 2-3, ..., builds a path of depth n-1, which exceeds floor(lg n) for n >= 4 — so the weighted-union hypothesis is doing real work.
verification: After a sequence of union operations on n sites, for each site i, follow parent[i] to root, counting steps. Assert count <= floor(log2(n)). This test can be expensive (O(n * depth)) but confirms the structural guarantee. Note: path compression reduces this bound further to near-constant (inverse Ackermann), so the lg n bound is conservative in the compressed variant.

prompt_injection: Verify that path compression does not alter set membership. After find(x), every node on the path from x to the root should point directly to the root. More importantly, find(x) must return the same root before and after compression. For any two elements that were in the same set before a find operation, they must still be in the same set after. Path compression is a correctness-preserving optimization: it only adds shortcuts, never changes which root any element ultimately reaches.
tensor_equation: ∀x: root(x) before compression = root(x) after compression. Formally: find(x) ⇒ let r = root(x); ∀v ∈ path(x→r): parent[v] = r after compression.
title: Union-find: path compression preserves set identity (compressed nodes still reach the same root)
verdict_evidence: Sedgewick & Wayne 4th ed. Sec. 1.5 (Case Study: Union-Find) presents path compression as a refinement of weighted quick-union: add a loop to find() that sets the id[] entry of every node on the search path to the root, which by construction leaves connectivity (the set partition) unchanged because only links within one already-connected component are rewritten. Counterexample showing the equation is falsifiable and non-trivial: a buggy 'compression' that sets parent[v] = x (the query node) rather than the root leaves parent[x] pointing at itself in the wrong tree and changes root(v); a further buggy variant that compresses across a union boundary would merge two components. Path halving (each node linked to its grandparent), which the book also mentions, satisfies the first form of the equation (root preserved) but NOT the second form as written.
verification: Before find(x), record root(x) by walking parent pointers. After find(x) with path compression, verify: (1) find(x) returns the same root, and (2) for every node v on the original path, parent[v] now points directly to that root (or to an ancestor that still leads to that root — two-pass compression may leave intermediate pointers). Then for a randomly sampled set of element pairs that were connected before compression, assert they are still connected after.

prompt_injection: Verify the cut property: for any partition of vertices into two sets, the minimum-weight edge crossing the cut must be in the MST (assuming distinct edge weights). This is the correctness foundation for both Kruskal's and Prim's algorithms. To test programmatically: after computing an MST via Kruskal or Prim, pick a random cut (random subset of vertices S). Find the minimum-weight edge from S to V\S. Assert that edge is in the computed MST. If it is not, either the edge weights are not distinct or the MST computation is incorrect.
tensor_equation: ∀ cut C of G, ∀ e = argmin{weight(e') : e' crosses C}: if all edge weights are distinct, then e ∈ every MST of G
title: MST: cut property (minimum-weight crossing edge belongs to every MST under distinct weights)
verdict_evidence: Sedgewick & Wayne 4th ed Section 4.3 states the cut property (Proposition J): given any cut in an edge-weighted graph, the crossing edge of minimum weight is in some MST; the text notes that when edge weights are distinct the MST is unique, so 'some MST' and 'every MST' coincide. The axiom's distinctness antecedent is exactly the condition that licenses the strengthening. Falsifiable, and the distinctness premise is load-bearing: in a triangle with all weights equal to 1, the cut {A} | {B,C} has two minimum crossing edges and neither lies in every MST - drop the antecedent and the axiom is false.
verification: After computing MST via Kruskal or Prim: (1) Randomly sample cuts: for each trial, randomly select a subset S of vertices, find min-weight edge e crossing S↔V\S. Assert e is in the MST edges. (2) Ensure edge weights are made distinct (e.g., by adding negligible epsilon to break ties) before testing. (3) Alternatively, verify the Kruskal check() method condition: for each MST edge e, removing e partitions the tree into two components S and V\S; assert e is the minimum-weight edge crossing that cut. This is what KruskalMST.java's check() already verifies.

prompt_injection: Verify the spanning tree is indeed a tree: it has exactly V-1 edges and contains no cycles. This is the definition of a spanning tree. If there are fewer than V-1 edges, the graph is disconnected and the MST is only a spanning forest. If there are more than V-1 edges, a cycle exists and the tree has redundant edges — removing one would yield a lighter spanning structure. Verify both edge count and cycle-freeness.
source: Sedgewick & Wayne 4th ed, Section 4.3; KruskalMST.java check()
tensor_equation: |E_MST| = |V| - 1 ∧ MST contains no cycles
title: MST: tree property (spanning tree has exactly V-1 edges and is acyclic)
verdict_evidence: Sedgewick & Wayne 4th ed Sec. 4.3: for an edge-weighted *connected* graph, a spanning tree is a connected acyclic subgraph containing all V vertices, hence exactly V-1 edges; KruskalMST.java's check() explicitly tests 'is it a spanning forest', 'is it a spanning tree', and 'is it a minimal spanning tree'. Has teeth: an implementation that forgets the union-find cycle test admits an edge joining two already-connected vertices, yielding V edges and a cycle — precisely what check() catches. Note the connectivity precondition is implicit in the axiom: on a disconnected graph the minimum spanning *forest* has V-k edges for k components, so the equation should be read under Sec. 4.3's standing assumption that the graph is connected.
verification: Edge count: assert |MST_edges| == V - 1 (or for a disconnected graph, |MST_edges| == V - components). Cycle check: run union-find or DFS on the MST edges. If any edge connects two already-connected vertices, a cycle exists. In Sedgewick's KruskalMST.java, the check() method computes a union-find on the MST edges and verifies the edge count equals V-1 for a connected graph.

prompt_injection: Verify that the hash table rejects null keys at every entry point. Sedgewick's implementation explicitly throws IllegalArgumentException for null keys in get, put, delete, and contains. This is a defensive invariant: null cannot be hashed, and allowing it would cause NullPointerException inside hashCode() or corrupt the key-based identity contract.
tensor_equation: ∀ operation op ∈ {put, get, delete, contains}: ¬accepts(op, null)
title: Hash table: null key prohibition (table operations reject null keys by contract)
verdict_evidence: The algs4 symbol-table API (Section 3.1, carried through 3.4's SeparateChainingHashST/LinearProbingHashST) specifies that keys must not be null, and the implementations enforce it: each of put, get, delete and contains begins with 'if (key == null) throw new IllegalArgumentException("argument to ... is null")'. Falsifiable and mechanically checkable: st.get(null) must throw rather than return null. Caveat recorded: this is an API contract of the cited implementation, not a mathematical property of hashing - java.util.HashMap permits a null key, so the invariant is scoped to algs4.
verification: For each public mutating/querying method (get, put, delete, contains), call it with a null key and assert an IllegalArgumentException is thrown. This is a simple unit-test contract check, not a structural invariant.

prompt_injection: Never MAC plaintext. Never encrypt a MAC. Always encrypt first, then MAC the ciphertext with a second, independent key. Verify the MAC before any decryption. Any authenticated encryption scheme you design or use must follow encrypt-then-MAC composition.
source_document: Ferguson, Schneier, Kohno. "Cryptography Engineering" (2010), Chapter 7: "The Secure Channel", Section 7.4 "Encrypt-then-MAC"
tensor_equation: ∀ authenticated_encryption scheme S: AE = E_k1(m) || MAC_k2(E_k1(m)). Encrypt-then-MAC is the only generically secure composition per Bellare & Namprempre 2000, not a universal property of all secure compositions.
title: Encrypt-then-MAC: Authentication must cover ciphertext
verdict_discrepancy: The absolute claim is misattributed and overstated. 'Encrypt-then-MAC is the only generically secure composition' is a provable-security result of Bellare & Namprempre, 'Authenticated Encryption: Relations among Notions and Analysis of the Generic Composition Paradigm' (ASIACRYPT 2000) — not of Ferguson/Schneier/Kohno, who write engineering guidance rather than generic-composition theorems. And 'only' is false as stated: Krawczyk, 'The Order of Encryption and Authentication for Protecting Communications (or: How Secure is SSL?)' (CRYPTO 2001), proves that MAC-then-encrypt IS secure when the encryption is CBC mode with a secure block cipher or a stream cipher xoring a pseudorandom pad. The correct statement is that encrypt-then-MAC is the only composition secure for *every* IND-CPA encryption scheme. I could not confirm that a section numbered 7.4 titled 'Encrypt-then-MAC' exists in that book; the section covering this is the chapter's 'order of encryption and authentication' discussion.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: Never implement your own cryptographic primitives, modes, or protocols for production use. Use established libraries (libsodium, Go crypto, OpenSSL, BoringSSL). If you find yourself writing AES, SHA, RSA, ECDH, or any mode-of-operation code from scratch, stop — you are creating a vulnerability. The only exception is educational or research code explicitly labeled as 'do not use in production'.
source_document: Schneier, Bruce. "Applied Cryptography" (1996), Foreword and Chapter 1. Ferguson, Schneier, Kohno. "Cryptography Engineering" (2010), Chapter 1, "The Context of Cryptography"
tensor_equation: ∀ crypto primitive P used in production: P ∈ {established, peer-reviewed, standards-track primitives}. (Schneier's "don't roll your own crypto" is a well-known maxim, but the cited Foreword is by Diffie and does not state it.)
title: Don't roll your own crypto
verdict_discrepancy: MISATTRIBUTION: the Foreword to Applied Cryptography (1996) is written by Whitfield Diffie, not Schneier, and is a historical essay on the cryptographic literature - it does not state 'don't roll your own crypto'. Applied Cryptography in fact drew later criticism (from Schneier himself, in the preface to Cryptography Engineering and in Crypto-Gram) for encouraging exactly that by handing readers primitives and source code without the engineering context. 'Schneier's Law' is likewise not from that foreword - it originates in Crypto-Gram (October 1998) and was named in a 2011 Schneier blog post.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: Every encryption nonce (IV, nonce) paired with a given key must be globally unique across all encryptions. Never reuse a nonce with the same key. For AES-GCM, nonce reuse is catastrophic — it reveals the authentication key. For AES-CBC, IV reuse leaks plaintext relationships. Use crypto/rand to generate nonces, or a strictly monotonic counter that persists across restarts.
source_document: Ferguson, Schneier, Kohno. "Cryptography Engineering" (2010), Chapter 4: "Block Cipher Modes", Section 4.7 "Nonce Generation"
tensor_equation: ∀ nonce n used with key k: n must never be reused with the same key k. ∀(k, n) pairs: each (k, n) used at most once for encryption.
title: Cryptographic nonce uniqueness
verdict_evidence: Ferguson, Schneier & Kohno, Cryptography Engineering (2010), Ch. 4 is 'Block Cipher Modes' (confirmed via the Wiley chapter listing, doi 10.1002/9781118722367.ch4), and the CTR/CBC discussions there state that the nonce must be unique for every message encrypted under a given key. Counterexample showing teeth and severity: AES-CTR with a repeated (k, n) yields identical keystreams, so C1 XOR C2 = P1 XOR P2 and both plaintexts leak; for GCM a repeated nonce additionally leaks the GHASH authentication subkey and permits forgeries. Note the axiom's scope is (key, nonce) pairs — reusing a nonce under a DIFFERENT key is permitted, which is what makes the equation the correct one rather than 'nonces are globally unique'.

prompt_injection: Every distinct cryptographic purpose requires a cryptographically independent key. Encryption and authentication keys must be separate. Keys for different channels or protocols must be separate. Derive multiple keys from a master secret using HKDF with distinct info strings. Never reuse a key for two different algorithms or purposes.
source_document: Ferguson, Schneier, Kohno. "Cryptography Engineering" (2010), Chapter 7: "The Secure Channel", Section 7.4 "Encrypt-then-MAC" and Chapter 13: "Key Negotiation"
tensor_equation: ∀ keys k1, k2 used for distinct cryptographic purposes (encryption vs authentication, or different channels): k1 and k2 are cryptographically independent. No single key is used for both encryption and MAC.
title: Independent keys for distinct purposes
verdict_discrepancy: CITATION: the section and chapter labels are wrong, verified against the book's own table of contents. Chapter 7's sections are 7.1 Properties of a Secure Channel, 7.2 Order of Authentication and Encryption, 7.3 Designing a Secure Channel: Overview, 7.4 Design Details, 7.5 Alternatives - there is no '7.4 Encrypt-then-MAC' (encrypt-then-MAC is discussed in 7.2). And Chapter 13 is 'Introduction to Cryptographic Protocols'; 'Key Negotiation' is Chapter 14. Both cited locations misname their content.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: All cryptographic randomness must come from a cryptographically secure pseudorandom number generator (CSPRNG) seeded from a hardware or OS entropy source. In Go: always use crypto/rand, never math/rand. In Java: use java.security.SecureRandom, never java.util.Random. Check that the CSPRNG is properly seeded — an unseeded or poorly seeded CSPRNG produces predictable output.
source_document: Ferguson, Schneier, Kohno. "Cryptography Engineering" (2010), Chapter 9: "Generating Randomness"
tensor_equation: ∀ random bytes r used in cryptographic context: r must be drawn from a CSPRNG seeded with ≥128 bits of entropy from a hardware or OS entropy source. r ∉ {deterministic PRNG, Math.random(), unseeded RNG}.
title: Secure random number generation: cryptographic entropy source
verdict_evidence: Ferguson, Schneier & Kohno, 'Cryptography Engineering' (2010) Ch. 9 is 'Generating Randomness' — confirmed by the publisher's chapter listing and by the authors' own Fortuna excerpt hosted on schneier.com. The chapter's whole argument is the axiom's content: real systems must use a cryptographic PRNG (they present Fortuna) seeded and reseeded from genuine entropy sources, must never use a language-library PRNG such as rand()/Math.random() for cryptographic values, and must handle the seed-file/boot-time entropy problem. Has teeth: the Debian OpenSSL entropy bug (CVE-2008-0166) reduced the effective seed to ~15 bits of pid and produced a fully enumerable key space — a direct violation. Note the specific '>= 128 bits' threshold is an interpolation (Fortuna uses a 256-bit seed/pool design), but it is a weaker bound than the book's own practice and so is not contradicted. Substantively overlaps AX-CRYPTO-021 in this batch; kept separate because the sources and the stated obligations (entropy quantity vs. enumerated use sites) differ.

prompt_injection: All MAC and signature verification comparisons must be constant-time. Never use early-exit string comparison (==, bytes.Equal, memcmp with early return). Use subtle.ConstantTimeCompare in Go, constant_time_compare in Python, or the equivalent in any language. A timing side channel in MAC verification enables existential forgery attacks.
source_document: Ferguson, Schneier, Kohno. "Cryptography Engineering" (2010), Chapter 7: "The Secure Channel", Section 7.4, and Chapter 15: "Implementation Issues"
tensor_equation: ∀ MAC verification: Compare(mac_received, mac_computed) uses constant-time comparison. timing_leak(mac_verify) = 0. No early exit on byte mismatch. (Correct source: Cryptography Engineering Ch. 8 Implementation Issues (I) and Ch. 13 Implementation Issues (II).)
title: Constant-time comparison for MAC verification
verdict_discrepancy: MISCITATION: in Cryptography Engineering (2010) Chapter 15 is 'RSA', not 'Implementation Issues'. The implementation-issues material is split across Chapter 8 'Implementation Issues (I)' and Chapter 18 'Implementation Issues (II)'; Chapter 16 is 'Introduction to Cryptographic Protocols'. Minor fidelity note: 'timing_leak(mac_verify) = 0' is an idealisation - constant-time comparison removes the data-dependent branch, but zero timing leakage is not achievable or claimed by the source, which discusses reducing side channels rather than eliminating them.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: Every cryptographic key in memory must be zeroized (overwritten with zeros) immediately after its last use. Never rely on GC to clean up key material. In Go: use subtle.XORBytes to wipe, and be aware that string-type keys are immutable and cannot be wiped — use []byte exclusively for key material. Memory-safe languages with GC make perfect zeroization hard; mitigate by minimizing key lifetime scope.
source_document: Ferguson, Schneier, Kohno. "Cryptography Engineering" (2010), Chapter 15: "Implementation Issues", Section on key management
tensor_equation: ∀ key material k in memory: after k is no longer needed, the memory region holding k is overwritten with zeros before deallocation. dangling_key_bytes = 0.
title: Key material zeroization after use
verdict_evidence: Cryptography Engineering devotes its Implementation Issues chapters to exactly this: keys and other plaintext secrets must be wiped from memory as soon as they are no longer needed, with the discussion covering wiping state, swap files, caches and the destruction of data. The requirement is echoed normatively by FIPS 140-3/ISO 19790 zeroization of SSPs. Counterexample showing teeth: a Go service that does `key := deriveKey(); use(key)` and lets the slice fall out of scope leaves the key recoverable in a heap dump or core file; `for i := range key { key[i] = 0 }` before release satisfies it. A second, subtler counterexample shows the property is real and not automatic: a compiler may elide a zeroing store as dead (hence memset_s / explicit_bzero / runtime.KeepAlive).

prompt_injection: Never use ECB mode for encrypting data longer than one block (16 bytes for AES). ECB reveals plaintext structure — identical plaintext blocks produce identical ciphertext blocks. The ECB penguin is the canonical demonstration. Use authenticated encryption modes: AES-GCM, ChaCha20-Poly1305, or AES-CBC with separate HMAC.
source_document: Ferguson, Schneier, Kohno. "Cryptography Engineering" (2010), Chapter 4: "Block Cipher Modes", Section 4.3 "Electronic Codebook Mode"
tensor_equation: ∀ block cipher encryption of data with length > block_size: mode ≠ ECB. ECB preserves plaintext patterns; blocks repeat identically. (Correct source: §4.2 is ECB, §4.3 is CBC.)
title: No ECB mode for structured data
verdict_discrepancy: CITATION + FIDELITY: (a) verified against the book's table of contents - Chapter 4's sections are 4.1 Padding, 4.2 ECB, 4.3 CBC, 4.4 OFB, 4.5 CTR. ECB is Section 4.2; Section 4.3 is CBC. The cited location is wrong. (b) The axiom's antecedent 'data with length > block_size' carves out an exemption the source does not grant: the book's objection is unconditional, and single-block ECB still leaks plaintext equality across messages under a fixed key (identical single-block records encrypt identically), so the guarded form is weaker than the cited position.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: Always authenticate ciphertext before decrypting. If authentication fails, produce a single generic error — never distinguish between padding errors, format errors, and MAC failures. The distinction between 'invalid padding' and 'invalid MAC' is a padding oracle. Use AEAD modes (AES-GCM, ChaCha20-Poly1305) which handle this correctly, or encrypt-then-MAC where MAC verification happens first.
source_document: Ferguson, Schneier, Kohno. "Cryptography Engineering" (2010), Chapter 7: "The Secure Channel", Section on padding oracle attacks
tensor_equation: ∀ ciphertext c: Authenticate(c) before Decrypt(c). If authentication fails, reject without revealing padding/format errors.
title: Padding oracle attack prevention: authenticate before decrypt
verdict_evidence: The claim itself is correct and standard — verifying the MAC over the ciphertext before performing any decryption or padding removal is what defeats Vaudenay's 2002 CBC padding oracle and its descendants (Lucky 13, POODLE), and it follows from Bellare-Namprempre/Krawczyk. The counterexample showing teeth is concrete: a receiver that strips PKCS#7 padding and returns a distinguishable error before checking the MAC leaks one plaintext byte per ~256 queries. However, I could not confirm the citation: I was unable to obtain the section listing for Chapter 7 ('The Secure Channel') of Ferguson/Schneier/Kohno, 'Cryptography Engineering' (2010), and the citation itself is vague ('Section on padding oracle attacks') rather than a numbered section. Padding-oracle attacks are Vaudenay (EUROCRYPT 2002) and Canvel et al. (CRYPTO 2003); the book predates Lucky 13 (2013). Per the tier rules an unverifiable citation is UNCERTAIN rather than VERIFIED, even though the technical content is sound. Also note this partially restates AX-CRYPTO-001's decrypt-ordering consequence.

prompt_injection: Every encryption scheme used in production must provide IND-CPA security at minimum. This means: (1) randomization/nonces so the same plaintext encrypts to different ciphertexts each time, (2) no deterministic encryption without explicit justification, (3) no ECB mode. Any scheme that produces identical ciphertext for identical plaintext fails IND-CPA immediately.
source_document: Katz, Jonathan and Lindell, Yehuda. "Introduction to Modern Cryptography" (3rd ed, 2020), Chapter 3: "Private-Key Encryption", Section 3.4 "CPA-Security"
tensor_equation: ∀ PPT adversary A with access to encryption oracle: |Pr[A(Enc_k(m_0)) = 1] - Pr[A(Enc_k(m_1)) = 1]| ≤ negl(λ). The oracle distinguishes IND-CPA from IND-EAV, which are provably different notions.
title: Semantic security (IND-CPA): ciphertext reveals nothing about plaintext
verdict_discrepancy: The equation states IND-EAV, not IND-CPA — it omits the defining feature of CPA-security, the adversary's encryption oracle. This is not pedantry: the two notions are provably different.
