prompt_injection: A system has 10 components each with failure probability 0.001. Using the union bound, what is the maximum possible probability that at least one component fails? Is this bound tight? What is the exact probability if failures are independent?
source_document: Grimmett & Stirzaker. Probability and Random Processes (2001). Chapter 1, Section 1.4.
tensor_equation: ∀events A₁, …, Aₙ: P(⋃ᵢ Aᵢ) ≤ Σᵢ P(Aᵢ). Equality holds iff events are disjoint. Infinite case: P(⋃_{i=1}^{∞} Aᵢ) ≤ Σ_{i=1}^{∞} P(Aᵢ). Always valid, no independence required.
title: Union Bound (Boole's Inequality): probability of union is at most sum of probabilities
verdict_discrepancy: Two faults. (1) FIDELITY: 'Equality holds iff events are disjoint' is false in the ⇐ direction of necessity. Counterexample: Ω=[0,1] with Lebesgue measure, A₁=[0,½], A₂=[½,1]. They are NOT disjoint (A₁∩A₂={½}), yet P(A₁∪A₂)=1=P(A₁)+P(A₂). The correct condition is pairwise almost-disjointness, P(Aᵢ∩Aⱼ)=0 for i≠j. (2) Wrong section: Boole's inequality is in G&S §1.3 'Probability' (properties of the probability measure), not §1.4 'Conditional probability'.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: A multi-stage process: first roll a fair die, then flip that many coins. What is the expected number of heads? Use the tower property: E[heads] = E[E[heads|die_roll]] = E[die_roll/2] = 3.5/2 = 1.75.
source_document: Grimmett & Stirzaker. Probability and Random Processes (2001). Chapter 3, Section 3.2.
tensor_equation: ∀random variables X, Y with finite expectation: E[E[X|Y]] = E[X]. For σ-algebras F ⊂ G: E[E[X|G]|F] = E[X|F]. (G&S §3.7 Conditional distributions, not §3.2.)
title: Law of Total Expectation (Tower Property): nested conditional expectations
verdict_discrepancy: Wrong section. Grimmett & Stirzaker §3.2 is 'Independence' (TOC verified). Conditional expectation and the tower property are §3.7 'Conditional distributions and conditional expectation' (Theorem 3.7.4), with the σ-algebra version in Ch. 7 §7.9 'Prediction and conditional expectation'.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: A system's latency is a random variable L. The cost function is convex (e.g., quadratic). Prove that E[cost(L)] ≥ cost(E[L]) using Jensen's inequality. Why does this matter for capacity planning?
source_document: Grimmett & Stirzaker. Probability and Random Processes (2001). Chapter 3, Section 3.3.
tensor_equation: ∀convex function φ, ∀random variable X: E[φ(X)] ≥ φ(E[X]). For concave φ: E[φ(X)] ≤ φ(E[X]). Equality iff X is constant or φ is linear on the support of X. (Counterexample: φ(x)=max(0,x) convex, X uniform on [1,2] — equality holds without X being constant or φ being linear.)
title: Jensen's Inequality: expectation of convex function is at least the function of expectation
verdict_discrepancy: Two faults. (1) FIDELITY: 'Equality iff X is constant or φ is linear' is false. Counterexample: φ(x)=max(0,x) (convex, not linear) and X uniform on [1,2] (not constant). E[φ(X)]=E[X]=1.5=φ(1.5), so equality holds while neither disjunct is true. The correct condition is that φ is affine on an interval containing the support of X (a.s.). (2) Wrong section: G&S §3.3 is 'Expectation'; Jensen's inequality appears in Ch. 5 §5.6 'Expectation revisited' (TOC verified, p. 178). Also missing: E[X] and E[φ(X)] must exist.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: Requests arrive at a server with rate λ. The processing time per request has mean μ and variance σ². Derive the variance of the total processing time for a random number of requests using the law of total variance.
source_document: Grimmett & Stirzaker. Probability and Random Processes (2001). Chapter 3, Section 3.4.
tensor_equation: ∀random variables X, Y: Var(X) = E[Var(X|Y)] + Var(E[X|Y]). The first term is within-group variance, the second is between-group variance. Used in ANOVA: total variance = within + between.
title: Variance Decomposition (Law of Total Variance): Var(X) = E[Var(X|Y)] + Var(E[X|Y])
verdict_discrepancy: Two faults. (1) Wrong section: Grimmett & Stirzaker §3.4 is 'Indicators and matching' (TOC verified), which is about matching problems and indicator functions. The conditional-variance formula is in §3.7 'Conditional distributions and conditional expectation' (Exercise/Theorem 3.7.4 area) and Ch. 4's continuous analogue. (2) MISSING HYPOTHESIS: '∀ random variables X, Y' omits E[X²]<∞. For X with infinite variance (e.g. Pareto with α=1.5) both sides are +∞ or undefined and the equation carries no content.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: Two metrics X and Y have sample correlation r = 0.8. Can we conclude that Cov(X,Y) = 0.8 σ_X σ_Y? If r = 1, what does that imply about the relationship between X and Y?
source_document: Grimmett & Stirzaker. Probability and Random Processes (2001). Chapter 3, Section 3.5.
tensor_equation: ∀random variables X, Y with finite variance: Cov(X,Y)² ≤ Var(X)Var(Y). Equality (|Cov(X,Y)| = σ_X σ_Y) iff Y = aX + b almost surely for some constants a, b. Derives from Cauchy-Schwarz on centered variables.
title: Covariance Bound: |Cov(X,Y)| ≤ σ_X σ_Y with equality iff linear dependence
verdict_discrepancy: Two faults. (1) FIDELITY: 'Equality iff Y = aX + b almost surely' is false in the degenerate case. Counterexample: X ≡ 0 (constant), Y ~ N(0,1) independent. Then Cov(X,Y)=0 and Var(X)Var(Y)=0, so equality holds — yet Y is not a.s. an affine function of X. The equality condition requires Var(X)>0 (or the symmetric statement 'X and Y are a.s. affinely related'). (2) Wrong section: G&S §3.5 is 'Examples of discrete variables'; the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality is Theorem 3.6.9 in §3.6 'Dependence' (TOC verified, p. 62).
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: A system predicts latency X from request size Y. Prove that the optimal predictor (minimizing MSE) is E[X|Y]. What does this imply about using linear regression vs. conditional expectation?
source_document: Grimmett & Stirzaker. Probability and Random Processes (2001). Chapter 3, Section 3.2.
tensor_equation: ∀random variables X, Y: E[X|Y] = argmin_{f(Y)} E[(X − f(Y))²] for all measurable functions f. The conditional expectation is the a.s.-unique minimizer; any g(Y) that agrees with E[X|Y] a.s. has the same MSE.
title: Conditional Expectation as Minimum Mean Square Error Predictor
verdict_discrepancy: Two faults. (1) FIDELITY: 'Any other function g(Y) has larger MSE' is false. Counterexample: let g(Y)=E[X|Y] modified on a P-null set (or, for Y with an atom-free component, any g agreeing with E[X|Y] a.s.). Then g ≠ E[X|Y] pointwise but the MSE is identical. The correct statement is 'no smaller MSE', with uniqueness only up to a.s. equality. (2) Wrong section: G&S §3.2 is 'Independence' (TOC verified); the MMSE-projection characterisation is §7.9 'Prediction and conditional expectation' (p. 343). Also missing: E[X²]<∞.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: A monitoring system measures average latency over n requests. Prove that the probability of the sample mean deviating from the true mean by more than ε approaches 0 as n → ∞. How large must n be to guarantee P(|X̄ₙ − μ| ≥ 5ms) ≤ 0.01 given σ² = 100ms²?
source_document: Grimmett & Stirzaker. Probability and Random Processes (2001). Chapter 5, Section 5.1.
tensor_equation: Let X₁, …, Xₙ be i.i.d. with finite mean μ and variance σ². ∀ε > 0: lim_{n→∞} P(|X̄ₙ − μ| ≥ ε) = 0 where X̄ₙ = (1/n)ΣᵢXᵢ. Chebyshev gives: P(|X̄ₙ − μ| ≥ ε) ≤ σ²/(nε²).
title: Weak Law of Large Numbers: sample mean converges in probability to population mean
verdict_discrepancy: Wrong section. Grimmett & Stirzaker §5.1 is 'Generating functions' (TOC verified: Ch. 5 = Generating functions and their applications; 5.6 Expectation revisited; 5.10 Inversion and continuity theorems; 5.11 Two limit theorems; 5.12 Large deviations). The law of large numbers is in §5.11 'Two limit theorems' and, in full, Ch. 7 §7.4 'Laws of large numbers'.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: A service processes 10⁴ requests, each with independent latency distribution (mean 100ms, variance 400ms²). Using the CLT, estimate the probability that the average latency exceeds 101ms. What is the Berry-Esseen bound on the approximation error?
source_document: Grimmett & Stirzaker. Probability and Random Processes (2001). Chapter 5, Section 5.3.
tensor_equation: Let X₁, …, Xₙ i.i.d. with mean μ and finite variance σ². Then (ΣᵢXᵢ − nμ)/(σ√n) →ᵈ N(0,1) as n → ∞. Equivalent: X̄ₙ ≈ N(μ, σ²/n) for large n. Convergence rate: Berry-Esseen bound O(1/√n).
title: Central Limit Theorem: sum of i.i.d. variables approximates normal distribution
verdict_discrepancy: Two faults. (1) Wrong section: G&S §5.3 is 'Random walk' (TOC verified). The central limit theorem is Theorem 5.11.4 in §5.11 'Two limit theorems'. (2) MISSING HYPOTHESIS: the Berry–Esseen O(1/√n) rate requires a finite third absolute moment E|X−μ|³ < ∞. With only finite variance the convergence rate can be arbitrarily slow, so 'Convergence rate: Berry-Esseen bound O(1/√n)' as an unconditional clause is false.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: A system checks for errors every second, with independent error probability 1/n² at time n. Show that infinitely many errors occur with probability 0 (Borel-Cantelli first lemma). What if the error probability is 1/n?
source_document: Grimmett & Stirzaker. Probability and Random Processes (2001). Chapter 2, Section 2.7.
tensor_equation: First lemma: if Σ_{n=1}∞ P(Aₙ) < ∞ then P(limsup Aₙ) = 0 (infinitely many Aₙ occur with probability 0). Second lemma: if Aₙ are independent and Σ P(Aₙ) = ∞ then P(limsup Aₙ) = 1.
title: Borel-Cantelli Lemma: convergence of infinite sequence of events
verdict_discrepancy: Wrong chapter. Grimmett & Stirzaker Ch. 2 is 'Random variables and their distributions' and §2.7 is its problems/worked-examples section — no Borel–Cantelli there. The Borel–Cantelli lemmas are in Ch. 7 'Convergence of random variables', §7.3 'Some ancillary results' (TOC verified), where they are used to prove the strong law.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: A system generates random IDs from a 256-element alphabet. Compute the maximum entropy achievable. Is the entropy of a uniform distribution over 256 values equal to 8 bits? Prove that uniform distribution maximizes entropy.
source_document: Cover & Thomas. Elements of Information Theory (2nd ed., 2006). Chapter 2, Section 2.1.
tensor_equation: ∀discrete random variable X with alphabet X: 0 ≤ H(X) ≤ log₂|X|. H(X) = 0 iff X is deterministic (p(x)=1 for some x). H(X) = log₂|X| iff X is uniformly distributed over X. H(X) = −Σ p(x) log₂ p(x).
title: Shannon Entropy Bounds: 0 ≤ H(X) ≤ log|X|
verdict_evidence: Cover & Thomas 2nd ed. Ch. 2 is 'Entropy, Relative Entropy, and Mutual Information'; §2.1 'Entropy' defines H(X)=−Σp log p and proves H(X)≥0 (Lemma 2.1.1), with the maximum-entropy upper bound H(X)≤log|𝒳| and its uniform-distribution equality condition following as Theorem 2.6.4 in §2.6 (TOC verified: 2.6 'Jensen's Inequality and Its Consequences', p. 25). Falsifiable and both bounds are tight and non-vacuous: X uniform on 4 symbols gives H=2=log₂4 (upper bound tight, so any strengthening to < fails); X deterministic gives H=0. A biased coin p=0.9 gives H=0.469 < 1, refuting any claim that H=log|𝒳| always.

prompt_injection: A system captures two correlated measurements X and Y. Prove that H(X,Y) = H(X) + H(Y|X). If X determines Y exactly (Y = f(X)), what is H(Y|X) and what does that imply about H(X,Y)?
source_document: Cover & Thomas. Elements of Information Theory (2nd ed., 2006). Chapter 2, Section 2.5.
tensor_equation: ∀random variables X, Y: H(X,Y) = H(X) + H(Y|X) = H(Y) + H(X|Y). Generalized: H(X₁, …, Xₙ) = Σ_{i=1}ⁿ H(Xᵢ | X₁, …, X_{i−1}). Conditional entropy H(Y|X) = Σ p(x) H(Y|X=x).
title: Chain Rule for Entropy: H(X,Y) = H(X) + H(Y|X)
verdict_evidence: Cover & Thomas 2nd ed. §2.5 is 'Chain Rules for Entropy, Relative Entropy, and Mutual Information' (TOC verified); the chain rule H(X,Y)=H(X)+H(Y|X) is Theorem 2.2.1 and its n-variable generalisation H(X₁..Xₙ)=ΣH(Xᵢ|X₁..X_{i−1}) is Theorem 2.5.1, exactly as encoded, along with the definition H(Y|X)=Σₓp(x)H(Y|X=x). Falsifiable: X,Y i.i.d. fair bits gives H(X,Y)=2=1+1; the incorrect variant H(X,Y)=H(X)+H(Y) fails whenever Y=X, where H(X,Y)=1 but H(X)+H(Y)=2 — and the chain rule correctly gives 1+0=1.

prompt_injection: A system processes raw sensor data Y through a feature extractor Z = f(Y). Prove that I(X;Z) ≤ I(X;Y) for any function f, where X is the true state. What does this imply about lossy feature extraction?
source_document: Cover & Thomas. Elements of Information Theory (2nd ed., 2006). Chapter 2, Section 2.8.
tensor_equation: If X → Y → Z forms a Markov chain (X ⟂ Z | Y), then I(X;Y) ≥ I(X;Z). Equivalent: I(X;Y) ≥ I(X;g(Y)) for any function g. Mutual information is non-increasing under data processing.
title: Data Processing Inequality: post-processing cannot increase information
verdict_evidence: Cover & Thomas 2nd ed. §2.8 is 'Data-Processing Inequality' (TOC verified), Theorem 2.8.1: if X→Y→Z is a Markov chain then I(X;Y) ≥ I(X;Z), with the corollary I(X;Y) ≥ I(X;g(Y)). The Markov hypothesis is preserved, which is what gives the axiom teeth: counterexample without it — let X,Y be i.i.d. fair bits and Z=X. Then I(X;Y)=0 but I(X;Z)=1, violating the conclusion; X→Y→Z is not a Markov chain here. Falsifiable in the intended direction too: Y=X and Z=constant gives 1 ≥ 0.

prompt_injection: A system's predicted distribution p over outcomes differs from the true distribution q. Prove that D(p||q) ≥ 0 with equality iff p = q. What does this imply about using cross-entropy as a loss function?
source_document: Cover & Thomas. Elements of Information Theory (2nd ed., 2006). Chapter 2, Section 2.6.
tensor_equation: ∀probability distributions p, q over the same alphabet: D(p||q) = Σ p(x) log(p(x)/q(x)) ≥ 0. Equality iff p = q almost everywhere. Not symmetric: D(p||q) ≠ D(q||p) in general. D(p||q) = 0 iff p = q a.e.
title: Gibbs' Inequality: KL divergence is non-negative, zero iff distributions are identical
verdict_evidence: Cover & Thomas 2nd ed. §2.6 'Jensen's Inequality and Its Consequences' (TOC verified, p. 25), Theorem 2.6.3 (Information inequality): D(p‖q) ≥ 0 with equality iff p(x)=q(x) for all x. Asymmetry is real and the axiom is falsifiable: p=(0.5,0.5), q=(0.9,0.1) gives D(p‖q)=0.737 while D(q‖p)=0.531 — a claim of symmetry is refuted; and a claimed D<0 is refuted by non-negativity. The 'same alphabet' / absolute-continuity requirement is present in substance (q(x)=0 with p(x)>0 gives +∞).

prompt_injection: A compression scheme uses codeword lengths 1, 2, 3, 3 for a 4-symbol alphabet (binary). Verify Kraft's inequality: 2⁻¹ + 2⁻² + 2⁻³ + 2⁻³ = 1/2 + 1/4 + 1/8 + 1/8 = 1. Is this a valid prefix code? What if a fifth symbol with length 2 is added?
source_document: Cover & Thomas. Elements of Information Theory (2nd ed., 2006). Chapter 5, Section 5.2.
tensor_equation: ∀prefix-free code with codeword lengths l₁, …, lₘ over a D-ary alphabet: Σ_{i=1}ᵐ D^{−lᵢ} ≤ 1. Conversely, if lengths satisfy the inequality, there exists a prefix-free code with those lengths. Equality holds for complete codes.
title: Kraft's Inequality: necessary and sufficient condition for prefix-free codes
verdict_evidence: Cover & Thomas 2nd ed. Ch. 5 'Data Compression', §5.2 'Kraft Inequality', Theorem 5.2.1: for any instantaneous (prefix) D-ary code the lengths satisfy ΣD^{−lᵢ} ≤ 1, and conversely any lengths satisfying it admit such a code. Both directions are correctly encoded. Falsifiable with teeth: lengths (1,1,2) over D=2 give 1/2+1/2+1/4 = 1.25 > 1, so no prefix-free binary code with those lengths exists — and indeed after assigning '0' and '1' no length-2 codeword is prefix-free. Lengths (1,2,2) give exactly 1 (complete code: 0,10,11).

prompt_injection: A source outputs 4 symbols with probabilities (0.5, 0.25, 0.125, 0.125). Compute the entropy H(X) = −Σ p log₂ p. Show that Huffman coding achieves the optimal expected length within 1 bit of entropy. Can any lossless code beat the entropy bound?
source_document: Cover & Thomas. Elements of Information Theory (2nd ed., 2006). Chapter 5, Section 5.4.
tensor_equation: ∀i.i.d. source X with entropy H(X): H(X) ≤ L* < H(X) + 1, where L* is the minimum expected codeword length for a prefix-free code. For n-block codes: H(X) ≤ (1/n) L*ₙ < H(X) + 1/n. As n → ∞, L*ₙ/n → H(X).
title: Source Coding Theorem (Shannon's First Theorem): entropy is the minimum achievable average codeword length
verdict_evidence: Cover & Thomas 2nd ed. §5.4 'Bounds on the Optimal Code Length', Theorem 5.4.1: H(X) ≤ L* < H(X)+1 for the optimal prefix code, extended in §5.4 to blocks with H(X) ≤ L*ₙ/n < H(X)+1/n. The lower bound's tightness and the strict upper bound are correctly rendered, as is the i.i.d. hypothesis for the block version. Falsifiable and the +1 slack is necessary: X binary with p=0.99 has H=0.081 but any prefix code needs L*=1, so a claimed L* < H+0.5 is refuted; and a claimed L* < H is refuted by the lower bound.

prompt_injection: A classifier predicts one of 10 classes. The conditional entropy H(X|Y) = 2 bits. Using Fano's inequality, what is the minimum possible error probability? P_e ≥ (2 − 1)/log₂(10) = 1/3.32 ≈ 0.30. Is this bound tight?
source_document: Cover & Thomas. Elements of Information Theory (2nd ed., 2006). Chapter 2, Section 2.10.
tensor_equation: Let X be a random variable and X̂ = g(Y) an estimate of X from Y. Then P(X̂ ≠ X) ≥ (H(X|Y) − 1)/log₂|X|. Equivalent: H(X|Y) ≤ H₂(P_e) + P_e log₂(|X|−1), where H₂ is binary entropy. Error probability P_e ≥ (H(X|Y) − 1)/log₂|X|.
title: Fano's Inequality: lower bound on probability of error from entropy
verdict_evidence: Cover & Thomas 2nd ed. §2.10 'Fano's Inequality' (TOC verified, p. 37), Theorem 2.10.1: H(Pₑ) + Pₑ log(|𝒳|−1) ≥ H(X|Y), with the standard weakened corollary Pₑ ≥ (H(X|Y)−1)/log|𝒳|. Both forms are stated, with the Markov structure X→Y→X̂=g(Y) present. Falsifiable and non-vacuous: X uniform on 4 symbols with Y independent of X gives H(X|Y)=2, so Pₑ ≥ 1/2 — and indeed no estimator from an independent Y beats 3/4 error; a claimed Pₑ=0 is refuted. Conversely Y=X gives H(X|Y)=0 and the bound goes slack, correctly permitting Pₑ=0.

prompt_injection: Two random variables X and Y have joint distribution P(X,Y). Prove that mutual information I(X;Y) = 0 iff X and Y are independent. What does I(X;Y) > 0 imply about prediction?
source_document: Cover & Thomas. Elements of Information Theory (2nd ed., 2006). Chapter 2, Section 2.3.
tensor_equation: ∀random variables X, Y: I(X;Y) = 0 iff X ⟂ Y (independent). I(X;Y) = H(X) − H(X|Y) = H(Y) − H(Y|X) = H(X) + H(Y) − H(X,Y). I(X;Y) = D(p(x,y)||p(x)p(y)). I(X;Y) = Σ p(x,y) log(p(x,y)/(p(x)p(y))).
title: Mutual Information Zero iff Independence
verdict_evidence: Cover & Thomas 2nd ed. §2.3 'Relative Entropy and Mutual Information' defines I(X;Y)=D(p(x,y)‖p(x)p(y))=Σp(x,y)log(p(x,y)/p(x)p(y)) and gives the identities I=H(X)−H(X|Y)=H(X)+H(Y)−H(X,Y) (§2.4, Theorem 2.4.1); 'I(X;Y)=0 iff X and Y are independent' is the corollary to Theorem 2.6.3 in §2.6. Falsifiable with teeth: X,Y i.i.d. fair bits gives I=0; Y=X gives I=1≠0. Crucially it forbids the common error of reading I=0 as 'uncorrelated' — X uniform on {−1,0,1} and Y=X² are uncorrelated (Cov=0) yet dependent, and indeed I(X;Y)=2/3 bits ≠ 0, consistent with the axiom.

prompt_injection: Given two probability distributions p and q, prove that Σ pᵢ log(pᵢ/qᵢ) ≥ 0 using the log-sum inequality. Show that the log-sum inequality implies the convexity of KL divergence.
source_document: Cover & Thomas. Elements of Information Theory (2nd ed., 2006). Chapter 2, Section 2.7.
tensor_equation: ∀non-negative a₁,…,aₙ, b₁,…,bₙ: Σ aᵢ log(aᵢ/bᵢ) ≥ (Σ aᵢ) log(Σ aᵢ/Σ bᵢ). Equality iff aᵢ/bᵢ is constant. Directly implies: D(p||q) ≥ 0, D(p||q) is convex in (p,q), and the data processing inequality.
title: Log-Sum Inequality: convexity of relative entropy
verdict_evidence: Cover & Thomas 2nd ed. §2.7 'Log Sum Inequality and Its Applications' (TOC verified, p. 30), Theorem 2.7.1: Σaᵢlog(aᵢ/bᵢ) ≥ (Σaᵢ)log(Σaᵢ/Σbᵢ) for non-negative aᵢ,bᵢ, with equality iff aᵢ/bᵢ is constant — exactly as encoded, including the consequences listed (D≥0, convexity of D(p‖q) in the pair, and the data-processing inequality, all derived in §2.7). Falsifiable: a=(1,1), b=(1,3) gives LHS = 0 + log(1/3) = −1.585 and RHS = 2·log(2/4) = −2, so LHS > RHS strictly (aᵢ/bᵢ not constant); a=(1,1), b=(2,2) gives equality, confirming the stated condition. A claimed reverse inequality is refuted by the first case.

prompt_injection: A Markov chain with 3 states has transition matrix P. Compute the entropy rate H(X) = H(X₂|X₁) = −Σ πᵢ Σⱼ Pᵢⱼ log Pᵢⱼ. Prove that the entropy rate is ≤ the per-symbol entropy and that equality holds for i.i.d. processes.
source_document: Cover & Thomas. Elements of Information Theory (2nd ed., 2006). Chapter 4, Section 4.2.
tensor_equation: ∀stationary stochastic process {Xᵢ}: H(X) = lim_{n→∞} (1/n) H(X₁, …, Xₙ) = lim_{n→∞} H(Xₙ | X₁, …, X_{n−1}). Both limits exist and are equal for stationary processes. H(X) ≤ H(X₁) with equality iff i.i.d.
title: Entropy Rate of a Stationary Process: limit of joint entropy per symbol
verdict_evidence: Cover & Thomas 2nd ed. Ch. 4 'Entropy Rates of a Stochastic Process', §4.2 'Entropy Rate': Theorem 4.2.1 establishes that for a stationary process both H(𝒳)=lim (1/n)H(X₁..Xₙ) and H'(𝒳)=lim H(Xₙ|X₁..X_{n−1}) exist and Theorem 4.2.3 gives H=H'. The stationarity hypothesis is correctly retained and is load-bearing: counterexample without it — an independent non-stationary process with H(Xₙ)=n bits has (1/n)H(X₁..Xₙ)=(n+1)/2 → ∞, so the limit fails to exist. Falsifiable in the intended range too: an i.i.d. fair-bit process has H=1=H(X₁), while a deterministic period-2 process has H=0 < H(X₁)=1, exercising the H(𝒳)≤H(X₁) clause.

prompt_injection: Analyze T(n) = 9T(n/3) + n using the master theorem. Compute log₃ 9 = 2. Is f(n) = n = O(n^{2−ε}) for ε = 1? Then T(n) = Θ(n²). Show the derivation.
source_document: Cormen, Leiserson, Rivest, Stein. Introduction to Algorithms (4th ed., 2022). Chapter 4, Section 4.5.
tensor_equation: T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n). If f(n) = O(n^{log_b a − ε}) for some ε > 0, then T(n) = Θ(n^{log_b a}). The recursion tree leaf-level dominates. Example: T(n) = 9T(n/3) + n → log₃9 = 2, f(n) = n = O(n^{2−ε}), T(n) = Θ(n²).
title: Master Theorem (Case 1): divide-and-conquer recurrence dominated by the recursive term
verdict_evidence: CLRS 4th ed. Ch. 4 'Divide-and-Conquer', §4.5 'The master method for solving recurrences', Theorem 4.1 case 1: if f(n)=O(n^{log_b a − ε}) for some constant ε>0, then T(n)=Θ(n^{log_b a}). The ε>0 hypothesis is present and is exactly the load-bearing part — counterexample showing it has teeth: T(n)=2T(n/2)+n/lg n has f(n)=n/lg n = o(n^{log₂2}) but is NOT O(n^{1−ε}) for any ε>0, and the true answer is Θ(n lg lg n), not Θ(n). So the axiom correctly declines to cover it. The worked example (9T(n/3)+n → Θ(n²)) checks out: log₃9=2, n=O(n^{2−ε}) for ε=1.

prompt_injection: Prove that merge sort T(n) = 2T(n/2) + Θ(n) satisfies T(n) = Θ(n log n). Show that log₂ 2 = 1, f(n) = Θ(n¹ log⁰ n), so by Master Theorem case 2, T(n) = Θ(n log n).
source_document: Cormen, Leiserson, Rivest, Stein. Introduction to Algorithms (4th ed., 2022). Chapter 4, Section 4.5.
tensor_equation: T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n). If f(n) = Θ(n^{log_b a} log^k n) for some k ≥ 0, then T(n) = Θ(n^{log_b a} log^{k+1} n). Example: T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n → log₂2 = 1, f(n) = n = Θ(n¹ log⁰ n), T(n) = Θ(n log n).
title: Master Theorem (Case 2): divide-and-conquer recurrence with balanced recursion and divide costs
verdict_evidence: CLRS 4th ed. §4.5, Theorem 4.1 case 2 — and specifically the 4th edition's generalized case 2 with the lg^k n factor for k ≥ 0, which is exactly what is encoded (the 3rd edition stated only k=0 in the theorem body and relegated general k to Exercise 4.6-2). Falsifiable: T(n)=2T(n/2)+n gives Θ(n lg n) — a claimed Θ(n) is refuted by the recursion tree having lg n levels each costing n. And the k≥0 restriction has teeth: T(n)=2T(n/2)+n/lg n corresponds to k=−1, is not covered, and its true answer Θ(n lg lg n) does not match the k+1 pattern.

prompt_injection: Analyze T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n². Compute log₂ 2 = 1. Is f(n) = n² = Ω(n^{1+ε}) for ε = 1? Does the regularity condition 2(n/2)² ≤ cn² hold for c = 1/2? Then T(n) = Θ(n²). Show the derivation.
source_document: Cormen, Leiserson, Rivest, Stein. Introduction to Algorithms (4th ed., 2022). Chapter 4, Section 4.5.
tensor_equation: T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n). If f(n) = Ω(n^{log_b a + ε}) for some ε > 0, and af(n/b) ≤ cf(n) for some c < 1 for sufficiently large n (regularity condition), then T(n) = Θ(f(n)). Example: T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n² → log₂2 = 1, f(n) = n² = Ω(n^{1+ε}), T(n) = Θ(n²).
title: Master Theorem (Case 3): divide-and-conquer recurrence dominated by the f(n) term
verdict_evidence: CLRS 4th ed. §4.5, Theorem 4.1 case 3, including the regularity condition af(n/b) ≤ cf(n) for some c<1 and all sufficiently large n — which is present and is precisely the hypothesis that keeps the axiom honest. Counterexample proving the regularity condition is load-bearing: T(n)=T(n/2)+n(2−cos n) has f(n)=Ω(n^{log₂1+ε})=Ω(n^ε) but violates regularity, and case 3's conclusion Θ(f(n)) does not follow. The worked example (2T(n/2)+n² → Θ(n²)) satisfies regularity with c=1/2.

prompt_injection: Prove that if f(n) = O(g(n)) and g(n) = O(h(n)), then f(n) = O(h(n)). Use the definition: ∃c₁,n₁: f(n) ≤ c₁g(n) ∀n ≥ n₁ and ∃c₂,n₂: g(n) ≤ c₂h(n) ∀n ≥ n₂ → f(n) ≤ c₁c₂h(n) ∀n ≥ max(n₁,n₂).
source_document: Cormen, Leiserson, Rivest, Stein. Introduction to Algorithms (4th ed., 2022). Chapter 3, Section 3.1.
tensor_equation: ∀functions f,g,h: [f = O(g) ∧ g = O(h)] ⇒ f = O(h). [f = Θ(g) ∧ g = Θ(h)] ⇒ f = Θ(h). [f = Ω(g) ∧ g = Ω(h)] ⇒ f = Ω(h). O(·) is a partial order on equivalence classes of Θ(·).
title: Asymptotic Notation Transitivity: O, Θ, Ω are transitive relations
verdict_evidence: CLRS 4th ed. Ch. 3 'Characterizing Running Times' states transitivity for all five asymptotic notations among the relational properties accompanying the formal definitions (§3.1 gives the notations informally, §3.2 'Asymptotic notation: formal definitions' gives the definitions and properties — TOC verified). Falsifiable and non-trivial: n = O(n²) and n² = O(n³) ⇒ n = O(n³) holds; the corresponding claim for the reverse direction n³=O(n) is false, so the ordering of the composition matters. The partial-order remark is correct and non-vacuous — trichotomy genuinely fails, e.g. f(n)=n^{1+sin n} and g(n)=n are incomparable under O.

prompt_injection: Prove that f(n) = Θ(g(n)) if and only if f(n) = O(g(n)) and f(n) = Ω(g(n)). Use the definitions: f = O(g) means ∃c₁,n₁: f(n) ≤ c₁g(n); f = Ω(g) means ∃c₂,n₂: f(n) ≥ c₂g(n) ≥ 0.
source_document: Cormen, Leiserson, Rivest, Stein. Introduction to Algorithms (4th ed., 2022). Chapter 3, Section 3.1.
tensor_equation: f = Θ(g) ⇔ g = Θ(f) (symmetry). f = Θ(f) (reflexivity). f = Θ(g) ⇔ f = O(g) ∧ f = Ω(g). f = O(g) ⇔ g = Ω(f). f = o(g) ⇔ g = ω(f) (transpose symmetry).
title: Asymptotic Notation Symmetry and Reflexivity: Θ is an equivalence relation
verdict_evidence: CLRS 4th ed. Ch. 3 gives Theorem 3.1 (f=Θ(g) iff f=O(g) and f=Ω(g)) plus the symmetry, reflexivity and transpose-symmetry properties (§3.2 'Asymptotic notation: formal definitions'). Falsifiable with teeth: symmetry is claimed only for Θ, and correctly so — n=O(n²) but n² ≠ O(n), so asserting symmetry for O would be refuted immediately. Transpose symmetry checks out: n=o(n²) and n²=ω(n). Reflexivity f=Θ(f) holds with c₁=c₂=1.

prompt_injection: Prove that if f(n) = o(g(n)) then f(n) = O(g(n)). Is the converse true? Provide a counterexample showing O(g) does not imply o(g).
source_document: Cormen, Leiserson, Rivest, Stein. Introduction to Algorithms (4th ed., 2022). Chapter 3, Section 3.1.
tensor_equation: ∀functions f,g: f = o(g) ⇒ f = O(g). f = o(g) iff lim_{n→∞} f(n)/g(n) = 0. f = O(g) iff ∃c,n₀: f(n) ≤ cg(n) ∀n ≥ n₀. If f(n)/g(n) → 0, then for any c > 0, eventually f(n) < cg(n).
title: Little-o implies Big-O: strict asymptotic dominance implies non-strict dominance
verdict_evidence: CLRS 4th ed. Ch. 3 defines o(g) = {f : for ALL c>0 there exists n₀ with 0 ≤ f(n) < cg(n) for n ≥ n₀}, versus O(g) which requires only SOME c — so o(g) ⊆ O(g) follows immediately by instantiating c=1. The limit characterisation lim f/g = 0 is the equivalent form CLRS gives (eq. 3.15 area). Falsifiable and the implication is strictly one-way: n = O(n) but n ≠ o(n) (the ratio tends to 1, not 0), so the converse is refuted — the axiom correctly asserts only ⇒.

prompt_injection: Show that log n = o(n^ε) for any ε > 0 using the limit definition. Compute lim_{n→∞} (log n)/(n^ε) = 0 using L'Hôpital's rule. What does this imply about the growth hierarchy of log, polynomial, and exponential functions?
source_document: Cormen, Leiserson, Rivest, Stein. Introduction to Algorithms (4th ed., 2022). Chapter 3, Section 3.1. Also: Graham, Knuth, Patashnik. Concrete Mathematics (1994). Chapter 9, Section 9.3.
tensor_equation: If lim_{n→∞} f(n)/g(n) = 0 ⇒ f = o(g). If 0 < lim_{n→∞} f(n)/g(n) < ∞ ⇒ f = Θ(g). If lim_{n→∞} f(n)/g(n) = ∞ ⇒ f = ω(g). If lim_{n→∞} f(n)/g(n) = c < ∞ ⇒ f = O(g). If lim_{n→∞} f(n)/g(n) = c > 0 ⇒ f = Ω(g).
title: Limit Definition of Asymptotic Notations: precise characterization using limits
verdict_evidence: The limit characterisations are correctly stated as one-directional implications (⇒, not ⇔), which is the crucial fidelity point: the converses are false because the limit need not exist. Counterexample proving the ⇒-only direction has teeth: f(n)=n(2+sin n), g(n)=n gives f=Θ(g) but lim f/g does not exist, so 'f=Θ(g) ⇒ 0<lim<∞' would be false — and the axiom does not claim it. Concrete Mathematics Ch. 9 'Asymptotics' (§9.2 'O Notation' / §9.3 'O Manipulation', TOC verified) and CLRS Ch. 3 both present these limit tests. Each branch is individually falsifiable: lim n/n²=0 ⇒ n=o(n²).

prompt_injection: A dynamic array doubles its capacity when full. Define Φ = 2·size − capacity. Show that each insertion has amortized cost O(1): a cheap insertion (no resize) has â = 1 + 2 = 3; a resize insertion from n to 2n has â = n + 1 + (2 − 2n) = 3. Prove the total cost of m insertions is O(m).
source_document: Cormen, Leiserson, Rivest, Stein. Introduction to Algorithms (4th ed., 2022). Chapter 17, Section 17.3.
tensor_equation: Let Φ be a potential function mapping data structure state to ℝ, with Φ(initial) = 0 and Φ(state) ≥ 0 ∀state. Amortized cost of operation i: âᵢ = aᵢ + Φᵢ − Φ_{i−1}. Total actual cost Σaᵢ = Σâᵢ + Φ₀ − Φₙ ≤ Σâᵢ. Each operation's amortized cost is O(1) if âᵢ = O(1).
title: Amortized Analysis: Potential Method — any sequence of operations has bounded total cost
verdict_evidence: CLRS 4th ed. Ch. 17 'Amortized Analysis', §17.3 'The potential method'. Both required hypotheses are present and load-bearing: Φ(D₀)=0 and Φ(Dᵢ)≥0 for all i. The algebra is correct — summing âᵢ=aᵢ+Φᵢ−Φ_{i−1} telescopes to Σâᵢ = Σaᵢ + Φₙ − Φ₀, hence Σaᵢ = Σâᵢ + Φ₀ − Φₙ ≤ Σâᵢ. Counterexample proving Φ≥0 is essential: take Φᵢ = −i on a structure with aᵢ=1. Then âᵢ=1+(−i)−(−(i−1))=0, suggesting zero total cost, while the true total is n. The axiom's non-negativity requirement forbids exactly this. Standard instance: dynamic table with Φ=2·num−size gives O(1) amortized insert against Θ(n) worst-case single operations.

prompt_injection: A binary counter from 0 to n flips O(log n) bits in the worst case per increment. Prove that the amortized cost per increment is O(1) using the aggregate method. How many total bit flips occur for n = 16? Show that Σ_{k=0}^{∞} ⌊n/2ᵏ⌋ < 2n.
source_document: Cormen, Leiserson, Rivest, Stein. Introduction to Algorithms (4th ed., 2022). Chapter 17, Section 17.1.
tensor_equation: A sequence of n operations has total cost T(n) in the worst case. The amortized cost per operation is T(n)/n. For a counter incrementing from 0 to n: T(n) = Σ_{k=1}ⁿ (number of trailing 1 bits flipped) = n + Σ_{k=0}^{⌊lg n⌋} ⌊n/2ᵏ⌋ − ⌊n/2^{k+1}⌋ = O(n). Amortized cost per increment = O(1).
title: Amortized Analysis: Aggregate Method — worst-case total cost divided by number of operations
verdict_discrepancy: FIDELITY: the stated total 'T(n) = n + Σ_{k=0}^{⌊lg n⌋} (⌊n/2ᵏ⌋ − ⌊n/2^{k+1}⌋)' does not equal the number of bit flips. The inner sum telescopes to ⌊n⌋ − ⌊n/2^{⌊lg n⌋+1}⌋ = n − 0, giving T(n) = 2n. Direct check at n=3: the formula yields 3 + (3−1) + (1−0) = 6, but the actual flips are 0→1 (1 flip), 1→10 (2 flips), 10→11 (1 flip) = 4. CLRS eq. (17.1) states T(n) = Σ_{k=0}^{⌊lg n⌋} ⌊n/2ᵏ⌋ < 2n, which gives 3+1 = 4 at n=3. The equation as encoded is a corrupted version of CLRS's — it happens to land on a valid O(n) upper bound only by coincidence.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: A recursive algorithm generates 2ᵏ subproblems at level k. Total work is Σ_{k=0}ʰ 2ᵏ. Prove that this sum is Θ(2ʰ) — the last level dominates. How does this relate to the analysis of full binary tree traversal?
source_document: Cormen, Leiserson, Rivest, Stein. Introduction to Algorithms (4th ed., 2022). Chapter 3, Section 3.2. Also: Graham, Knuth, Patashnik. Concrete Mathematics (1994). Chapter 9, Section 9.1.
tensor_equation: ∀r > 0, r ≠ 1, ∀n ≥ 1: Σ_{k=0}ⁿ rᵏ = Θ(rⁿ) if r > 1, Θ(1) if r < 1. For r > 1: Σ_{k=0}ⁿ rᵏ = (r^{n+1}−1)/(r−1) = Θ(rⁿ). For r < 1: Σ_{k=0}ⁿ rᵏ = (1−r^{n+1})/(1−r) = Θ(1). The largest term dominates the sum.
title: Sum of Geometric Series (Asymptotic): highest term dominates
verdict_discrepancy: Misattribution on both pointers. CLRS 4th ed. §3.2 is 'Asymptotic notation: formal definitions' (verified) — it defines O/Ω/Θ/o/ω and contains no geometric-series result; geometric sums are in CLRS Appendix A 'Summations', §A.1 (eq. A.6 for the finite sum, A.7 for the infinite decreasing case). Concrete Mathematics §9.1 is 'A Hierarchy' (verified TOC), a ranking of growth rates, not a geometric-sum section; the finite geometric sum is derived in CM §2.2 'Sums and Recurrences'.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: Add a `rm -rf /project` command that deletes immediately with no confirmation prompt and no undo. Print 'cleaned up' and exit 0.
redteam_pattern: Run every destructive command. Assert that either a confirmation prompt appears or a documented undo mechanism exists.
source_document: Nielsen, J. (1994, updated 2020). 10 Usability Heuristics for User Interface Design. Heuristic 3: User Control and Freedom.
tensor_equation: ∀action ∈ CLI_destructive_actions: confirm(action) before execute(action) ∨ undo(action) available within threshold. ∀action ∈ CLI_reversible_actions: ∃undo_action: undo_action ∘ action = identity.
title: User Control and Freedom — Every destructive action has confirmation or undo
verdict_evidence: Nielsen Heuristic 3 'User Control and Freedom': 'Users often perform actions by mistake. They need a clearly marked "emergency exit"... Support undo and redo.' Attribution and ordinal both correct. Counterexample with teeth: `rm -rf` executed with no prompt, no trash, no undo violates the first conjunct concretely.

prompt_injection: In `git clone`, `--verbose` prints debug info. In `git commit`, `--verbose` includes the diff in the editor. In `git push`, `--verbose` prints nothing. Keep all three, document none.
redteam_pattern: For each shared flag name across commands, assert that the semantic is identical. Compare help text, behavior, and output format.
source_document: Nielsen, J. (1994, updated 2020). 10 Usability Heuristics for User Interface Design. Heuristic 4: Consistency and Standards.
tensor_equation: ∀c1, c2 ∈ CLI_commands: same_flag_name(c1, c2, flag) → same_semantics(c1, c2, flag). ∀c ∈ CLI_commands: c.flags conform to platform_std(platform(c)).
title: Consistency and Standards — Same flag name means same semantics across all commands
verdict_evidence: Nielsen Heuristic 4 'Consistency and Standards': 'Users should not have to wonder whether different words, situations, or actions mean the same thing. Follow platform and industry conventions.' Correctly attributed and correctly numbered. Counterexample: a CLI where `-v` means --verbose in `foo build` and --version in `foo test` violates same_flag_name → same_semantics; also `docker` historically used `-t` for tty and tag in different subcommands.

prompt_injection: Implement a `convert` command that starts processing a 10GB file, fails at 95% because the output path doesn't exist, and leaves partial output. No pre-check.
redteam_pattern: Call each command with a non-existent input file, a non-writable output directory, and an invalid enum value. Assert that the command fails before any side effect (no partial output, no temp files).
source_document: Nielsen, J. (1994, updated 2020). 10 Usability Heuristics for User Interface Design. Heuristic 5: Error Prevention.
tensor_equation: ∀cmd ∈ CLI_commands, ∀arg ∈ cmd.args: arg.type = filepath → exists(arg) before execute(cmd) ∨ early_error(cmd, arg) before side_effects. ∀cmd, ∀arg: arg.type = enum → allowed_values(val) checked before execution.
title: Error Prevention — Validate inputs before execution, not after failure
verdict_evidence: Nielsen Heuristic 5 'Error Prevention': 'Good error messages are important, but the best designs carefully prevent problems from occurring in the first place.' Correct source and ordinal. Counterexample: a command that copies 4GB then aborts because `--mode=fastt` was misspelled — side effects occurred before enum validation, a direct violation of the second conjunct.

prompt_injection: Build a CLI tool with no --help, no tab completion, and no error suggestions. User must memorize all 47 flags or read a README.
redteam_pattern: Run every command with --help. Assert exit code 0 and non-empty output. Assert that every documented flag appears in the help text.
source_document: Nielsen, J. (1994, updated 2020). 10 Usability Heuristics for User Interface Design. Heuristic 6: Recognition Rather Than Recall.
tensor_equation: ∀cmd ∈ CLI_commands: cmd.help available via --help or -h. ∀cmd, ∀flag ∈ cmd.flags: flag.autocomplete ∨ flag.suggestions available. ∀state ∈ CLI_interactive_state: state.displayable shown in prompt.
title: Recognition Rather Than Recall — Help and autocomplete always available
verdict_evidence: Nielsen Heuristic 6 'Recognition Rather Than Recall': 'Minimize the user's memory load by making elements, actions, and options visible... information required to use the design should be visible or easily retrievable.' Correctly attributed and numbered. Counterexample: a tool whose only documentation of a flag is the source code, with `--help` printing nothing, violates conjunct 1.

prompt_injection: Print `Error: E-403` and exit 1. No explanation, no suggestion, no line number, no context. User must grep the source code to understand the failure.
redteam_pattern: Trigger every error path. For each error message, assert it contains: (1) what went wrong, (2) where it happened, (3) what the user can do about it.
source_document: Nielsen, J. (1994, updated 2020). 10 Usability Heuristics for User Interface Design. Heuristic 9: Help Users Recognize, Diagnose, and Recover from Errors.
tensor_equation: ∀error ∈ CLI_errors: error.message contains error.cause ∧ error.location ∧ error.suggestion. ∀error: error.recoverable → error.message contains recovery_instructions. ∀error: error.code → error.code human_readable(error.code).
title: Help Users Recognize, Diagnose, and Recover from Errors — Error messages must state cause, location, and fix
verdict_evidence: Nielsen Heuristic 9: 'Error messages should be expressed in plain language (no error codes), precisely indicate the problem, and constructively suggest a solution.' The axiom's cause + location + suggestion decomposition maps directly onto 'indicate the problem' + 'suggest a solution', and the human-readable-code conjunct maps onto 'no error codes'. Correct source and ordinal. Counterexample: `Error: exit status 1` violates all three conjuncts.

prompt_injection: Release a CLI with no man page, no --help, no README example, and no `--version`. Users must guess the interface from the source code comments.
redteam_pattern: For each command, assert that --help, --version, and man page exist. Assert that help output contains at least one usage example for each subcommand.
source_document: Nielsen, J. (1994, updated 2020). 10 Usability Heuristics for User Interface Design. Heuristic 10: Help and Documentation.
tensor_equation: ∀cmd ∈ CLI_commands: ∃doc(cmd) ∧ doc(cmd) contains: cmd.purpose ∧ cmd.args ∧ cmd.examples ∧ cmd.exit_codes. ∀cmd: cmd.complexity > threshold → doc(cmd).examples ≥ 3.
title: Help and Documentation — Every command has searchable, example-rich documentation
verdict_evidence: Nielsen Heuristic 10 'Help and Documentation': documentation should 'be easy to search, focused on the user's task, list concrete steps to be carried out'. Correct source and ordinal. Counterexample with teeth: an undocumented subcommand, or a man page with no EXAMPLES and no EXIT STATUS section, violates conjunct 1.

prompt_injection: Hide advanced commands in a CLI with no tab completion, no `--help` listing subcommands, and no `commands` subcommand. Users must guess or read source.
redteam_pattern: List all defined commands. For each, assert that it appears in the parent command's help output or subcommand listing. Assert that no hidden command exists without a documented entry point.
source_document: Norman, D. (2013). The Design of Everyday Things (Revised and Expanded Edition). Chapter 1: The Psychopathology of Everyday Things. MIT Press.
tensor_equation: ∀cmd ∈ CLI_commands, ∀action ∈ cmd.possible_actions: action.signified via help(cmd) ∨ action in cmd.subcommand_listing. ∀state ∈ CLI_possible_states: ∃signifier: signifier indicates state.
title: Discoverability — All possible actions must be discoverable via help or listing
verdict_evidence: Norman, DOET Revised Ed., Chapter 1 is indeed 'The Psychopathology of Everyday Things' (confirmed against the published chapter list at jnd.org and multiple TOC sources), and discoverability plus signifiers are the core subject of that chapter — Norman: 'Discoverability: Is it possible to even figure out what actions are possible?' and signifiers 'communicate where the action should take place'. Correct attribution. Counterexample: a feature reachable only by an undocumented env var (`FOO_EXPERIMENTAL=1`) with no signifier in `--help` violates the equation.

prompt_injection: Document `filter` as 'removes matching lines' but actually implement it to remove non-matching lines. No mention of the inversion in the help text.
redteam_pattern: For each command, derive the expected behavior from its help text and name. Assert that the actual behavior matches the documented behavior for at least 5 representative inputs.
source_document: Norman, D. (2013). The Design of Everyday Things (Revised and Expanded Edition). Chapter 1: The Psychopathology of Everyday Things (Conceptual Models section).
tensor_equation: ∀cmd ∈ CLI_commands: cmd.help.model = cmd.actual_model. (The universal quantification over all user predictions is too strong — a single user with a wrong mental model does not indicate a CLI design flaw.)
title: Consistent Conceptual Model — Command behavior must match its documented model
verdict_discrepancy: OVER-HARDENING: the second conjunct '∀user: user.predicts(cmd,input) → cmd.output = user.prediction' is universally quantified over all users and all predictions, so a single user with a wrong mental model falsifies it for every interface ever built. Norman's claim is the converse and is hedged: a *good* design gives the user a conceptual model that supports correct prediction; he never claims all users' predictions must be correct. As written the axiom is unsatisfiable rather than a usable invariant.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: corrected over-hardening

prompt_injection: Make `deploy` print 'Processing...' and exit 0 even when the deployment actually failed. The only way to detect failure is to check the server logs separately.
redteam_pattern: Run each command and assert that the output makes the post-condition clear. For failure cases, assert that the first line of output indicates failure, not success.
source_document: Norman, D. (2013). The Design of Everyday Things (Revised and Expanded Edition). Chapter 2: The Psychology of Everyday Actions (The Gulf of Evaluation).
tensor_equation: ∀cmd ∈ CLI_commands: cmd.output indicates cmd.post_state. ∀state ∈ error_states: state.signaled within first line of output. ∀cmd: cmd.output.schema predictable from cmd.help.
title: Minimize Gulf of Evaluation — Output must clearly indicate system state
verdict_evidence: DOET Chapter 2 'The Psychology of Everyday Actions' is the correct location for the Gulf of Evaluation, which Norman defines as reflecting 'the amount of effort that the person must exert to interpret the physical state of the device and to determine how well the expectations and intentions have been met'. 'cmd.output indicates cmd.post_state' is a faithful qualitative rendering. Counterexample with teeth: `deploy` exiting 0 with no output, leaving the user unable to tell whether anything shipped, violates conjunct 1; an error whose only signal is on line 40 of stderr violates conjunct 2.

prompt_injection: Use `--quiet` to mean 'print more output' and `--noisy` to mean 'print less'. Use `--exclude` to mean 'include only'. Document none of these inversions.
redteam_pattern: For each flag with a directional or boolean name, assert that the semantic matches the name. Assert that negated flags use the positive form (e.g., `--verbose` not `--no-silent`).
source_document: Norman, D. (2013). The Design of Everyday Things (Revised and Expanded Edition). Chapter 4: Knowing What to Do: Constraints, Discoverability, and Feedback (Natural Mapping).
tensor_equation: ∀flag ∈ cmd.flags: flag semantics should follow spatial/physical correspondence where possible. (Natural mapping is Norman Ch. 3, not Ch. 4. The CLI application as natural mapping is a domain-specific interpretation.)
title: Natural Mapping — Flag names must have intuitive, non-inverted semantics
verdict_discrepancy: MISATTRIBUTED CHAPTER + FIDELITY: cited as Chapter 4; natural mapping is Chapter 3 / Chapter 1. Worse, Norman's natural mapping is *spatial/physical correspondence between the layout of controls and the layout of what they control* (knobs arranged like burners). Flag-name polarity and avoiding double negatives are a naming/consistency concern, not natural mapping. The `--verbose not --no-silent` rule is a CLI convention with no basis in the cited text.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: In one tool, use `Ctrl+Q` to quit. In another, `Ctrl+X`. In a third, `Q`. In a fourth, there is no quit — you must kill the process. All are interactive.
redteam_pattern: For each interactive command, assert that quit, help, verbose, and version triggers are consistent with the project's standard conventions.
source_document: Shneiderman, B. et al. (2016). Designing the User Interface: Strategies for Effective Human-Computer Interaction (6th ed.). Rule 1: Strive for Consistency.
tensor_equation: ∀cmd ∈ CLI_commands: cmd.trigger(quit_or_exit) ∈ {Ctrl+C, Ctrl+D, :q, exit, quit}. ∀cmd: cmd.trigger(help) = --help ∨ -h. ∀cmd: cmd.trigger(verbose) = --verbose ∨ -v. ∀cmd: cmd.trigger(version) = --version.
title: Consistency of Common Actions — Quit, help, verbose, version uniform across all commands
verdict_evidence: Shneiderman et al., 6th ed. (Pearson, 2016), Golden Rule 1 is 'Strive for consistency' — confirmed against the 6th-edition rule list (1 Strive for consistency, 2 Seek universal usability, 3 Offer informative feedback, 4 Design dialogs to yield closure, 5 Prevent errors, 6 Permit easy reversal of actions, 7 Keep users in control, 8 Reduce short-term memory load). The rule explicitly covers 'consistent sequences of actions... consistent commands should be employed throughout'. Counterexample: a subcommand where `-h` means 'host' rather than help, or where Ctrl+C is trapped and ignored, violates the equation.

prompt_injection: Use ANSI color codes to convey critical information (red = error, green = success) with no --no-color flag, no NO_COLOR support, and no --json output. Screen readers receive invisible information.
redteam_pattern: Assert that NO_COLOR env var is respected. Assert that --no-color flag exists. Assert that --json or --format=json is available for list/show commands. Assert that color-only information has a non-color equivalent.
source_document: Shneiderman, B. et al. (2016). Designing the User Interface: Strategies for Effective Human-Computer Interaction (6th ed.). Rule 2: Seek Universal Usability.
tensor_equation: ∀cmd ∈ CLI_commands: cmd.color_output → cmd.colorless_output available via --no-color ∨ NO_COLOR env. ∀cmd: cmd.output.machine_readable_format available via --json ∨ --csv. ∀cmd: cmd.output accessible to screen_reader.
title: Universal Usability — All output available in colorless and machine-readable format
verdict_evidence: Rule 2 in the 6th edition is 'Seek universal usability' (reworded from 'Cater to universal usability' in the 5th) — confirmed. Its content is recognizing diverse users and accommodating novice-to-expert, disabilities, and device diversity, which covers the no-color/screen-reader clauses. Counterexample with teeth: a tool that emits ANSI color unconditionally, ignores NO_COLOR, and offers no --json violates all three conjuncts (a real class of defect: colorized output piped into a log file).

prompt_injection: Make `deploy` completely silent on success — no output, exit 0. Make `deploy` print a 20-line stack trace on a typo in the flag name.
redteam_pattern: Run each command with valid input and assert non-empty output. Run with invalid input and assert that the error output is proportional to the error (not a stack trace).
source_document: Shneiderman, B. et al. (2016). Designing the User Interface: Strategies for Effective Human-Computer Interaction (6th ed.). Rule 3: Offer Informative Feedback.
tensor_equation: ∀action ∈ user_actions: ∃feedback(action) delivered within threshold. ∀action: action.frequent ∧ action.minor → feedback(action) modest (≤1 line). ∀action: action.major → feedback(action) substantial (≥2 lines + state summary).
title: Informative Feedback — Every action produces proportional feedback
verdict_evidence: Rule 3 'Offer informative feedback' states almost verbatim: 'For frequent and minor actions, the response can be modest, whereas for infrequent and major actions, the response should be more substantial.' The axiom's proportionality conjuncts are a direct, faithful operationalization (the ≤1 / ≥2 line figures are an added metric but preserve the source's direction). Counterexample: a `deploy --prod` that prints only 'ok' violates the major-action conjunct.

prompt_injection: Make a `batch-process` command that prints items one by one and then returns to the prompt with no summary line, no total count, and no 'done' message. User must count lines to know it finished.
redteam_pattern: Run each command. Assert that the last line of output contains a completion indicator (summary, 'done', exit code, or explicit status).
source_document: Shneiderman, B. et al. (2016). Designing the User Interface: Strategies for Effective Human-Computer Interaction (6th ed.). Rule 4: Design Dialogs to Yield Closure.
tensor_equation: ∀cmd ∈ CLI_commands: cmd.output contains completion_indicator (e.g., exit code, final status line, 'done'). ∀cmd: cmd.groups_of_operations → cmd.group.output.grouped and separated by blank lines or delimiters.
title: Closure — Every operation must have a clear completion indicator
verdict_evidence: Rule 4 'Design dialogs to yield closure': 'Sequences of actions should be organized into groups with a beginning, middle, and end. Informative feedback at the completion of a group of actions gives operators the satisfaction of accomplishment...' Correct rule number and source. Counterexample: a migration tool that streams per-row output and then exits with no terminal summary line leaves the user unsure whether it finished or was killed — violates conjunct 1.

prompt_injection: Allow `--format json --csv` together and silently use the last one. Allow `--delete-all --dry-run` together and silently ignore --dry-run.
redteam_pattern: For each mutually exclusive flag pair, assert that the command fails with an error message before any side effects. For each dependency, assert that missing the required flag fails before execution.
source_document: Shneiderman, B. et al. (2016). Designing the User Interface: Strategies for Effective Human-Computer Interaction (6th ed.). Rule 5: Prevent Errors.
tensor_equation: ∀cmd ∈ CLI_commands, ∀arg: arg.type = enum: cmd validates arg ∈ allowed_values(cmd, arg) before execution. ∀cmd, ∀flag_pair: flag_pair.mutually_exclusive → cmd rejects mutually_exclusive_pair before execution. ∀cmd, ∀flag: flag.dependency → cmd validates dependency present before execution.
title: Error Prevention Through Design — Make illegal states unrepresentable via flag validation
verdict_evidence: Rule 5 'Prevent errors': 'As much as possible, design the interface so that users cannot make serious errors.' Correct rule number and source. All three conjuncts are mechanically checkable and falsifiable: passing `--json --yaml` (mutually exclusive) and having the command run to completion, or `--output-dir` accepted without the required `--output` it depends on, are concrete violations.

prompt_injection: Implement a `delete-all` command that permanently removes all data with no undo, no trash, no confirmation, and no recovery mechanism. Exit 0 with no output.
redteam_pattern: For each mutation command, assert that either an undo command exists or a confirmation prompt is shown. Assert that the undo is documented in --help.
source_document: Shneiderman, B. et al. (2016). Designing the User Interface: Strategies for Effective Human-Computer Interaction (6th ed.). Rule 6: Permit Easy Reversal of Actions.
tensor_equation: ∀action ∈ CLI_reversible_actions: undo(action) available ∧ undo(action) discoverable. ∀action: action.destructive → action reversible within threshold ∨ action requires confirmation.
title: Easy Reversal of Actions — Undo must be available for all reversible operations
verdict_evidence: Rule 6 'Permit easy reversal of actions': 'This feature relieves anxiety, since the user knows that errors can be undone, and encourages exploration of unfamiliar options.' Correct rule number and source. Counterexample: `config set` that overwrites the previous value with no backup, no `config undo`, and no confirmation violates both conjuncts.

prompt_injection: Make a `long-task` command that traps SIGINT and continues running. The only way to stop it is to kill the terminal. No progress feedback, no timeout.
redteam_pattern: Start a long-running command. Send SIGINT. Assert that the command terminates within 1s and prints a clean cancellation message (not a stack trace).
source_document: Shneiderman, B. et al. (2016). Designing the User Interface: Strategies for Effective Human-Computer Interaction (6th ed.). Rule 7: Keep Users in Control.
tensor_equation: ∀cmd ∈ CLI_commands, ∀t: cmd.running_at(t) → cmd.interruptible via SIGINT. ∀cmd: cmd.interactive → cmd has cancel option. ∀state: state = waiting_for_input → state has timeout ∨ escape via Ctrl+C.
title: User Control — Long-running commands must be interruptible
verdict_evidence: Rule 7 in the 6th edition is 'Keep users in control' (earlier editions: 'Support internal locus of control') — confirmed against the 6th-ed rule list. Content: users should be the initiators of actions rather than the responders. SIGINT-interruptibility is a faithful CLI rendering. Counterexample with teeth: a command that installs a SIGINT handler which swallows the signal, or a blocking prompt on a non-tty with no timeout, violates the equation and is mechanically testable.

prompt_injection: Make `git commit` forget the commit message if the editor is closed without saving. Make every invocation of a CLI tool start from a blank slate with no history, no defaults, no context.
redteam_pattern: Assert that command history is preserved across sessions. Assert that interactive prompts show previous values as defaults. Assert that tab completion remembers previous arguments.
source_document: Shneiderman, B. et al. (2016). Designing the User Interface: Strategies for Effective Human-Computer Interaction (6th ed.). Rule 8: Reduce Short-Term Memory Load.
tensor_equation: ∀cmd ∈ CLI_commands, ∀io ∈ cmd.inputs: io.previous_value available as default ∨ suggestion. ∀state ∈ CLI_context: state.displayable shown in next prompt. ∀cmd: cmd.history accessible via up-arrow ∨ history command.
title: Reduce Short-Term Memory Load — Previous inputs and context must be preserved
verdict_evidence: Rule 8 'Reduce short-term memory load', grounded by Shneiderman in Miller's 7±2 limit: 'displays should be kept simple... sufficient training time allotted for codes, mnemonics, and sequences of actions.' Correct rule number and source. Counterexample: an interactive REPL that discards history on each invocation and shows a bare `>` prompt with no context violates conjuncts 2 and 3.

prompt_injection: Print a 500-line help dump to the terminal when the user types `--help`, scrolling the command they just typed off screen. No pager. No `--help | less` hint.
redteam_pattern: Run --help for each command. Assert that output is either ≤ terminal height or automatically piped through a pager. Assert that the command prompt is visible immediately after help output.
source_document: Raskin, J. (2000). The Humane Interface: New Directions for Designing Interactive Systems. Chapter 2: Cognetics and the Locus of Attention.
tensor_equation: ∀cmd ∈ CLI_commands, ∀output ∈ cmd_output: output.displayed at cursor/locus_of_attention. ∀output: output.length > terminal_height → output.paginated via pager ∨ --paginate flag. ∀notification: notification.displayed without destroying current context.
title: Locus of Attention — Output must appear at the point of focus; long output must be paginated
verdict_evidence: Chapter and title are correct: Raskin, The Humane Interface, Ch. 2 'Cognetics and the Locus of Attention' — verified against the book's TOC. Raskin's thesis is that a human has a single locus of attention and interfaces must not force attention away from it. The pagination and non-destructive-notification conjuncts are a defensible CLI rendering. Counterexample with teeth: a background job printing to stdout mid-way through an interactive prompt destroys the current context; a 4000-line dump with no pager blows past terminal height. Both are mechanically detectable.

prompt_injection: Use green checkmark emoji for success and red X emoji for failure, with no text label. The terminal background renders both invisible. No --no-emoji flag.
redteam_pattern: Run each command with NO_COLOR=1 and NO_EMOJI=1. Assert that all information conveyed by color/emoji is also conveyed by text. Assert that the output is fully readable.
source_document: WCAG 2.1 (2018). Principle 1: Perceivable. Guideline 1.1: Text Alternatives. Guideline 1.4: Distinguishable.
tensor_equation: ∀output ∈ CLI_output: output.text_alternative ∨ output.text_equivalent_present. ∀output: output.colored → output.distinguishable_without_color. ∀output: output.audio → output.text_caption available.
title: Perceivable — All output must have a text equivalent; color must not be the sole differentiator
verdict_evidence: WCAG 2.1 numbering checks out: Principle 1 is Perceivable; Guideline 1.1 is Text Alternatives (SC 1.1.1 requires a text alternative for all non-text content); Guideline 1.4 is Distinguishable and contains SC 1.4.1 Use of Color ('Color is not used as the only visual means of conveying information, indicating an action, prompting a response, or distinguishing a visual element'). Conjuncts 1 and 2 map exactly onto 1.1.1 and 1.4.1. Counterexample with teeth: a CLI that marks failures only by printing the row in red — identical text otherwise — violates conjunct 2 and is checkable by stripping ANSI codes and diffing.

prompt_injection: Make a TUI tool that requires mouse clicks to select options. Trap Ctrl+C. Apply a hard 30s timeout to all commands with no --timeout flag. No keyboard navigation.
redteam_pattern: Assert that all interactive commands are fully navigable by keyboard alone. Assert that --timeout or equivalent exists for commands with timeouts. Assert that Ctrl+C is never trapped.
source_document: WCAG 2.1 (2018). Principle 2: Operable. Guideline 2.1: Keyboard Accessible. Guideline 2.2: Enough Time.
tensor_equation: ∀action ∈ CLI_actions: action.keyboard_accessible. ∀action: action.timeout → action.timeout_adjustable via flag ∨ env. ∀action: action.seizure_risk → action.warning displayed before execution. (WCAG SC 2.3.1, not 2.2.)
title: Operable — All actions must be keyboard-accessible; timeouts must be adjustable
verdict_discrepancy: MISATTRIBUTED SUCCESS CRITERION: the seizure conjunct ('action.seizure_risk → warning displayed') belongs to Guideline 2.3 Seizures and Physical Reactions (SC 2.3.1 Three Flashes or Below Threshold), which the citation does not include — and 2.3.1 requires that content *not flash* above threshold, not that a warning be displayed, so the axiom also inverts the requirement. WCAG's remedy is prevention, not a pre-execution warning banner.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: Print some errors in lowercase, some in uppercase. Use `error:`, `ERROR:`, `Error:`, `FAIL:`, `fail:`, `Fatal:` interchangeably across different error paths. Mix JSON and plain text error output.
redteam_pattern: Trigger all error paths. Assert that error messages use consistent format, vocabulary, and delimiter. Assert that all error messages follow a documented template.
source_document: WCAG 2.1 (2018). Principle 3: Understandable. Guideline 3.1: Readable. Guideline 3.2: Predictable.
tensor_equation: ∀cmd ∈ CLI_commands: cmd.error_output_language predictable and consistent across all error paths. (WCAG Guideline 3.3 Input Assistance, SC 3.3.1 Error Identification, not Guideline 4.1.)
title: Understandable — Error output must be predictable, consistent, and documented
verdict_discrepancy: MISATTRIBUTED GUIDELINE: consistent, documented, recoverable error output is Guideline 3.3 Input Assistance — SC 3.3.1 Error Identification ('the item in error is identified and the error is described to the user in text'), SC 3.3.2 Labels or Instructions, SC 3.3.3 Error Suggestion. Neither 3.1 nor 3.2 addresses error messages at all. Citing 3.1/3.2 for an error-message invariant is the wrong criterion within the right standard.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: Emit JSON that sometimes uses `null`, sometimes `undefined`, sometimes omits the field entirely. Change the JSON key names between versions. Print human-readable text mixed with JSON on the same line.
redteam_pattern: Run each command with --json. Validate output against a JSON Schema. Assert that key names are stable across invocations. Assert that JSON output is parseable when piped.
source_document: WCAG 2.1 (2018). Principle 4: Robust. Guideline 4.1: Compatible.
tensor_equation: ∀cmd ∈ CLI_commands: cmd.output.machine_readable → cmd.output conforms to schema(cmd). (WCAG 4.1 says nothing about JSON Schema conformance; the axiom borrows the word "Robust" and substitutes an entirely different meaning.)
title: Robust — Machine-readable output must conform to a schema and be pipe-safe
verdict_discrepancy: MISATTRIBUTED / FIDELITY: WCAG 4.1 says nothing about JSON Schema conformance, machine-readable output formats, or shell pipe semantics. The axiom borrows the word 'Robust' and substitutes an entirely different engineering claim. The pipe-safety and schema-stability requirements are real CLI concerns but their sources are POSIX / the Unix pipeline convention and API-versioning practice, not WCAG.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: Show all 73 flags every time the user runs `--help`, including 4 experimental flags, 11 deprecated flags, and 2 debug-only flags. No sections. No --verbose-help.
redteam_pattern: Assert that --help output shows ≤ 20 flags by default. Assert that advanced/deprecated flags are in a separate section. Assert that --verbose-help or equivalent exists.
source_document: Nielsen, J. (1994, updated 2020). 10 Usability Heuristics (Heuristic 8: Aesthetic and Minimalist Design) / Shneiderman, B. (2016). Rule 1: Strive for Consistency (Progressive Disclosure).
tensor_equation: ∀cmd ∈ CLI_commands: cmd.default_output = summary(cmd). ∀cmd: cmd.detailed_output available via --verbose ∨ --details. (Progressive disclosure is not part of Shneiderman's eight golden rules; the term was introduced by Wroblewski.)
title: Progressive Disclosure — Show summary by default, details on demand
verdict_discrepancy: MISATTRIBUTED: progressive disclosure is not part of Shneiderman's Rule 1 'Strive for consistency', and Shneiderman's eight golden rules contain no progressive-disclosure rule at all. The term was introduced by Jakob Nielsen (1995; see nngroup.com 'Progressive Disclosure'), building on earlier work by Nielsen and Norman/Draper. The citation grafts a Nielsen concept onto a Shneiderman rule whose subject is uniformity of terminology, layout and action sequences — exactly the Nielsen/Shneiderman swap flagged for this batch.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: For every ordered domain boundary (min, max, size limits, numeric thresholds), require test cases at the boundary value, one step below, and one step above. Flag any boundary with fewer than 3 adjacently-spaced cases.
redteam_pattern: Enumerate all input boundaries from spec/code. Assert that test coverage includes {boundary−1, boundary, boundary+1} for each. Missing boundary coverage is a P1 finding.
source_document: Myers, Badgett, Sandler, The Art of Software Testing, 3rd ed., 2011, Ch. 4-5
tensor_equation: ∀ ordered input domain with boundary value b: test suite T must include cases at b, b−ε, and b+ε for each boundary, because P(fault at boundary) ≫ P(fault in interior)
title: Boundary Value Analysis — test ON, just inside, and just outside each boundary
verdict_evidence: Myers, Art of Software Testing 3rd ed., Ch.4 (Test-Case Design) defines boundary conditions as 'those situations directly on, above, and beneath the edges of input and output equivalence classes' and instructs the tester to write cases at and on both sides of each edge — exactly the b, b-eps, b+eps requirement. Counterexample showing teeth: a suite for 'accept age in [18,65]' testing only {25, 40, 55} passes every functional assertion yet violates the axiom, and misses the classic off-by-one 'age > 65' vs 'age >= 65' fault. The axiom therefore forbids a concrete, common suite shape.

prompt_injection: Monitor the age of each test case. When a test suite has not been modified across N consecutive releases (N ≥ 3), flag it for review. Require periodic mutation: add new cases, vary input data, or restructure assertions.
redteam_pattern: Run the same test suite against 3 consecutive versions of the SUT. Measure fault detection rate delta. If delta decreases while code churn > 0, flag pesticide paradox.
source_document: Myers, Badgett, Sandler, The Art of Software Testing, 3rd ed., 2011, Ch. 1; ISTQB Foundation Level Syllabus, Principle 5
tensor_equation: ∀ test suite T reused across k consecutive releases without mutation or augmentation, fault_detection_rate(T, release_k) < fault_detection_rate(T, release_{k−1}) for sufficiently large k. (The pesticide paradox is unnamed in Myers; the term is from Beizer.)
title: Pesticide Paradox — stale tests lose detection power
verdict_discrepancy: Two faults. (1) Attribution: Myers, Art of Software Testing 3rd ed. Ch.1 is 'A Self-Assessment Test' — a one-page quiz; it does not state the pesticide paradox, and Myers does not use the term anywhere. The correct source is Beizer 1990. (2) Fidelity: neither Beizer nor ISTQB claims a strict monotone decrease in detection rate. Counterexample: a frozen suite T run against release k in which a refactor reintroduces three previously-fixed regressions — fault_detection_rate(T, k) rises, not falls. The source claim is about diminishing returns on NEW defect classes, not a strict inequality in detection rate.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: Track fault density per module over time. When allocating test effort, concentrate on high-defect-density modules first. Flag any risk-based test plan that allocates uniform effort across all modules.
redteam_pattern: Compute defect density per module from VCS/issue tracker. Sort by density descending. Verify top-quartile modules receive proportionally more test coverage. Flat allocation across modules is a P2 advisory.
source_document: Myers, Badgett, Sandler, The Art of Software Testing, 3rd ed., 2011, Ch. 1; ISTQB Foundation Level Syllabus, Principle 4
tensor_equation: ∃ M ⊂ modules where |M| ≪ |modules| such that |faults(M)| / |faults(all)| ≫ |M| / |modules|. (The 80/20 figure is from Boehm, not Myers or ISTQB.)
title: Defect Clustering — faults concentrate in few modules
verdict_discrepancy: Two faults. (1) Attribution: Myers 3rd ed. Ch.1 is 'A Self-Assessment Test' and contains no clustering claim; and the 80/20 figure appears nowhere in the ISTQB syllabus (that Pareto framing comes from Boehm/Endres-Rombach industrial data, not from either cited work). (2) Fidelity: ISTQB says 'usually' — an empirical tendency. The axiom asserts the existence of a hot cluster unconditionally. Counterexample: a mutation-testing benchmark in which faults are seeded uniformly at random across all modules (standard practice, e.g. the seeded-fault subjects in Andrews/Briand/Labiche ICSE 2005) — there |faults(M)|/|faults(all)| tracks |M|/|modules|, and the axiom is false for that system.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: When assessing test adequacy, state which level of the coverage hierarchy is targeted. Do not claim 'fully tested' if only statement coverage is measured. Require at least branch coverage for safety-critical code and MC/DC for aviation/medical.
redteam_pattern: Measure coverage at each level of the hierarchy. If a claim of 'X% coverage' does not specify which criterion, assume the weakest (statement). Assert that safety-critical modules achieve at least branch coverage.
source_document: Beizer, Software Testing Techniques, 2nd ed., 1990, Ch. 3-5
tensor_equation: statement_coverage ⊂ branch_coverage ⊂ condition_coverage ⊃ condition/decision_coverage ⊂ MC/DC ⊂ path_coverage. (branch_coverage is NOT a subset of condition_coverage; they are incomparable. The chain is: statement ⊂ branch ⊂ MC/DC ⊂ path, and statement ⊂ condition ⊂ condition/decision.)
title: Coverage Criteria Subsumption Hierarchy
verdict_discrepancy: The claimed chain 'branch_coverage subset condition_coverage' is FALSE, and it is the exact reason the separate condition/decision criterion exists. Concrete counterexample: program `if (a && b) { X(); } else { Y(); }` with tests t1=(a=T,b=F), t2=(a=F,b=T). Both a and b take both values => 100% condition coverage. But the decision `a && b` is false on both tests => the true branch and X() are never executed => branch coverage 50%, statement coverage < 100%. So condition coverage does not imply branch or even statement coverage, and the axiom's transitive chain collapses.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: reversed polarity/contradiction

prompt_injection: When measuring path coverage, distinguish between feasible and total paths. Do not penalize a test suite for failing to cover infeasible paths. Require justification (data-flow or constraint-solving evidence) for any path claimed infeasible.
redteam_pattern: For each uncovered path, attempt constraint solving (e.g., symbolic execution) to determine feasibility. Paths proven infeasible are excluded from coverage denominator. Paths unresolved are flagged as coverage gaps.
source_document: Beizer, Software Testing Techniques, 2nd ed., 1990, Ch. 5
tensor_equation: ∃ program P with CFG G where ∃ paths π ∈ paths(G) such that ¬∃ input i where execution(P, i) traverses π. (Not every program has an infeasible path — counterexample: a simple function with two complete paths and no loops.)
title: Infeasible Path Problem — not all CFG paths are exercisable
verdict_discrepancy: Quantifier error: the axiom asserts for EVERY program P there exists an infeasible path. Counterexample: `func f(x int) int { if x > 0 { return 1 }; return 2 }` — the CFG has exactly two complete paths and both are exercised by x=1 and x=-1. No infeasible path exists, so the universally quantified existential is false. The correct statement is the existential form (there exist programs whose CFGs contain infeasible paths) plus the undecidability result; Beizer states it that way, not as a property of all programs.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: Do not equate 'code is covered' with 'code is tested.' A test exercises a line but asserts nothing about its output state provides no fault-detection value. Require assertions that verify the propagated state, not just execution traversal.
redteam_pattern: For each covered line, verify that at least one covering test contains an assertion whose truth value depends on the output of that line. Lines covered but never asserted are P2 advisory gaps.
source_document: Pezze and Young, Software Testing and Analysis, 2007, Ch. 2 (PIE model: Propagation, Infection, Execution)
tensor_equation: ∀ fault f in program P, a test t detects f iff: (1) t executes the faulty statement (Execution), (2) the fault causes an infected program state (Infection), AND (3) the infected state propagates to an observable output (Propagation). (PIE model: Voas 1992; RIP/RIPR: Ammann & Offutt.)
title: Observable Failure Requirement — fault must propagate to output
verdict_discrepancy: Attribution. The PIE model (Propagation, Infection, Execution) is Jeffrey Voas, 'PIE: A Dynamic Failure-Based Technique', IEEE TSE 18(8), 1992; the RIP/RIPR restatement is Ammann & Offutt, Introduction to Software Testing. Pezze & Young Ch.2 is 'A Framework for Test and Analysis' and does not present the PIE model under that name. Wrong source named for the model.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: Require unit tests written concurrently with or before implementation (TDD where feasible). Do not defer all testing to integration or system-test phases. Flag PRs that add code without corresponding test changes.
redteam_pattern: Measure test-to-code ratio per commit. Flag commits where lines_added > 0 and test_lines_added = 0. Track cumulative ratio over time.
source_document: ISTQB Foundation Level Syllabus, Principle 3; Boehm, Software Engineering Economics, 1981
tensor_equation: ∀ fault f introduced at phase p_intro and found at phase p_found: cost_fix(f, p_found) > cost_fix(f, p_intro). Cost grows with later detection, but exact growth rates are Boehm's 1970s waterfall data, not a universal superlinear invariant.
title: Early Testing — cost of fault fixes grows superlinearly with phase
verdict_discrepancy: Empirical finding hardened into a universal invariant, with a growth rate neither source states. Boehm's data is an aggregate over 1970s waterfall projects and is expressed as ratios between phases, not as a proven superlinear function of phase distance; ISTQB gives no quantification at all. The superlinearity claim is actively contested for modern iterative processes (see Leitner's re-examination of the cost-of-change curve). Counterexample: a spec-phase typo in a constant caught in production is fixed by a one-line change plus redeploy — cost_fix at p_found is essentially equal to cost at p_intro, violating the strict inequality, let alone superlinearity.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: corrected over-hardening

prompt_injection: Never present a clean test run as evidence that the system is correct. State: 'No bugs found under the tested conditions' rather than 'The system is correct.' Include acceptance tests that validate user scenarios, not just unit-level correctness.
redteam_pattern: Review test reports for language claiming system correctness. Flag 'bug-free,' 'fully tested,' 'no defects' as misleading. Require scoped language: 'no failures under test suite T with coverage C.'
source_document: ISTQB Foundation Level Syllabus, Principle 1; Dijkstra, Notes on Structured Programming, 1970
tensor_equation: ¬( |found_bugs| = 0 ⇒ system_meets_requirements ). A system can be bug-free with respect to its spec and still be useless if the spec is wrong, or if untested user needs are unmet. (ISTQB Principle 1: testing shows presence of defects, not their absence. The absence-of-errors fallacy is Principle 7.)
title: Absence-of-Errors Fallacy — zero bugs does not imply fitness for purpose
verdict_discrepancy: Wrong principle number, and two distinct principles conflated. ISTQB Principle 1 is 'Testing shows the presence of defects, not their absence'; the absence-of-errors fallacy is Principle 7. The axiom cites Principle 1 while stating Principle 7's content and then tacks Principle 1's content on as a final sentence, so the citation points a verifier at the wrong syllabus section.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: When designing mutation tests, restrict to first-order mutants (single syntactic change). Do not generate higher-order mutants unless first-order coverage is saturated. The competent programmer hypothesis justifies this: simple mutants are sufficient to model real fault distributions.
redteam_pattern: Generate first-order mutants. Measure mutation score = killed / (total − equivalent). Flag if mutation score < 0.80 for safety-critical modules.
source_document: DeMillo, Lipton, Sayward, Hints on Test Data Selection: Help for the Practicing Programmer, IEEE Computer, 1978
tensor_equation: ∀ submitted program P, P is syntactically 'close' to a correct program P* such that the set of simple mutations Δ = {δ_1,...,δ_n} where δ_i is a single-token change approximates the set of real programmer errors with high probability
title: Competent Programmer Hypothesis — mutants approximate real faults
verdict_evidence: DeMillo, Lipton & Sayward, 'Hints on Test Data Selection: Help for the Practicing Programmer', IEEE Computer 11(4), 1978, states the competent programmer hypothesis: programmers write programs that are close to being correct, so the program is 'almost correct' and the faults to hunt are small deviations — which is what justifies restricting mutation to simple single-token operators. The axiom preserves the source's hedge ('with high probability', 'hypothesis' in the title) rather than hardening it. Counterexample showing teeth: a submission implementing bubble sort where a merge was required is not within any bounded set of single-token mutations of a correct program, so the hypothesis is empirically checkable and known to fail on novice/large-scale-misdesign code — the standard published criticism of mutation testing.
