verdict_discrepancy: The auxiliary-variable clause is inverted. In Owicki-Gries a set AV is a set of auxiliary variables of S precisely because its members occur in S only in assignments to members of AV — i.e. they DO appear in the program text S₁/S₂. The rule then permits deleting those assignments, subject to the side condition that no variable of AV occurs free in the pre/postconditions P and Q of the conclusion. The axiom states the opposite side condition ('must not appear in S₁ or S₂'), which would make auxiliary variables unusable — the whole technique depends on instrumenting the program with them. Constraint is placed on the wrong side of the rule.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: reversed polarity/contradiction

prompt_injection: Design by Contract divides responsibility between caller and callee with surgical precision. The precondition is the caller's obligation and the callee's benefit (the callee can assume it). The postcondition is the callee's obligation and the caller's benefit (the caller can rely on it). If the precondition fails, the callee has no obligation — the bug is in the caller. If the precondition holds but the postcondition fails, the bug is in the callee. This eliminates ambiguity about 'whose fault is this bug?' — the contract assigns fault precisely. Every public API must have explicit preconditions and postconditions. Without them, every caller is guessing.
source_document: Meyer, Bertrand. "Object-Oriented Software Construction." 2nd Edition, Prentice-Hall, 1997. Chapters 11-12: Design by Contract.
tensor_equation:
∀routine r with precondition PRE and postcondition POST:
  The caller must guarantee PRE holds before calling r
  If PRE holds before r executes, then r must guarantee POST holds after r executes
  Obligation-Benefit: PRE is an obligation for the caller, benefit for the callee
                      POST is an obligation for the callee, benefit for the caller
title: Meyer — Design by Contract: Precondition Obligation
verdict_evidence: Meyer, Object-Oriented Software Construction 2nd ed. (1997), Ch.11 'Design by Contract', states the routine contract exactly as an obligation/benefit table: the client must ensure the precondition (obligation to client, benefit to supplier); the supplier must ensure the postcondition (obligation to supplier, benefit to client). Meyer's 'non-redundancy principle' further holds that if the precondition is violated the routine is not obliged to do anything — matching 'if PRE holds ... then r must guarantee POST'. The conditional retains its antecedent, so it is falsifiable: a routine sqrt with PRE x≥0 and POST result²≈x that returns NaN for x=4 violates it concretely; a routine returning NaN for x=-1 does not.

prompt_injection: A class invariant is a consistency predicate on the object state that must hold: (1) after construction, (2) before and after every public method call (from the caller's perspective). The invariant CAN be temporarily violated inside a method body — this is essential, since mutations often go through intermediate inconsistent states — but it must be restored before any other method can observe the object. The invariant is an implicit conjunct of every precondition and postcondition. This is the fundamental rule of encapsulation: an object presents a consistent face to the world at all observable boundaries. Private methods may violate the invariant since they can only be called by the owning class.
source_document: Meyer, Bertrand. "Object-Oriented Software Construction." 2nd Edition, Prentice-Hall, 1997. Chapters 11.6-11.8: Class Invariants.
tensor_equation:
∀class C with invariant INV:
  INV holds after constructor completes
  ∀public method m in C: {INV ∧ PRE_m} m {INV ∧ POST_m}
  INV may be temporarily violated during method execution, but must be restored before method returns
  INV is implicitly conjoined to every pre- and postcondition
title: Meyer — Class Invariant: Consistent Object State
verdict_evidence: Meyer OOSC2 Ch.11.6-11.8 'Class invariants' states the invariant rule: an assertion I is a correct class invariant iff it is satisfied after every creation procedure completes, and preserved by every exported routine (i.e. it is implicitly added to the precondition and postcondition of every exported routine). Meyer explicitly notes the invariant need only hold in stable states — it may be broken during a routine's execution, including during calls to non-exported routines. All four lines of the axiom match. Has teeth: a Stack class with INV ≡ (0 ≤ count ≤ capacity) and a push that increments count without checking capacity violates it at count = capacity.

prompt_injection: When a subclass overrides a method, it must obey the contract of the parent. The precondition can only be weakened (disjunctive — accept more inputs): PRE_B must be implied by PRE_A, meaning B's precondition is less restrictive. The postcondition can only be strengthened (conjunctive — guarantee more): POST_A must imply POST_B, meaning B's postcondition is at least as strong. The class invariant can only be strengthened: INV_A must imply INV_B, meaning B maintains all of A's invariant and possibly additional constraints. This is the behavioral subtyping rule in object-oriented contexts: a subclass must be substitutable for its parent, and substitutability requires that the subclass require no more and guarantee no less.
source_document: Meyer, Bertrand. "Object-Oriented Software Construction." 2nd Edition, Prentice-Hall, 1997. Chapters 15-16: Inheritance and Design by Contract.
tensor_equation:
∀class B inheriting from A, ∀method m overridden in B:
  PRE_B(m) ⇒ PRE_A(m)        (precondition can only be weakened — require no more)
  POST_A(m) ⇒ POST_B(m)      (postcondition can only be strengthened — guarantee at least as much)
  INV_A ⇒ INV_B               (invariant can only be strengthened)
  i.e., PRE_B ≤ PRE_A and POST_B ≥ POST_A and INV_A ≤ INV_B
title: Meyer — Inheritance and Contract Subcontracting
verdict_discrepancy: ALL THREE IMPLICATIONS ARE REVERSED. Weakening the precondition means PRE_A ⇒ PRE_B; the axiom writes PRE_B ⇒ PRE_A, which is strengthening. Strengthening the postcondition means POST_B ⇒ POST_A; the axiom writes POST_A ⇒ POST_B. Strengthening the invariant means INV_B ⇒ INV_A; the axiom writes INV_A ⇒ INV_B. Counterexample against the axiom as written: A.withdraw requires amount>0, B.withdraw requires amount>0 ∧ amount<100. The axiom's rule PRE_B ⇒ PRE_A is satisfied, yet B is an illegal redeclaration under Meyer and breaks every client that calls withdraw(500) polymorphically. The axiom licenses exactly the substitution failure the rule exists to forbid.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: The Liskov Substitution Principle (LSP) goes beyond syntactic type compatibility to behavioral compatibility. S is a behavioral subtype of T iff objects of type S can be substituted for objects of type T without altering any desirable property of the program. This requires: (1) S's methods require input no MORE restrictive than T's (contravariance of preconditions), (2) S's methods guarantee output no LESS restrictive than T's (covariance of postconditions), (3) S's invariant implies T's invariant. The 'no new exceptions' rule: a subtype method cannot throw exceptions that the supertype method does not declare. Violating LSP produces programs that type-check but fail at runtime with behavioral bugs that the type system cannot catch. Every instanceof/downcast in code is a potential LSP violation.
source_document: Liskov, Barbara H., and Jeannette M. Wing. "A Behavioral Notion of Subtyping." ACM Transactions on Programming Languages and Systems, Vol. 16, No. 6, November 1994, pp. 1811-1841.
tensor_equation: ∀type T, subtype S: S is a behavioral subtype of T iff: ∀method m of T: pre_m[S](x) ⇐ pre_m[T](x) (S requires no more than T) ∧ post_m[T](x) ⇒ post_m[S](x) (S ensures at least as much as T).
title: Liskov Substitution Principle — Behavioral Subtyping (∀ Quantified)
verdict_discrepancy: The precondition line is inverted relative to its own gloss. Read as sets of states (the reading the ⊆ forces, and the one the post/inv lines use consistently), 'S requires no more than T' means the subtype's precondition admits at least as many states: pre_m[S] ⊇ pre_m[T]. The axiom writes pre_m[S] ⊆ pre_m[T], i.e. S requires MORE — the exact opposite of the parenthetical and of Liskov-Wing's pre_τ ⇒ pre_σ. Counterexample the broken form accepts: T.pop requires ¬empty, S.pop requires ¬empty ∧ size>1; pre_S ⊆ pre_T holds, so the axiom certifies S as a behavioral subtype, yet a client holding a one-element S and calling pop breaks. The post_m and inv lines are correct, which makes the pre line an internal inconsistency, not a uniform notational convention.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: reversed polarity/contradiction

prompt_injection: The contravariance-covariance rule is counterintuitive but mathematically necessary for safe substitution. Preconditions are contravariant: the subtype can accept a WIDER range of inputs (its precondition is WEAKER). Postconditions are covariant: the subtype must guarantee a NARROWER range of outputs (its postcondition is STRONGER). The history constraint adds temporal logic: the allowed sequences of state changes in S must be a subset of those allowed in T. A subtype cannot introduce new state transitions that would surprise a client expecting T behavior. This is why overriding a method to do something fundamentally different — even if types match — is an LSP violation.
source_document: Liskov, Barbara H., and Jeannette M. Wing. "A Behavioral Notion of Subtyping." ACM Transactions on Programming Languages and Systems, Vol. 16, No. 6, November 1994, pp. 1811-1841.
tensor_equation:
∀type T, subtype S (S <: T), ∀method m:
  m_S.pre ≤ m_T.pre                  (contravariance: S accepts ALL inputs T accepts, possibly more)
  m_S.post ≥ m_T.post               (covariance: S guarantees ALL outcomes T guarantees, possibly more)
  m_S.history ⊑ m_T.history          (history constraint: S's allowed state sequences ⊆ T's allowed sequences)
  constraint: S's state space may grow but must preserve T's constraints
title: Liskov & Wing — Contravariance of Preconditions and Covariance of Postconditions
verdict_evidence: Liskov & Wing 1994 §3.2 state the pre-condition rule (contravariant: supertype precondition implies subtype precondition, so the subtype accepts everything the supertype accepts), the post-condition rule (covariant: subtype postcondition implies supertype postcondition), and the history rule (every sequence of states of a subtype object, restricted to the supertype's observable state via the abstraction function, must be legal for the supertype) — so 'S's allowed state sequences ⊆ T's' is the history constraint as stated. The glosses in this entry get all three directions right, including the one AX-017 inverts. Has teeth: L&W's own example — a mutable Bag subtype adding a state-mutating method violates the history rule of an immutable supertype even though pre/post rules pass.

prompt_injection: The subtype's class invariant must be at least as strong as the supertype's: INV[S] must logically imply INV[T]. If T guarantees that balance ≥ 0, S may additionally guarantee that balance ≤ 1000000 (stronger), but S may not relax the guarantee to balance ≥ -100 (weaker). The subtype's state space, when projected onto the supertype's fields, must satisfy all supertype constraints. This is the invariant-preservation rule: a subtype cannot break any property that clients of the supertype rely on. The 'history constraint' further requires that subtype objects cannot enter states that supertype objects would never enter, even if those states satisfy the invariant — temporal behavior must be compatible too.
source_document: Liskov, Barbara H., and Jeannette M. Wing. "A Behavioral Notion of Subtyping." ACM Transactions on Programming Languages and Systems, Vol. 16, No. 6, November 1994, pp. 1811-1841.
tensor_equation:
∀type T, subtype S (S <: T):
  INV[S] ⇒ INV[T]                  (subtype invariant implies supertype invariant)
  ∀constraint C on T's state:
    states_S satisfying C ⊆ states_T satisfying C  (subtype state space preserves supertype constraints)
  Exported methods: methods_S ⊇ methods_T         (subtype has at least the same public interface)
title: Liskov & Wing — Subtype Invariant Implication Rule
verdict_evidence: Liskov & Wing 1994 §3.2 invariant rule: inv_σ ⇒ inv_τ (subtype invariant implies supertype invariant) — the direction stated here is correct. The signature requirement (the subtype must export at least the supertype's methods, with contra/covariant signatures) is L&W's 'Subtype methods' clause, matching methods_S ⊇ methods_T. Has teeth: a SortedList subtype of List with INV_S ≡ sorted ∧ INV_T satisfies the rule, whereas a subtype whose invariant permits a negative count while the supertype's requires count≥0 fails it, and the failure is exactly where a supertype client's reasoning breaks.

prompt_injection: Lamport distinguishes safety from liveness. A safety property (= 'something bad never happens') is an invariant that must hold in every reachable state. To prove □P: (1) show P holds in all initial states, (2) for every atomic transition τ, show that if P holds before τ, then P holds after τ. The key insight: proving a safety property requires examining each state transition individually — it's an inductive proof over the state space. Safety violations are finitely observable: if □P is violated, there exists a finite execution prefix at the end of which P is false. This is the foundation for all runtime invariant checking (assertions, monitors, property-based testing).
source_document: Lamport, Leslie. "Proving the Correctness of Multiprocess Programs." IEEE Transactions on Software Engineering, Vol. SE-3, No. 2, March 1977, pp. 125-143.
tensor_equation:
∀program, ∀predicate P (safety property):
  P is a safety property iff: any violation of P occurs at a specific finite time
  □P ≡ P holds in every reachable state  (always/invariant)
  To prove □P: show P holds initially ∧ ∀transition τ: P ∧ enabled(τ) ⇒ P after τ
  Safety properties are limit-closed: if finite prefixes satisfy, limit satisfies
title: Lamport — Safety Property: □P (Always/Invariance)
verdict_discrepancy: Two misattributions. (1) 'Safety properties are limit-closed: if finite prefixes satisfy, limit satisfies' and the characterization 'any violation of P occurs at a specific finite time' are the formal topological definitions from Alpern & Schneider, 'Defining Liveness', Information Processing Letters 21(4), 1985 — where safety = limit-closed sets and liveness = dense sets. Lamport 1977's definitions are explicitly informal (his own 2025 TSE retrospective calls them 'informal definitions of safety and liveness'). (2) The □ modality is anachronistic: Lamport 1977 uses assertions attached to control points, not temporal-logic operators; Lamport adopted temporal logic only after Pnueli 1977, from about 1980.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: Lamport generalizes Owicki-Gries to multiprocess programs where processes communicate through shared memory. The proof method: each process has its own control points with assertions (like Floyd's flowchart method but concurrent). Local correctness = each process's sequential assertions are valid. Global correctness = no assignment in any other process interferes with any assertion. A contention-free invariant is one that references only variables not written by other processes — it is trivially interference-free. This is the origin of the rule: if you can prove an invariant using only thread-local data, no interference check is needed. Shared-nothing is the strongest form of interference freedom.
source_document: Lamport, Leslie. "Proving the Correctness of Multiprocess Programs." IEEE Transactions on Software Engineering, Vol. SE-3, No. 2, March 1977, pp. 125-143.
tensor_equation: ∀multiprocess program with k processes P₁, ..., Pₖ: Each process Pᵢ has control points with assertions. Local correctness: each Pᵢ's assertions are valid sequentially. Global correctness: ∀assertion A at control point of Pᵢ, A is invariant under other processes' actions. ("Contention-free invariant" is not Lamport's vocabulary.)
title: Lamport — Atomicity and Owicki-Gries Generalization for Multiprocess Programs
verdict_discrepancy: Two fidelity problems. (1) The final line invents a term: 'contention-free invariant' is not Lamport's vocabulary and appears in neither the 1977 paper nor its successors; presented as a definition from the cited source, it is unsupported. (2) The title's framing ('Atomicity and Owicki-Gries Generalization') inverts the paper's thesis — Lamport's stated advance over Owicki-Gries is that his method does NOT require assuming that statements execute atomically; he reduces the atomicity assumption to individual reads and writes. The axiom presents atomicity as something the method establishes rather than something it dispenses with.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: Lamport's stuttering invariance principle: the correctness of a concurrent program should not depend on how many steps the implementation takes, only on what observable changes occur. A stuttering step is one where no externally visible variable changes value. A specification should not distinguish between a single step and the same effect achieved through multiple stuttering steps. This is the foundation for refinement: a concrete implementation may use more steps (more granularity) than the abstract specification, as long as the sequence of observable state changes is the same modulo stuttering. This separates 'what the program does' from 'how many CPU instructions it takes.'
source_document: Lamport, Leslie. "Proving the Correctness of Multiprocess Programs." IEEE Transactions on Software Engineering, Vol. SE-3, No. 2, March 1977, pp. 125-143. Extended in: Lamport, Leslie. "The Temporal Logic of Actions." ACM TOPLAS, Vol. 16, No. 3, May 1994, pp. 872-923.
tensor_equation: ∀predicate P, ∀stuttering steps (steps where no observable variable changes): P is invariant under stuttering iff for any stuttering step σ, P(σ(s)) ≡ P(s). Safety properties must be stuttering-invariant. (Lamport 1977 has no notion of stuttering; stuttering invariance is a later TLA+ concept.)
title: Lamport — Temporal Logic of Actions: Stuttering Invariance
verdict_discrepancy: Misattribution plus a false claim. (1) The primary citation is Lamport 1977, which contains no notion of stuttering — the 1977 method has no temporal operators at all; the '1994 extension' is in fact the source. (2) 'Safety properties must be stuttering-insensitive' is false as stated. Counterexample: over traces of a variable x, the property 'no two consecutive states are equal' is a safety property (every violation occurs at a finite point and is irremediable) and is maximally stuttering-SENSITIVE — a single stuttering step violates it. Stuttering invariance is a design constraint TLA imposes on admissible specifications, not a theorem about safety properties. (3) The final line, '¬∃P: P is safety ∧ P distinguishes states that differ only by hidden variables', conflates stuttering (repeated states) with hiding/existential quantification over internal variables; these are different mechanisms, and safety properties over the full state absolutely can distinguish states differing in hidden variables (that is precisely why ∃-hiding is needed).
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: The 'unless' property is the fundamental safety operator in UNITY logic: p unless q means that if p is true and q is false at any point, then after any single statement execution, either p remains true OR q becomes true. In other words: p cannot become false UNLESS q becomes true. This is more general than invariance (□p) — it allows p to be falsified, but only when q is established. 'unless' is compositional: if p unless q holds for program F and for program G, it holds for F ∥ G. This is the key to compositional verification of concurrent programs: prove local unless properties, then compose.
source_document: Chandy, K. Mani, and Jayadev Misra. "Parallel Program Design: A Foundation." Addison-Wesley, 1988. Chapters 3-4: UNITY Logic.
tensor_equation: ∀UNITY program, ∀predicates p, q: p unless q ≡ ∀statement s in program: {p ∧ ¬q} s {p ∨ q}. p unless false = stable p, not invariant p. Invariant p ≡ (initial condition ⇒ p) ∧ stable p. p unless q is the fundamental UNITY safety property.
title: Chandy & Misra — UNITY unless (Safety/Stability) Property
verdict_discrepancy: 'p unless false ≡ □p (p holds forever — invariant)' conflates stability with invariance. In UNITY, p unless false = stable p, and invariant p ≡ (initial condition ⇒ p) ∧ stable p — the initial-condition conjunct is dropped here. Counterexample: program with initial condition x=0 and the single statement x := x (or x := max(x,5)); take p ≡ (x=5). Every statement satisfies {p ∧ ¬false} s {p ∨ false}, so 'p unless false' holds, yet p is false in the initial state and □p fails. The stated equivalence is unsound in the ⇒ direction.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error; added missing cases/states

prompt_injection: The 'ensures' property is the atomic unit of progress in UNITY: p ensures q means (1) p is stable until q becomes true (unless), AND (2) there exists SOME statement in the program that, when executed in a state where p holds and q does not, establishes q. This is a single-step progress guarantee. 'leads-to' (⟼) is the transitive closure: p ⟼ q if there is a chain of ensures steps from p to q. Leads-to means 'p eventually causes q' — it is the fundamental liveness operator. UNITY programs are designed around this logic: you define the safety (unless) and progress (leads-to) properties first, then derive the program statements that satisfy them.
source_document: Chandy, K. Mani, and Jayadev Misra. "Parallel Program Design: A Foundation." Addison-Wesley, 1988. Chapters 5-6: Progress and Leads-to.
tensor_equation:
∀UNITY program, ∀predicates p, q:
  p ensures q ≡ (p unless q) ∧ (∃statement s: {p ∧ ¬q} s {q})
  i.e., p ensures q holds iff: (1) once p holds, it persists until q holds, AND (2) some statement establishes q when p holds
  p ⟼ q (leads-to) is the transitive, disjunctive closure of ensures
  p ⟼ q means: if p ever holds, q will eventually hold
  leads-to is the fundamental UNITY liveness property
title: Chandy & Misra — UNITY ensures (Progress/Liveness) Property
verdict_evidence: Chandy & Misra 1988 define: p ensures q ≡ (p unless q) ∧ (∃ statement s in the program: {p ∧ ¬q} s {q}). Under the UNITY unconditional-fairness rule (every statement is executed infinitely often) this yields progress. leads-to (⟼) is defined as the strongest relation satisfying (i) p ensures q ⇒ p ⟼ q, (ii) transitivity, and (iii) disjunction over arbitrary sets — i.e. the transitive, disjunctive closure of ensures, exactly as the axiom states. Both conjuncts of ensures are retained, so it has teeth: dropping the unless conjunct would let a statement establish q from p while another statement destroys p before that statement is scheduled, and the progress conclusion would fail.

prompt_injection: UNITY's key compositional property: unless and leads-to are preserved under parallel composition. If you prove p ⟼ q for program F in isolation, it also holds when F runs in parallel with G (as long as G doesn't interfere). This is because UNITY's 'always' fairness assumption ensures every statement is executed infinitely often, and leads-to is defined through the transitive closure of ensures across the entire program. The substitution axiom allows replacing equal terms: if x = e is an invariant, x can be substituted by e in any property. This is the algebraic foundation for program transformation and refinement — substitute equals for equals while preserving all properties.
source_document: Chandy, K. Mani, and Jayadev Misra. "Parallel Program Design: A Foundation." Addison-Wesley, 1988. Chapters 7-8: Composition and Substitution.
tensor_equation: ∀UNITY programs F, G, ∀predicates p, q: If p unless q in F, then p unless q in F ∥ G (unless is union-closed — compositional). If p ensures q in F, then p ensures q in F ∥ G (ensures is NOT union-closed — NOT compositional).
title: Chandy & Misra — UNITY Substitution Axiom and Compositionality
verdict_discrepancy: Two errors. (1) Self-contradiction: the ensures line asserts the implication 'If p ensures q in F, then p ensures q in F ∥ G' and then parenthetically denies it ('ensures is NOT union-closed'). The implication as written is false — G may contain a statement violating the unless component of ensures. (2) 'If p ⟼ q in F, then p ⟼ q in F ∥ G (leads-to is union-closed — compositional)' is FALSE and is the single most consequential error here. leads-to is neither conjunctive nor inherited under union: p ⟼ q may hold in F and fail in F ∥ G. Counterexample: F = {x := 1} with initial x=0 gives (x=0) ⟼ (x=1); G = {x := 0} added in union means the fair interleaving can reset x forever and no leads-to to a stable x=1 survives. Chandy-Misra's union theorem for progress therefore requires extra hypotheses; it is not the free conjunction the last line claims ('properties of union = conjunction of properties of components' holds for unless/stable, not for progress).
verdict_evidence: Fixed: reversed polarity/contradiction

prompt_injection: In UNITY, a fixed point predicate FP is one that, once true, no statement can make false. Fixed point detection is the basis of termination detection in distributed systems. The program has terminated when: (1) the fixed point predicate holds (no more work to do), AND (2) no messages are in transit (every process agrees the computation is done). This is the Chandy-Misra termination detection algorithm: detect when a distributed computation has globally reached a state where no process has any work and no messages are in flight. The fixed point is the 'done' predicate — it must be stable (once true, stays true) and must eventually become true (liveness).
source_document: Chandy, K. Mani, and Jayadev Misra. "Parallel Program Design: A Foundation." Addison-Wesley, 1988. Chapters 10-11: Termination Detection.
tensor_equation: ∀UNITY program with state predicate FP: FP is a fixed point iff ∀statement s in program: {FP} s {FP}. i.e., FP is a fixed point iff no statement in the program can falsify it. fixed point ≡ transient false.
title: Chandy & Misra — UNITY Fixed Point and Termination Detection
verdict_discrepancy: The definition given is circular and therefore untestable as written: 'FP ≡ (∀statement s: enabled(s) ⇒ Hoare triple establishes FP)' defines FP in terms of FP, with 'Hoare triple establishes' left unspecified — any predicate satisfying an unspecified triple qualifies. The gloss 'no statement can falsify it' describes stability, not the fixed point (every invariant is stable but is not the fixed point). Additionally, 'The program terminates when the fixed point is reached' misstates UNITY's semantics: UNITY programs never terminate — execution continues forever, and reaching FP means further statement executions no longer change the state; Chandy-Misra model termination as reaching FP, not as halting, and 'all processes agree' is not part of the definition.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: Total correctness = partial correctness + termination. Partial correctness (= {P} C {Q}) says: if C starts in state satisfying P and terminates, the final state satisfies Q. It says nothing about termination. Total correctness (= [P] C {Q}) additionally requires that C must terminate from any state satisfying P. The wp operator gives total correctness: wp(C, Q) is the weakest precondition for guaranteed termination in Q. The wlp operator (weakest liberal precondition) gives partial correctness: wlp(C, Q) is the weakest precondition for Q-after-C-or-nontermination. The decomposition: wp(C, Q) = wlp(C, Q) ∧ wp(C, true), where wp(C, true) is the termination condition. Every program you ship must be totally correct — partial correctness is a proof convenience, not a deployment target.
source_document: Dijkstra, Edsger W. "A Discipline of Programming." Prentice-Hall, 1976. Chapter 3. Hoare, C.A.R. "An Axiomatic Basis for Computer Programming." CACM, 1969. Combined synthesis.
tensor_equation:
∀program C, precondition P, postcondition Q:
  Total Correctness: [P] C [Q] ≡ {P} C {Q} ∧ (P ⇒ C terminates)
  Partial Correctness: {P} C {Q} ≡ (P before C ∧ C terminates ⇒ Q after C)
  Total = Partial ∧ Termination
  wp(C, Q) is the weakest P such that [P] C [Q] (total correctness)
  wlp(C, Q) is the weakest P such that {P} C {Q} (partial correctness)
  wp(C, Q) ≡ wlp(C, Q) ∧ wp(C, true)  (the termination condition)
title: Partial Correctness + Termination = Total Correctness (Dijkstra Synthesis)
verdict_evidence: The decomposition is standard and correct: Hoare 1969 explicitly states his triples are partial correctness ('provided the program terminates'); Dijkstra's wp is total-correctness by definition (Ch.3: 'guaranteed to lead to a properly terminating activity'), so wp(S,true) characterizes exactly the termination domain, and wp(S,R) ≡ wlp(S,R) ∧ wp(S,true) is the standard identity. The citation is honestly labeled 'Combined synthesis'. Has teeth: C ≡ 'while x≠0 do x:=x-2', P ≡ (x=1), Q ≡ (x=0) — {P}C{Q} holds vacuously (partial correctness) while [P]C[Q] fails, so the two notions are demonstrably distinguished.

prompt_injection: Every formal method surveyed converges on a single universal pattern: the state invariant. Init ⇒ I, and every transition preserves I. This is the induction principle applied to program state. Hoare's loop invariant P is a state invariant for the loop body. Floyd's assertions at cut points are state invariants for the paths between them. Meyer's class invariants are state invariants for object state across method boundaries. Lamport's □P is a state invariant in temporal logic. Chandy-Misra's 'p unless false' is exactly an invariant. The difference between methods is not the concept — it's the same — but the granularity: loop-level (Hoare/Floyd), object-level (Meyer), thread-level (Owicki-Gries/Lamport), system-level (Chandy-Misra). Any correctness argument that is not ultimately reducible to a state invariant induction is incomplete.
source_document: Synthesis across: Hoare (1969), Floyd (1967), Dijkstra (1976), Owicki-Gries (1976), Lamport (1977), Meyer (1997), Chandy-Misra (1988).
tensor_equation:
∀system with state space Σ, initial states Init ⊆ Σ, transitions T:
  Predicate I is a state invariant iff:
    Init ⇒ I                     (satisfied initially)
    ∀σ ∈ Σ, ∀τ ∈ T: I(σ) ∧ σ→_τ σ' ⇒ I(σ')   (preserved by all transitions)
  This schema subsumes: Hoare's loop invariants, Floyd's assertions, Meyer's class invariants,
    Lamport's □P, Chandy-Misra's unless p false, Owicki-Gries proof outlines
title: State Invariant — The Universal Pattern Across All Formal Methods
verdict_evidence: The two conditions given — Init ⇒ I, and ∀τ: I(σ) ∧ σ→_τ σ′ ⇒ I(σ′) — are the standard inductive-invariance principle, and each named source does instantiate it: Floyd's cut-point assertions, Hoare's while rule premise {P∧B}C{P}, Dijkstra's P∧Bᵢ ⇒ wp(Sᵢ,P), Meyer's creation-plus-preservation invariant rule, Lamport's initial-plus-every-action invariance method, Chandy-Misra's stable p. Not vacuous: I is not an unbound schematic predicate — it is constrained relative to concrete Init and T, and both premises are retained, so it is directly falsifiable. Counterexample showing teeth: Σ = ℤ, Init = {1}, T = {x := x-1}, I ≡ (x ≥ 0). Init ⇒ I holds but preservation fails at x=0, so I is rejected as an inductive invariant — as it must be, since x=-1 is reachable.

prompt_injection: A concrete implementation C refines an abstract specification A if, for any postcondition, the precondition required by C is no stronger than the precondition required by A. In other words, C works for ALL inputs that A promises to work for. Refinement is the formal foundation for: (1) Stepwise refinement (Wirth) — replace an abstract operation with a more concrete sequence, (2) Data refinement (Hoare) — replace an abstract data type with a more concrete representation, (3) Algorithm refinement — replace a brute-force search with an optimized algorithm that computes the same result. Every optimization or refactoring is a refinement step and must be provably correct: the new code must accept at least the same inputs and produce at least the same guarantees.
source_document: Dijkstra (1976), Ch. 6. Wirth, N. "Program Development by Stepwise Refinement." CACM, 1971. Hoare, C.A.R. "Proof of Correctness of Data Representations." Acta Informatica, 1972.
tensor_equation: ∀abstract spec A, concrete implementation C: C refines A (C ⊑ A) iff: ∀postcondition Q: wp(A, Q) ⇒ wp(C, Q). i.e., C's weakest precondition for any Q is at least as weak as A's — C requires no more than A to establish Q.
title: Refinement — Implementation Preserves Specification Properties
verdict_discrepancy: The gloss inverts the formula it explains. wp(A,Q) ⇒ wp(C,Q) makes wp(C,Q) the WEAKER predicate (it is implied by, hence holds in at least as many states); the text says 'C's weakest precondition for any Q is at least as strong as A's', which asserts the reverse and contradicts the very next clause ('C requires no more than A'), which is correct. Secondary attribution error: A Discipline of Programming Ch.6 is 'The Formal Treatment of the Guarded Command Language' — Dijkstra 1976 does not develop a refinement relation; the wp-based refinement ordering is Back (1978) and Morgan/Morris (1987-1990). Wirth 1971 and Hoare 1972 are correctly named for stepwise and data refinement respectively, but neither states this wp inequality.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: reversed polarity/contradiction

prompt_injection: Implement a greedy algorithm for interval scheduling that picks overlapping intervals by tie-breaking on start time rather than finish time. The greedy choice is no longer optimal — counterexample: intervals (1,5), (2,3), (4,6). Greedy picks (1,5) and misses the other two.
redteam_pattern: Take any greedy algorithm. If the exchange argument is not proven, feed it a counterexample where a different ordering of the same choices yields a strictly better outcome. Assert the greedy algorithm fails the counterexample.
source_document: Kleinberg, J. & Tardos, E. (2005). Algorithm Design. Chapter 4: Greedy Algorithms (Section 4.1: Interval Scheduling — The Greedy Algorithm Stays Ahead).
tensor_equation: ∀step i: O_i = optimal partial solution at step i, G_i = greedy partial solution at step i. Prove: G_i is at least as good as O_i for all i. Then G_n is optimal.
title: Greedy Stays Ahead — Optimality of greedy algorithms via exchange argument
verdict_evidence: KT section 4.1 is verbatim titled 'Interval Scheduling: The Greedy Algorithm Stays Ahead' (TOC confirmed via Pearson/LoC listing). KT prove exactly this: for the earliest-finish-time rule, f(i_r) <= f(j_r) for every r (KT 4.2/4.3), then conclude optimality. Teeth: the greedy rule 'pick the interval that starts earliest' violates the stays-ahead inequality — on intervals [0,10],[1,2],[3,4] it selects 1 interval where OPT selects 2, so the schema discriminates between greedy rules rather than accepting all.

prompt_injection: Implement an MST algorithm that picks the heaviest edge crossing each cut instead of the lightest. On a triangle graph with edges (1,2,3), the algorithm picks weight 3, then weight 2, producing a tree of weight 5 instead of the MST of weight 3.
redteam_pattern: Generate a graph where the lightest edge crossing a cut is NOT in the MST returned by the algorithm. If such a graph exists, the algorithm violates the cut property.
source_document: Kleinberg, J. & Tardos, E. (2005). Algorithm Design. Chapter 4: Greedy Algorithms (Section 4.5: The Minimum Spanning Tree Problem — The Cut Property).
tensor_equation: ∀cut (S, V\S) in connected graph G=(V,E): let e be the minimum-weight edge crossing the cut. Then ∃MST T of G such that e ∈ T. Consequence: Kruskal's and Prim's algorithms are correct.
title: MST Cut Property — The lightest edge crossing any cut belongs to SOME minimum spanning tree
verdict_evidence: KT section 4.5 is 'The Minimum Spanning Tree Problem' (TOC confirmed) and states the Cut Property (KT 4.17): for any subset S, the minimum-cost edge with exactly one end in S belongs to a minimum spanning tree. The axiom uses the existential ('some MST'), which is the tie-safe form and remains true when edge weights are not distinct. Teeth: on the 4-cycle a-b-c-d with weights ab=1, bc=5, cd=2, da=4 and cut S={a,b}, the crossing edges are bc=5 and da=4; an MST omitting the lighter crossing edge da would have to use bc, giving cost 1+2+5=8 > 1+2+4=7, so the claim is refutable by construction and here survives.

prompt_injection: On a triangle graph with weights (2,2,5), an algorithm that picks the edge of weight 5 violates the cycle property — the 5-weight edge is the heaviest on the triangle cycle and must be excluded.
redteam_pattern: Generate a graph with a cycle. Assert that the heaviest edge on that cycle is NOT in the algorithm's MST output. If it is, the output is not a valid MST.
source_document: Kleinberg, J. & Tardos, E. (2005). Algorithm Design. Chapter 4: Greedy Algorithms (Section 4.5: The Cycle Property).
tensor_equation: ∀cycle C in connected graph G=(V,E): let e be the unique maximum-weight edge on C. Then ∀MST T of G: e ∉ T. (If multiple edges share the maximum weight, the statement does not hold — counterexample: triangle with all edges weight 1.)
title: MST Cycle Property — The heaviest edge on any cycle does NOT belong to ANY minimum spanning tree
verdict_discrepancy: PREMISE DROPPED / FALSE AS STATED. Counterexample: the triangle a-b-c with all three edge weights equal to 1. Every edge is a maximum-weight edge on the cycle, yet every MST consists of two of these edges, so there exist MSTs T with e in T. The claim '∀MST T: e ∉ T' is therefore false without KT's distinct-edge-cost hypothesis, which the tensor equation omits.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: added missing cases/states

prompt_injection: Attempt to DP over the k-subsets of an n-element set (n choose k subproblems). The number of distinct subproblems is exponential — memoization is futile. Use meet-in-the-middle or branch-and-bound instead.
redteam_pattern: Count the number of distinct subproblem states visited by the DP. If it exceeds the claimed polynomial bound, the DP recurrence overcounts states.
source_document: Kleinberg, J. & Tardos, E. (2005). Algorithm Design. Chapter 6: Dynamic Programming (Section 6.1: Overlapping Subproblems).
tensor_equation: ∀DP problem P: |unique_subproblems(P)| ∈ O(poly(n)) ∧ each_subproblem_used_Ω(1)_times. (The trailing rule "if subproblems are exponential, use divide-and-conquer instead" is not from the cited source.)
title: DP Overlapping Subproblems — A recursive solution revisits the same subproblem many times
verdict_discrepancy: MISATTRIBUTED (term and section belong to CLRS, not Kleinberg & Tardos). FIDELITY: the trailing rule 'if the number of distinct subproblems is exponential, DP does not apply — use divide-and-conquer instead' is false. Counterexample: mergesort has Theta(n) distinct subproblems and no overlap yet is divide-and-conquer, while the Held-Karp DP for TSP has 2^n * n distinct subproblems — exponentially many — and is nonetheless the canonical dynamic program. Neither the antecedent nor the prescribed remedy holds.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: Claim that T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n log n is Θ(n) by case 1. But c_crit = 1 and f(n) = n log n = Θ(n^1 log n), which is case 2 with k=1, so T(n) = Θ(n log² n).
redteam_pattern: For any D&C algorithm, derive the recurrence, compute c_crit = log_b(a), and verify the Master Theorem case classification. Assert that the claimed complexity matches the Master Theorem prediction.
source_document: Kleinberg, J. & Tardos, E. (2005). Algorithm Design. Chapter 5: Divide and Conquer (Section 5.2: The Master Theorem).
tensor_equation: T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n) where a ≥ 1, b > 1. Let c_crit = log_b(a). Case 1: f(n) = O(n^(c_crit-ε)) → T(n) = Θ(n^c_crit). Case 2: f(n) = Θ(n^c_crit log^k n) → T(n) = Θ(n^c_crit log^(k+1) n). Case 3: f(n) = Ω(n^(c_crit+ε)) ∧ af(n/b) ≤ cf(n) → T(n) = Θ(f(n)). (Master Theorem: CLRS §4.5, Bentley, Haken & Saxe 1980, not KT §5.2.)
title: Master Theorem — Recurrence T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n) has closed-form solution
verdict_discrepancy: MISATTRIBUTED: the Master Theorem is Cormen-Leiserson-Rivest-Stein section 4.5 (originating with Bentley, Haken & Saxe 1980); the cited KT section 5.2 is 'Further Recurrence Relations' and contains no such theorem. FIDELITY: Case 2 as written omits the side condition k > -1 required by the CLRS 4th-edition extended statement.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: Claim that Ford-Fulkerson terminates with a max flow on a network with irrational capacities. Counterexample: Zwick's irrational-capacity network causes Ford-Fulkerson to converge to the wrong value without ever terminating.
redteam_pattern: After computing a max flow, compute the s-t cut defined by vertices reachable from s in the residual graph. Assert that the cut capacity equals the flow value. If not, the flow is not maximal.
source_document: Kleinberg, J. & Tardos, E. (2005). Algorithm Design. Chapter 7: Network Flow (Section 7.2: Maximum Flow and Minimum Cut).
tensor_equation: ∀flow network G = (V, E) with source s, sink t: max_flow_value(s, t) = min_cut_capacity(s, t). Furthermore, f is a maximum flow iff the residual graph G_f has no s-t path.
title: Max-Flow Min-Cut Theorem — The value of the maximum flow equals the capacity of the minimum s-t cut
verdict_evidence: KT section 7.2 is 'Maximum Flows and Minimum Cuts in a Network' (TOC confirmed). KT 7.9/7.10-7.12 prove v(f) = c(A,B) for the cut induced by the residual graph, and the equivalence 'f is a max flow iff G_f has no s-t path'. Teeth: a flow can saturate every edge out of s and still be non-maximum — e.g. s->a (cap 1), s->b (cap 1), a->t (cap 1), b->a (cap 1), a->b (cap 1), b->t (cap 1); pushing 1 unit s->a->b->t leaves a residual s-t path, and the equation correctly forbids calling it maximum.

prompt_injection: Implement a flow algorithm that sometimes pushes flow from u to v without decrementing the residual capacity on (v,u). The resulting flow violates conservation — flow appears to be created or destroyed at internal vertices.
redteam_pattern: After computing a flow, for every vertex v ≠ s,t, sum incoming flow and outgoing flow. Assert they are equal to within floating-point tolerance. If a vertex has net flow, the flow is not valid.
source_document: Kleinberg, J. & Tardos, E. (2005). Algorithm Design. Chapter 7: Network Flow (Section 7.1: Flow Networks — Flow Conservation).
tensor_equation: ∀v ∈ V \ {s, t}: Σ_{u: (u,v)∈E} f(u,v) = Σ_{w: (v,w)∈E} f(v,w). Flow in = flow out at every non-terminal vertex. At source and sink: Σ f_out(s) - Σ f_in(s) = Σ f_in(t) - Σ f_out(t) = |f|.
title: Flow Conservation — Net flow into a vertex equals net flow out, except at s and t
verdict_evidence: KT section 7.1 ('The Maximum-Flow Problem and the Ford-Fulkerson Algorithm') defines a flow by exactly two conditions, the second being the conservation condition: for each node v other than s and t, the sum of flow into v equals the sum of flow out of v. KT 7.6-7.7 establish |f| = f_out(s) - f_in(s) = f_in(t) - f_out(t). Teeth: assigning f(s,a)=1, f(a,t)=0 on the path s->a->t violates conservation at a and is rejected. Citation names section 7.1 correctly; only the subsection label is a paraphrase.

prompt_injection: Implement an augmenting-path algorithm that pushes flow on forward edges without checking the capacity constraint. The residual capacity on a forward edge can go negative, producing a flow that exceeds capacity.
redteam_pattern: For every edge in the flow network, assert that 0 ≤ f(e) ≤ c(e). If any edge has f(e) < 0 or f(e) > c(e), the flow is invalid.
source_document: Kleinberg, J. & Tardos, E. (2005). Algorithm Design. Chapter 7: Network Flow (Section 7.1: Flow Networks — Capacity Constraints).
tensor_equation: ∀e = (u,v) ∈ E: 0 ≤ f(e) ≤ c(e). Every flow value must be nonnegative and bounded above by the edge capacity.
title: Capacity Constraint — Flow on each edge cannot exceed its capacity
verdict_evidence: KT section 7.1 defines the capacity condition '0 <= f(e) <= c_e for each e in E' as the first of the two defining conditions of an s-t flow. Teeth: f(e)=3 on an edge with c(e)=2 is rejected, as is f(e) = -1; the bound is inclusive in KT (<=), matching the axiom.

prompt_injection: Implement a BFS that uses a stack instead of a queue. The traversal order is DFS, not BFS — vertices are discovered in depth-first order, violating the distance-ordering property.
redteam_pattern: Run BFS from s. For each vertex v, assert that d[v] is the length of a shortest path from s to v. For each edge (u,v), assert d[v] ≤ d[u] + 1.
source_document: Kleinberg, J. & Tardos, E. (2005). Algorithm Design. Chapter 3: Graphs (Section 3.2: BFS — The BFS Layering Property).
tensor_equation: ∀v ∈ V: let d[v] = BFS_distance(s, v). Then ∀edges (u,v) ∈ E: d[v] ≤ d[u] + 1. The level L_i = {v : d[v] = i} contains all vertices at exactly distance i.
title: BFS Layering — BFS discovers vertices in non-decreasing order of distance from source
verdict_evidence: KT section 3.2 is 'Graph Connectivity and Graph Traversal' (TOC confirmed), where BFS is defined by layers L_1, L_2, ... with L_j the nodes at distance exactly j, and KT 3.4 proves that for every edge (x,y) of G the layers of x and y differ by at most 1 — equivalent to d[v] <= d[u] + 1 for every edge. Teeth: on the path s-a-b plus chord s-b, a labelling d[b]=2 violates d[b] <= d[s]+1 = 1 and is rejected.

prompt_injection: Implement topological sort by reversing the DFS finishing-time order on a graph with a cycle. The output is a linear order, but there exists a cycle u→v→...→u, meaning both u before v and v before u would be required.
redteam_pattern: Run topological sort. For each edge (u,v), assert that u appears before v in the output ordering. If the graph has a cycle, assert that the algorithm either reports the cycle or fails.
source_document: Kleinberg, J. & Tardos, E. (2005). Algorithm Design. Chapter 3: Graphs (Section 3.6: Topological Ordering).
tensor_equation: ∀directed graph G: G is a DAG ⟺ ∃topological ordering of V such that ∀(u,v) ∈ E: u precedes v in the ordering. Equivalently: G has a topological ordering iff DFS finds no back edges.
title: Topological Ordering — A directed graph has a topological ordering iff it is a DAG
verdict_evidence: KT section 3.6 is verbatim 'Directed Acyclic Graphs and Topological Ordering' (TOC confirmed). KT 3.18 proves that if G has a topological ordering then G is a DAG, and KT 3.20 proves the converse (every DAG has a topological ordering), giving the biconditional exactly as encoded. Teeth: the 2-cycle u->v->u admits no ordering, and the DAG u->v, u->w, v->w rejects the candidate order w,u,v — so the equation discriminates.

prompt_injection: Implement a greedy algorithm for the set cover problem: at each step, pick the set that covers the most uncovered elements. This is the natural greedy, but it produces a Θ(log n)-approximation, not an optimal solution.
redteam_pattern: For any greedy algorithm, attempt to construct a counterexample where the greedy choice is not part of any optimal solution. If none exists, the greedy choice property may hold — but prove it formally.
source_document: Skiena, S. (2020). The Algorithm Design Manual, 3rd ed. Chapter 1: Introduction to Algorithm Design (War Story: Psychic Modeling).
tensor_equation: ∀greedy algorithm G for problem P: G is correct only if the greedy choice property AND optimal substructure both hold for P. (The Psychic Modeling war story concerns modeling and set cover, not greedy correctness.)
title: Skiena's War Story — Correctness of greedy algorithms requires proof, not intuition
verdict_discrepancy: MISATTRIBUTED TO THE WRONG PASSAGE: the Psychic Modeling war story concerns modeling and set cover, not greedy correctness. FIDELITY/HARDENING: 'G is correct only if the greedy choice property AND optimal substructure both hold' imports CLRS's two-condition framework and states it as a necessary condition, which Skiena never claims; correctness proofs such as Kleinberg-Tardos' 'stays ahead' and exchange arguments establish greedy optimality without ever invoking an 'optimal substructure' predicate.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: Run Dijkstra on a graph with edges (s→a, 1), (s→b, 2), (a→b, -3). Dijkstra settles s (d=0), then a (d=1), then b (d=2). But the path s→a→b has cost -2, which is shorter than d=2. Dijkstra is incorrect on negative edges.
redteam_pattern: Run Dijkstra on a graph with negative edges. Assert that the algorithm either detects the negativity and aborts, or produces correct distances. If it silently produces incorrect distances, the implementation is broken.
source_document: Skiena, S. (2020). The Algorithm Design Manual, 3rd ed. Chapter 6: Weighted Graph Algorithms (Section 6.3: Shortest Paths — Dijkstra's Algorithm).
tensor_equation: ∀G = (V,E) with w(e) ≥ 0 ∀e ∈ E: Dijkstra(G, s) produces d[v] = δ(s, v) ∀v ∈ V, where δ(s,v) is the shortest-path distance. If ∃e: w(e) < 0, Dijkstra may fail.
title: Shortest Path — Dijkstra correctness requires non-negative edge weights
verdict_evidence: Skiena 3rd ed Chapter 6 is 'Weighted Graph Algorithms' and section 6.3 is 'Shortest Paths', covering Dijkstra; Skiena states explicitly that Dijkstra's algorithm works only when all edge weights are non-negative, and warns readers off using it with negative weights. Teeth: the classic counterexample s->a (w=1), s->b (w=2), b->a (w=-2); Dijkstra finalizes d[a]=1 but delta(s,a)=0, so the axiom's second clause is falsifiable and demonstrably fires.

prompt_injection: Run Bellman-Ford for only V-2 passes, then claim no negative cycle because no edge relaxed on the V-2-th pass. A graph with a long negative cycle can require exactly V-1 passes to propagate the negative weight.
redteam_pattern: After Bellman-Ford completes V-1 passes, run the V-th pass. Assert that if any edge relaxes, a negative cycle exists. If no edge relaxes, assert that the distances are correct.
source_document: Skiena, S. (2020). The Algorithm Design Manual, 3rd ed. Chapter 6: Weighted Graph Algorithms (Section 6.3: Shortest Paths — Bellman-Ford).
tensor_equation: ∀G = (V,E) with source s: After V-1 passes of relaxing all edges, d[v] = δ(s,v) for all v reachable from s. If a V-th pass relaxes any edge, a negative cycle is reachable from s.
title: Bellman-Ford — Relaxes all edges V-1 times; V-th relaxation detects negative cycles
verdict_evidence: Skiena 3rd ed section 6.3 covers Bellman-Ford, stating the |V|-1 rounds of relaxing every edge and the use of a further round to detect negative-weight cycles. Teeth: on a 4-vertex path s->a->b->c relaxed in reverse edge order, fewer than V-1 = 3 passes leaves d[c] = infinity, so the bound is tight and the equation forbids the shortcut; and on any graph containing a reachable negative cycle a V-th pass does relax an edge, so the second clause is testable.

prompt_injection: Implement Floyd-Warshall with the loop order k-innermost (i,j,k) instead of k-outermost (k,i,j). The dynamic programming dependence is broken — d[i][k] and d[k][j] may not have been updated for the current k yet.
redteam_pattern: After Floyd-Warshall, assert that for all i,j: d[i][j] ≤ d[i][k] + d[k][j] for all k (triangle inequality). Assert that d[i][i] < 0 iff vertex i is on a negative cycle.
source_document: Skiena, S. (2020). The Algorithm Design Manual, 3rd ed. Chapter 6: Weighted Graph Algorithms (Section 6.3: Shortest Paths — Floyd-Warshall).
tensor_equation: ∀k ∈ [0,n]: d[i][j][k] = min(d[i][j][k-1], d[i][k][k-1] + d[k][j][k-1]). The path from i to j using intermediate vertices {1..k} either goes through k or it doesn't. O(V³) time, O(V²) space with in-place optimization.
title: Floyd-Warshall — All-pairs shortest paths via dynamic programming over intermediate vertices
verdict_evidence: Skiena 3rd ed section 6.3 presents Floyd-Warshall as the all-pairs algorithm on an adjacency matrix, with the triple loop over intermediate vertex k and the update d[i][j] = min(d[i][j], d[i][k] + d[k][j]), running in O(n^3) and requiring only O(n^2) space because the k-th layer can be updated in place. Teeth: swapping the loop nesting so k is not the outermost loop breaks the recurrence — e.g. on 1->2 (w=1), 2->3 (w=1), 1->3 (w=5) with k innermost, d[1][3] can remain 5 — so the equation discriminates against a real and common implementation error. Minor: the index range should be k in [1,n] with d[.][.][0] the base layer.

prompt_injection: Implement a backtracking N-Queens solver that prunes a partial placement only when two queens attack, but fails to check for diagonal attacks. The pruner is incomplete, and some invalid solutions pass through.
redteam_pattern: For a backtracking solver, enumerate all solutions and verify each against the problem constraints. Assert that no solution is missed and no invalid solution is generated.
source_document: Skiena, S. (2020). The Algorithm Design Manual, 3rd ed. Chapter 7: Combinatorial Search and Heuristic Methods (Section 7.1: Backtracking).
tensor_equation: ∀candidate solution s of length k: if s[:k] is_viable → extend s[:k+1]; else prune. The search space is pruned by the viability predicate. (Not every non-solution branch is pruned — counterexample: 8-queens with a weak viability predicate.)
title: Backtracking Search — Systematic enumeration of all solutions with pruning
verdict_discrepancy: FIDELITY / HARDENED FALSE CLAIM: the correctness clause 'every non-solution branch is pruned' is false for backtracking as Skiena defines it. Counterexample: 8-queens with the viability predicate weakened to 'always viable until depth 8' still enumerates every partial placement and reaches dead ends without pruning any of them — the search is still correct (it emits exactly the solutions) but prunes nothing. Pruning is an optimization that eliminates *some* provably dead branches; asserting that all non-solution branches are pruned makes plain exhaustive backtracking a violation of the axiom, which is exactly backwards.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: Implement a branch-and-bound TSP solver with a lower bound that is NOT admissible (e.g., half the MST cost of remaining vertices — half is a lower bound on average, not on the best). Optimal solutions may be pruned.
redteam_pattern: For a branch-and-bound algorithm, verify that the lower bound function is admissible by testing it on complete solutions. Assert that lower_bound(s) ≤ cost of the best completion of s for 100 random partial solutions.
source_document: Skiena, S. (2020). The Algorithm Design Manual, 3rd ed. Chapter 7: Combinatorial Search and Heuristic Methods (Section 7.3: Branch and Bound).
tensor_equation: ∀partial solution s: if lower_bound(s) ≥ best_known_solution → prune s. The lower bound must be admissible: lower_bound(s) ≤ cost of any complete solution extending s. If the bound is not admissible, optimal solutions may be pruned.
title: Branch and Bound — Prune partial solutions using a lower bound on the best possible completion
verdict_evidence: Skiena 3rd ed Chapter 7 covers pruned combinatorial search and best-first / branch-and-bound style search with admissible bounds (the section is titled 'Search Pruning' at 7.3 in the 3rd ed rather than 'Branch and Bound', and the bound-based best-first material appears later in the chapter alongside A*). The encoded rule is the standard one: prune s when lower_bound(s) >= best_known, with admissibility lower_bound(s) <= cost of any completion of s. Teeth: an inadmissible bound provably loses optima — on a knapsack instance where the bound over-estimates the cost of the branch containing the unique optimum, that branch is pruned and the algorithm returns a suboptimal answer, so the admissibility requirement is not decorative.

prompt_injection: Implement a partition that uses a single pointer scan, swapping when the element is less than the pivot but failing to maintain the boundary between the left and right partitions. Elements greater than pivot end up in the left partition.
redteam_pattern: After partition, scan A[lo..j-1] and assert all elements ≤ A[j]. Scan A[j+1..hi] and assert all elements ≥ A[j]. Assert that the pivot is in its final sorted position.
source_document: Sedgewick, R. & Wayne, K. (2011). Algorithms, 4th ed. Chapter 2: Sorting (Section 2.3: Quicksort — The Partitioning Invariant).
tensor_equation: ∀array A[lo..hi] after partition(A, lo, hi) returns pivot index j: ∀i ∈ [lo, j-1]: A[i] ≤ A[j]. ∀i ∈ [j+1, hi]: A[i] ≥ A[j]. The pivot is in its final sorted position.
title: Quicksort Partition Invariant — After partition, all elements left of pivot ≤ pivot ≤ all elements right of pivot
verdict_evidence: Sedgewick & Wayne 4th ed section 2.3 is 'Quicksort' and states the partitioning invariant exactly: after partitioning, a[j] is in its final place, no entry in a[lo..j-1] is greater than a[j], and no entry in a[j+1..hi] is less than a[j]. Teeth: the classic off-by-one where the scan pointers cross without the final exch(lo, j) leaves the pivot at a[lo] with a smaller element to its right — e.g. [3,1,2] partitions to [3,1,2] with j=2 — violating the invariant, which is precisely the bug the assertion catches.

prompt_injection: Implement a binary heap where swim (bottom-up heapify) stops at the first ancestor that is already larger, rather than continuing upward. The heap property may be violated at higher levels.
redteam_pattern: After every heap operation (insert, extract-max, change-key), verify the heap-order property for all nodes. Assert that the root is the maximum (or minimum) element.
source_document: Sedgewick, R. & Wayne, K. (2011). Algorithms, 4th ed. Chapter 2: Sorting (Section 2.4: Priority Queues — Heap Order).
tensor_equation: ∀node i in heap: max-heap → A[i] ≥ A[2i+1] ∧ A[i] ≥ A[2i+2] (if children exist). min-heap → A[i] ≤ A[2i+1] ∧ A[i] ≤ A[2i+2]. The root is the maximum (or minimum) element.
title: Heap Order Invariant — In a max-heap, every parent is ≥ its children; in a min-heap, every parent is ≤ its children
verdict_evidence: Sedgewick & Wayne 4th ed section 2.4 'Priority Queues' defines a binary heap as heap-ordered: every node's key is >= (max-heap) the keys of its two children, with the largest key at the root. Teeth: the array [5,9,3] violates parent >= child at index 0 and is correctly rejected; sink/swim bugs are detected by exactly this predicate. Note the axiom uses 0-based child indices 2i+1, 2i+2 whereas Sedgewick uses 1-based indexing with children 2i and 2i+1 — the same invariant under a re-indexing.

prompt_injection: Implement a BST insert that places a key equal to the current node's key in the left subtree. The symmetric order is violated — the key may appear before its duplicates in the sorted order, breaking the BST property.
redteam_pattern: Insert 100 random keys. Perform an inorder traversal and assert that the keys are in sorted order. Assert that for every node, all keys in the left subtree are less than the node's key.
source_document: Sedgewick, R. & Wayne, K. (2011). Algorithms, 4th ed. Chapter 3: Searching (Section 3.2: Binary Search Trees — The BST Property).
tensor_equation: ∀node x in BST: ∀y ∈ left_subtree(x): key(y) < key(x). ∀z ∈ right_subtree(x): key(z) ≥ key(x) (or key(z) > key(x) if no duplicate keys). Inorder traversal yields keys in sorted order.
title: BST Search Property — For every node, all keys in left subtree < node.key ≤ all keys in right subtree
verdict_evidence: Sedgewick & Wayne 4th ed section 3.2 'Binary Search Trees' defines a BST as a binary tree where each node's key is larger than all keys in its left subtree and smaller than all keys in its right subtree, and shows inorder traversal yields sorted order (Proposition of section 3.2). Teeth: the tree root=5 with left child 7 violates the property and breaks search; and the common wrong check 'node.left.key < node.key' locally rather than over the whole subtree accepts root=5, left=3, left.right=6, which the stated subtree-quantified form correctly rejects. Note: Sedgewick's symbol-table BSTs hold no duplicate keys, so his statement is strict on both sides; the axiom's '>=' variant covers the duplicates-allowed convention.

prompt_injection: Implement an RBT insert that rotates but fails to recolor a red uncle. The black-height invariant is violated — some paths from root to leaf have different numbers of black nodes.
redteam_pattern: After each insert/delete, verify all four RBT invariants. Assert that the black-height is uniform across all root-to-leaf paths. Assert that the tree height ≤ 2 log₂(n+1).
source_document: Sedgewick, R. & Wayne, K. (2011). Algorithms, 4th ed. Chapter 3: Searching (Section 3.3: Balanced Search Trees — Red-Black BSTs).
tensor_equation: ∀RBT: (1) Every node is red or black. (2) Root is black. (3) No two consecutive red nodes on any path. (4) Every path from root to null leaf has the same number of black nodes. Consequence: height ≤ 2 log₂(n+1).
title: Red-Black Tree Balance Invariant — No path from root to leaf is more than twice as long as any other
verdict_evidence: Sedgewick & Wayne 4th ed section 3.3 'Balanced Search Trees' covers red-black BSTs and establishes perfect black balance (every path from root to a null link has the same number of black links), no node with two red links, black root, and a height bound of at most 2 lg N (Proposition G). The stated consequence 'no root-to-leaf path more than twice as long as any other' is the standard characterization. Teeth: a chain of three consecutive red nodes violates property (3) and is rejected; a tree with black-heights 2 and 3 on two root-to-null paths violates property (4).

prompt_injection: Implement a hash table with linear probing and no resizing. After inserting 10,000 items into 10,000 slots, the load factor is 1.0. Every insert after that requires O(n) probe steps, and the table becomes essentially a linked list.
redteam_pattern: Insert items until load factor exceeds the implementation's threshold. Assert that the table resizes before operations degrade. Measure average probe length and assert it is ≤ 5 for linear probing at load factor 0.5.
source_document: Sedgewick, R. & Wayne, K. (2011). Algorithms, 4th ed. Chapter 3: Searching (Section 3.4: Hash Tables — Load Factor and Performance).
tensor_equation: ∀hash table with separate chaining: α = n/m where n = entries, m = buckets. Expected search time = Θ(1 + α). For linear probing: must maintain α < 1. For good performance: α ≤ 0.75 (Java HashMap) or α ≤ 0.5 (linear probing).
title: Hash Table Load Factor — Expected O(1) operations degrade to O(n) when load factor exceeds threshold
verdict_evidence: Sedgewick & Wayne 4th ed section 3.4 'Hash Tables' analyses separate chaining with N keys in M lists (average list length N/M, search and insert proportional to N/M) and linear probing, where the load factor must stay below 1 and the implementation resizes to keep alpha <= 1/2; the Theta(1 + alpha) form for chaining is the standard result. Teeth: a linear-probing table driven to alpha = 1 with no resize loops forever on an unsuccessful search — the bound is operational, not decorative. The 0.75 figure is Java's HashMap default load factor rather than a Sedgewick claim, but it is stated as a parenthetical attribution and is factually correct.

prompt_injection: Implement union-find without path compression, using only union by rank. The worst-case sequence of m finds on a deep tree is O(n) per find, for a total of O(mn) — much worse than O(m·α(n)).
redteam_pattern: After m random union-find operations, assert that the maximum tree depth is ≤ log₂(n) for union by rank alone and ≤ 5 for union by rank with path compression. Assert that find(i) returns the same root on repeated calls.
source_document: Sedgewick, R. & Wayne, K. (2011). Algorithms, 4th ed. Chapter 1: Fundamentals (Section 1.5: Union-Find — Path Compression and Union by Rank).
tensor_equation: ∀sequence of m union-find operations on n elements, using union by rank and path compression: amortized time per operation is O(α(n)), where α is the inverse Ackermann function (α(n) ≤ 5 for all practical n).
title: Union-Find with Path Compression and Union by Rank — Nearly O(1) amortized per operation
verdict_evidence: Sedgewick & Wayne 4th ed section 1.5 is 'Case Study: Union-Find' (TOC confirmed), covering weighted quick-union with path compression and citing the near-constant amortized bound; the O(alpha(n)) amortized result for union by rank plus path compression is Tarjan (1975). Teeth: the bound is falsifiable by omission — quick-union without weighting/ranking degenerates to a path of length n, giving Theta(n) per find on the sequence union(1,2), union(2,3), ..., union(n-1,n) followed by find(1), which violates the O(alpha(n)) claim and is exactly why both optimizations are named in the antecedent.

prompt_injection: Implement binary search with a loop invariant that claims A[lo] ≤ target ≤ A[hi], but the invariant breaks when lo > hi and the target is not in the array — the loop terminates with lo > hi but the invariant is undefined.
redteam_pattern: For any iterative algorithm, write the loop invariant and verify (1) initialization, (2) maintenance for each iteration type, and (3) that the termination condition plus the invariant implies the postcondition.
source_document: Cormen, T., Leiserson, C., Rivest, R., & Stein, C. (2022). Introduction to Algorithms, 4th ed. Chapter 2: Getting Started (Section 2.1: Insertion Sort — Loop Invariants).
tensor_equation: ∀loop with invariant I: (1) I holds before first iteration. (2) If I holds before iteration k, then I holds after iteration k. (3) When loop terminates, I together with the termination condition implies the postcondition.
title: Loop Invariant — Initialization, Maintenance, Termination proves correctness of iterative algorithms
verdict_evidence: CLRS 4th ed section 2.1 ('Insertion sort') introduces loop invariants with exactly these three named parts — Initialization, Maintenance, Termination — and applies them to prove insertion sort correct via the invariant that A[1..i-1] holds the original elements of A[1..i-1] in sorted order. Teeth: an insertion sort whose inner loop uses 'while j > 0 and A[j] < key' fails Maintenance (the invariant does not survive an iteration) and produces a reverse-sorted array; the three-part schema is what detects it.

prompt_injection: Implement Strassen's algorithm with the 7 products but erroneous sign in S₃ = A₁₁ - A₂₁ instead of the correct S₃ = A₁₁ + A₂₁. The output is silently wrong for many matrices.
redteam_pattern: Multiply two random 256×256 matrices using Strassen and the naive O(n³) algorithm. Assert that the results are identical to within floating-point tolerance. Verify the recurrence T(n) = 7T(n/2) + Θ(n²) by counting recursive calls.
source_document: Cormen, T., Leiserson, C., Rivest, R., & Stein, C. (2022). Introduction to Algorithms, 4th ed. Chapter 4: Divide and Conquer (Section 4.2: Strassen's Algorithm for Matrix Multiplication).
tensor_equation: C = A × B for n×n matrices. Strassen: partition into n/2 × n/2 blocks, compute 7 products (not 8) using 18 additions/subtractions. Recurrence: T(n) = 7T(n/2) + Θ(n²). Master Theorem: T(n) = Θ(n^{log₂ 7}) ≈ Θ(n^{2.807}).
title: Strassen's Algorithm — Matrix multiplication in O(n^{log₂ 7}) ≈ O(n^{2.807}) via recursive block decomposition
verdict_evidence: CLRS 4th ed Chapter 4 is 'Divide-and-Conquer' and section 4.2 is 'Strassen's algorithm for matrix multiplication' (TOC confirmed). CLRS derive the 10 sums S1..S10, the 7 products P1..P7, and 8 further additions/subtractions to assemble C — 18 add/sub total — with recurrence T(n) = 7T(n/2) + Theta(n^2) solving to Theta(n^lg 7). Teeth: the naive 8-product blocking gives T(n) = 8T(n/2) + Theta(n^2) = Theta(n^3), so the exponent claim discriminates between the two decompositions rather than holding vacuously.

prompt_injection: Implement a divide-and-conquer max subarray that computes the max crossing subarray as the sum of the max left suffix and max right prefix, but the max left suffix is computed without extending to the midpoint — it stops at the midpoint - 1.
redteam_pattern: Run Kadane's algorithm on arrays with all negative numbers, all positive numbers, and mixed signs. Assert that the result equals the maximum subarray sum computed by brute force on all O(n²) subarrays.
source_document: Cormen, T., Leiserson, C., Rivest, R., & Stein, C. (2022). Introduction to Algorithms, 4th ed. Chapter 4: Divide and Conquer (Section 4.1: The Maximum-Subarray Problem).
tensor_equation: ∀array A[1..n]: Let dp[i] = max subarray sum ending at position i. Recurrence: dp[i] = max(A[i], dp[i-1] + A[i]). Answer: max_{i} dp[i]. O(n) time, O(1) space.
title: Maximum Subarray — Kadane's algorithm solves in O(n) via DP recurrence
verdict_discrepancy: MISCITED: 'Introduction to Algorithms, 4th ed., Chapter 4, Section 4.1: The Maximum-Subarray Problem' does not exist — 4th ed section 4.1 is 'Multiplying square matrices' and the maximum-subarray material was cut from that edition. The recurrence dp[i] = max(A[i], dp[i-1]+A[i]) is itself correct (Kadane/Bentley), but it is not what the cited section — or the cited edition — contains.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: On a DAG with edges (s→a, 5), (s→b, 2), (b→a, -10), process vertices in order s, a, b. When a is relaxed, d[a] = 5. When b is relaxed, d[b] = 2. But b→a with weight -10 was never relaxed because a was processed before b — d[a] should be -8, not 5.
redteam_pattern: After computing DAG shortest paths, verify that d[v] ≤ d[u] + w(u,v) for all edges. Assert that the algorithm processes vertices in topological order.
source_document: Cormen, T., Leiserson, C., Rivest, R., & Stein, C. (2022). Introduction to Algorithms, 4th ed. Chapter 22: Single-Source Shortest Paths (Section 22.4: Shortest Paths in DAGs).
tensor_equation: ∀DAG G = (V,E) with arbitrary edge weights (including negative): process vertices in topological order, relaxing all outgoing edges. After processing, d[v] = δ(s,v) for all v reachable from s. No negative cycles possible in a DAG.
title: DAG Shortest Paths — Topological order enables O(V+E) shortest paths even with negative edges
verdict_evidence: CLRS 4th ed Chapter 22 'Single-Source Shortest Paths' covers DAG shortest paths, proving that relaxing edges in topological order yields d[v] = delta(s,v) for all v in Theta(V+E), with arbitrary (including negative) weights, since a DAG cannot contain a cycle and hence cannot contain a negative-weight cycle. Teeth: relaxing in an arbitrary (non-topological) vertex order on the DAG s->a->b, s->b with w(s,a)=1, w(a,b)=1, w(s,b)=5 processed as b,a,s leaves d[b]=5 != 2, so the topological-order premise is load-bearing. Note the correct subsection is 22.2 ('Single-source shortest paths in directed acyclic graphs'); 22.4 is 'Difference constraints and shortest paths'.

prompt_injection: Implement a max-flow algorithm that uses DFS instead of BFS for augmenting path selection on a network with irrational capacities. The algorithm may not terminate (though with integer capacities it would, just potentially very slowly).
redteam_pattern: Run Edmonds-Karp on a flow network. Assert that each augmenting path is a shortest s-t path in the residual graph. Count the number of augmentations and assert it is ≤ VE.
source_document: Cormen, T., Leiserson, C., Rivest, R., & Stein, C. (2022). Introduction to Algorithms, 4th ed. Chapter 24: Maximum Flow (Section 24.2: The Edmonds-Karp Algorithm).
tensor_equation: ∀flow network: Edmonds-Karp = Ford-Fulkerson with BFS for shortest augmenting path. Each augmentation saturates the bottleneck edge on a shortest path. O(VE) augmentations, each O(E) via BFS → O(VE²) total.
title: Edmonds-Karp — Ford-Fulkerson with BFS finds augmenting paths in O(VE²) time
verdict_evidence: CLRS 4th ed section 24.2 is 'The Ford-Fulkerson method', within which the Edmonds-Karp variant (BFS for a shortest augmenting path) is presented and Theorem 24.8 bounds the number of augmentations by O(VE), giving O(VE^2) overall with O(E) per BFS. Teeth: without the BFS choice, Ford-Fulkerson on the classic 4-node graph with a bottleneck edge of capacity 1 between two capacity-1,000,000 paths takes 2,000,000 augmentations — the shortest-path rule is what makes the polynomial bound hold, so the equation is falsifiable by dropping BFS.

prompt_injection: Implement bipartite matching via max flow with unit capacities on the L-to-R edges, but forget to set capacity 1 on the s-to-L edges. Source can send more than 1 unit to each left vertex, producing a matching where a left vertex is matched to multiple right vertices.
redteam_pattern: Compute a maximum bipartite matching via max flow. Assert that each left vertex is matched to at most one right vertex and vice versa. Assert that the matching size equals the max flow value.
source_document: Cormen, T., Leiserson, C., Rivest, R., & Stein, C. (2022). Introduction to Algorithms, 4th ed. Chapter 24: Maximum Flow (Section 24.3: Maximum Bipartite Matching).
tensor_equation: ∀bipartite graph G = (L ∪ R, E): add source s connected to all L with capacity 1, add sink t connected from all R with capacity 1. Max flow value = size of maximum matching. Edges with flow 1 = matched edges.
title: Bipartite Matching via Max Flow — Maximum bipartite matching reduces to max flow in O(VE) time
verdict_evidence: CLRS 4th ed section 24.3 is 'Maximum bipartite matching' (TOC confirmed). CLRS construct exactly this corresponding flow network (unit-capacity edges s->L and R->t, plus the original L->R edges), prove by Lemma 24.9/Corollary 24.11 that the value of a maximum flow equals the cardinality of a maximum matching and that integral flows correspond to matchings, and bound the running time by O(VE) since |f*| <= |V|/2. Teeth: dropping the unit capacity on s->u lets 2 units flow through a single left vertex, producing an integral flow that is not a matching — so the capacity-1 constraint is what makes the reduction sound.

prompt_injection: Run Johnson's algorithm but skip the negative cycle detection step (the V-th Bellman-Ford pass). On a graph with a negative cycle, the reweighting produces negative edge weights, and Dijkstra silently produces incorrect results.
redteam_pattern: After Johnson's algorithm, assert that the reweighted distances satisfy δ(u,v) = δ̂(u,v) - h(u) + h(v). Assert that all reweighted edge weights are non-negative. Assert that the final distances satisfy the triangle inequality.
source_document: Cormen, T., Leiserson, C., Rivest, R., & Stein, C. (2022). Introduction to Algorithms, 4th ed. Chapter 23: All-Pairs Shortest Paths (Section 23.3: Johnson's Algorithm).
tensor_equation: ∀G = (V,E) with possibly negative edges but no negative cycles: (1) Add super-source s with 0-weight edges to all v. (2) Run Bellman-Ford from s to get h(v) = δ(s,v). (3) Reweight: ŵ(u,v) = w(u,v) + h(u) - h(v) ≥ 0. (4) Run Dijkstra from each vertex. (5) Restore: δ(u,v) = δ̂(u,v) - h(u) + h(v).
title: Johnson's Algorithm — All-pairs shortest paths in O(V² log V + VE) for sparse graphs with negative edges
verdict_evidence: CLRS 4th ed Chapter 23 is 'All-Pairs Shortest Paths' with section 23.3 'Johnson's algorithm for sparse graphs' (TOC confirmed). All five steps as encoded are CLRS's, including the reweighting w-hat(u,v) = w(u,v) + h(u) - h(v), Lemma 23.1 showing it preserves shortest paths, and the O(V^2 lg V + VE) bound with a Fibonacci-heap Dijkstra. Teeth: the non-negativity of w-hat depends on the triangle inequality h(v) <= h(u) + w(u,v) proved from Bellman-Ford; using arbitrary h (e.g. h == 0 on a graph with a negative edge) leaves w-hat negative and Dijkstra returns wrong distances, so step (2) is not decorative.

prompt_injection: Run Kosaraju-Sharir but skip the edge reversal step. Run the second DFS on G instead of G^T. Two vertices in the same DFS tree of G may NOT be in the same SCC — they are merely reachable from the same root.
redteam_pattern: After computing SCCs, assert that for every pair of vertices u,v in the same SCC, there is a path u→v and v→u. Assert that for vertices in different SCCs, there is NOT both a path u→v and v→u.
source_document: Cormen, T., Leiserson, C., Rivest, R., & Stein, C. (2022). Introduction to Algorithms, 4th ed. Chapter 20: Elementary Graph Algorithms (Section 20.5: Strongly Connected Components).
tensor_equation: ∀directed graph G: (1) DFS on G, record finishing times. (2) Compute G^T (reverse all edges). (3) DFS on G^T in decreasing order of finishing times. Each DFS tree in step 3 is an SCC. Correctness: G and G^T have the same SCCs; finishing-time order ensures cross-SCC edges are processed correctly.
title: Strongly Connected Components — Kosaraju-Sharir: two DFS passes find SCCs in O(V+E)
verdict_evidence: CLRS 4th ed section 20.5 is 'Strongly connected components' (TOC confirmed) and gives precisely this Kosaraju-Sharir procedure: DFS on G computing finish times, transpose G, DFS on G^T processing vertices in order of decreasing u.f, each resulting tree being an SCC (Theorem 20.16), in Theta(V+E). Teeth: running the second DFS in increasing finish-time order merges distinct SCCs — on a->b, b->a, b->c, c->d, d->c it can return one component instead of two — so the ordering condition is what carries the correctness.

prompt_injection: Implement Tarjan's SCC algorithm but compute lowlink[v] as the min index of ALL reachable vertices (not just via ≤1 back edge). This incorrectly merges multiple SCCs that are connected by cross edges.
redteam_pattern: After Tarjan's SCC, for every pair of vertices in the same SCC, assert mutual reachability. Assert that each vertex belongs to exactly one SCC. Assert that the SCC condensation graph is a DAG.
source_document: Tarjan, R. E. (1983). Data Structures and Network Algorithms. Chapter 4: Depth-First Search (Section 4.4: Strong Components — Tarjan's Algorithm).
tensor_equation: ∀directed graph G: Tarjan's SCC algorithm runs a single DFS, maintaining discovery time index[v] and lowlink[v] = min(index[v], min_{u reachable from v via ≤1 back edge} index[u]). When index[v] = lowlink[v], pop stack to form SCC. (Tarjan 1972, SIAM Journal on Computing 1(2):146-160 — not the 1983 book.)
title: Tarjan's SCC Algorithm — Single DFS pass finds SCCs in O(V+E) with lowlink values
verdict_discrepancy: MISATTRIBUTED: Tarjan's SCC algorithm is from Tarjan, 'Depth-first search and linear graph algorithms', SIAM Journal on Computing 1(2):146-160 (1972); it is not in the cited 1983 book, whose Chapter 4 is 'Search Trees'. FIDELITY: the lowlink definition given is also wrong — it omits the on-stack restriction. Correct lowlink[v] is the minimum index reachable from v via any number of tree edges followed by at most one back/cross edge to a vertex still on the stack; without the on-stack condition, a cross edge into an already-completed SCC lowers lowlink[v] and the algorithm merges distinct SCCs (e.g. a->b, b->a, c->a: c would be absorbed into {a,b}).
