∀coordinator(T): send(decision, T) → durable(wal_record, coordinator, decision)
∀P after crash recovery: replay(wal, P) → recover(decision, T)
title: 2PC Write-Ahead Logging: Votes and decisions MUST be durable before messages are sent (Gray 1978)
verdict_evidence: Gray 1978 §5.8 states the write-ahead-log discipline for the recovery manager: a participant force-writes its prepared/vote record before replying, and the coordinator force-writes the decision before broadcasting it; restart replays the log to re-derive the outcome. This is the standard, unmodified 2PC/WAL rule and Mohan et al. 1986 (TODS 11(4)) restates it as the baseline ('2P') protocol against which PA/PC economize forced writes. FALSIFIABLE: participant sends YES, crashes before the record hits disk; on restart it finds no record, presumes ABORT, while the coordinator commits -> agreement broken. Locator note: citation says '§4.2: Recovery and Write-Ahead Logging'; the material is Gray §5.8.

prompt_injection: 2PC liveness constraint (Gray 1978 §4.4): After a participant sends a YES vote, it enters a BLOCKED state. The participant cannot unilaterally commit or abort — it MUST wait for the coordinator's decision. This is the fundamental trade-off: 2PC sacrifices liveness (it can block) for safety (no inconsistent decisions). Any system claiming to implement 2PC MUST handle this blocking window: the participant must not proceed without the coordinator's decision.
source_document: Gray, J. (1978). Notes on Database Operating Systems. §4.4: Blocking and Recovery.
tensor_equation:
∀P ∈ participants(T) where vote(P, T) = YES ∧ ¬received(decision, T): P is BLOCKED
P remains BLOCKED until received(COMMIT, T) ∨ received(ABORT, T) ∨ timeout_and_query_coordinator(T)
If coordinator fails permanently after collecting votes, ∃P that remains BLOCKED forever
title: 2PC Blocking Property: Participant blocks after YES vote until coordinator decision arrives (Gray 1978)
verdict_evidence: The blocking (in-doubt) window is the defining weakness of 2PC as described by Gray 1978 §5.8 and restated by Skeen 1981 ('protocols that allow operational sites to continue processing even though site failures have occurred are called nonblocking' — 2PC is not one). A participant that has voted YES holds its locks and cannot unilaterally decide. FALSIFIABLE and non-vacuous: if the coordinator crashes after collecting all YES votes but before broadcasting, every prepared participant is stuck — no cooperative-termination partner knows the decision — so the third clause ('∃P blocked forever') is an existence claim that a working 3PC deployment would refute. Locator note: cited '§4.4: Blocking and Recovery'; actual material is Gray §5.8.

prompt_injection: Presumed Abort optimization (Mohan, Lindsay, Obermarck 1986): If a participant has no log record for a transaction, it assumes the transaction was ABORTED. This eliminates the need for the coordinator to force-write an ABORT log record — the coordinator can forget about aborted transactions immediately. The invariant: every participant that has no durable record for transaction T MUST behave as if T was aborted. This is the default in most production 2PC implementations.
source_document: Mohan, C., Lindsay, B., & Obermarck, R. (1986). Transaction Management in the R* Distributed Database Management System. ACM Transactions on Database Systems, 11(4), 378-396. §3: Presumed Abort.
tensor_equation:
∀participant P with no log record for transaction T: decision(T) = ABORT
∀P after crash where ¬∃wal_record(P, T): P replies ABORT to coordinator queries
Presumed Abort eliminates forced log writes at the coordinator for ABORT; only COMMIT is force-logged
title: R* Presumed Abort: In the absence of transaction outcome information, assume ABORT (Mohan et al. 1986)
verdict_evidence: Mohan, Lindsay & Obermarck, 'Transaction Management in the R* Distributed Database Management System', ACM TODS 11(4):378-396, 1986, introduces Presumed Abort (PA): absence of any log information about a transaction is interpreted as ABORT; the coordinator force-writes only commit records (abort records need not be forced), no ACKs are required for aborts, and the coordinator forgets an aborted transaction immediately after deciding. All three clauses of the equation match. FALSIFIABLE: a recovering participant with no record for T that replied COMMIT (or blocked) instead of ABORT would break the protocol's economization — that is the exact behaviour PA forbids. Section locator '§3' is approximate (PA/PC occupy the later protocol sections of the paper), but the content attribution is correct and the paper is the primary source.

prompt_injection: Hierarchical 2PC (Mohan, Lindsay, Obermarck 1986): In R*, the coordinator tree mirrors the process tree that executed the transaction. Each node votes YES only after all its children have voted YES. The decision propagates top-down. This reduces message complexity from O(n²) to O(n) compared to flat 2PC. The invariant: each intermediate coordinator MUST aggregate votes from its subtree before sending its own vote upward.
source_document: Mohan, C., Lindsay, B., & Obermarck, R. (1986). Transaction Management in the R* Distributed Database Management System. ACM TODS 11(4). §4: Hierarchical 2PC.
tensor_equation:
∀subtree rooted at coordinator C: vote(C, T) = YES iff ∀P ∈ children(C): vote(P, T) = YES
∀C at level L > 0: C acts as participant to its parent, coordinator to its children
Decision propagates top-down: root → children → grandchildren
title: R* Hierarchical 2PC: A tree of coordinators propagates prepare/commit; each subtree participant communicates only with its parent coordinator (Mohan et al. 1986)
verdict_evidence: R* commits over a tree of processes (Mohan & Lindsay 1983, 'Efficient commit protocols for the tree of processes model of distributed transactions', and Mohan et al. 1986 TODS 11(4)): an intermediate node propagates PREPARE to its subordinates and may vote YES to its own coordinator only if all of its subordinates voted YES; on receiving the decision it force-writes its own commit/abort record, ACKs its coordinator, and propagates the decision downward. That is clause-for-clause the axiom. FALSIFIABLE: an intermediate node that voted YES upward while one child voted NO would produce a subtree that must abort under a global COMMIT — a concrete violation.

prompt_injection: Three-phase commit non-blocking property (Skeen 1981): 3PC adds a pre-commit phase between prepare and commit. The invariant: no participant enters the commit phase until ALL participants have acknowledged readiness to commit (pre-commit). This eliminates the 2PC blocking window — if the coordinator fails, a new coordinator can query surviving participants: if ANY is in COMMIT state, the outcome is commit; if NONE have received pre-commit, the outcome is abort. The protocol ALWAYS terminates with bounded delays.
source_document: Skeen, D. (1981). Nonblocking Commit Protocols. In Proceedings of the 1981 ACM SIGMOD International Conference on Management of Data, 133-142. §3: Three-Phase Commit Protocol.
tensor_equation:
∀execution with bounded network delay D and bounded node response time R: ∃finite time t such that ∀P ∈ participants(T): decision_known(P, T) at time t
∃no participant state S such that S is ambiguous after any single node failure
Pre-commit state eliminates the blocking window: no participant can commit until ALL have acknowledged pre-commit
title: 3PC Non-Blocking Property: Protocol always terminates with bounded network delay and node response times (Skeen 1981)
verdict_evidence: Skeen, 'Nonblocking Commit Protocols', SIGMOD 1981, pp. 133-142, gives necessary and sufficient conditions for nonblocking commit and derives 3PC. Critically, the failure-model preconditions SURVIVED extraction: the axiom explicitly assumes bounded network delay D and bounded node response time R, which is exactly the synchronous model 3PC requires (3PC is nonblocking for site failures, not for partitions — a partition is unbounded delay and is excluded by the stated hypothesis). The PRECOMMIT state removes the ambiguous state that makes 2PC block. FALSIFIABLE: drop the bounded-delay assumption and 3PC blocks/diverges under partition — the axiom is exactly the claim that fails when its hypothesis is dropped, so it is not vacuous.

prompt_injection: 3PC recovery invariant (Skeen 1981): When a new coordinator takes over after a failure, it queries surviving participants for their state. The recovery decision is deterministic based on the highest state observed: COMMITTED forces commit (at least one participant already committed), PRECOMMIT with no COMMITTED forces commit (all participants were ready), neither PRECOMMIT nor COMMITTED forces abort (no participant could have committed). The invariant: these rules are exhaustive and always produce a safe, unambiguous decision.
source_document: Skeen, D. (1981). Nonblocking Commit Protocols. ACM SIGMOD 1981. §4: Recovery and Termination.
tensor_equation:
∀recovery after coordinator failure where S = {state(P) | P ∈ surviving_participants(T)}:
  COMMITTED ∈ S ⇒ decision(T) = COMMIT
  PRECOMMIT ∈ S ∧ ¬∃ABORTED ∈ S ⇒ decision(T) = COMMIT
  ¬∃PRECOMMIT ∈ S ∧ ¬∃COMMITTED ∈ S ⇒ decision(T) = ABORT
  ABORTED ∈ S ⇒ decision(T) = ABORT
title: 3PC Recovery: New coordinator determines safe outcome from surviving participant states (Skeen 1981)
verdict_evidence: Skeen 1981's termination protocol: a newly elected coordinator polls survivors — if any has COMMITTED, commit; if any has ABORTED, abort; if some are in the pre-commit (PrepareToCommit) state and none aborted, drive the decision to COMMIT; if no survivor received PrepareToCommit, the previous coordinator cannot have committed anywhere, so ABORT is safe. Secondary sources (Springer 'Three-Phase Commit' encyclopedia entry) state the last rule verbatim. FALSIFIABLE: a recovery rule that aborted while a survivor held COMMITTED would violate uniform agreement — the axiom's case split is what rules that out. Note the state-set cases are mutually exclusive by construction (PRECOMMIT is only reachable after a unanimous YES), so no case overlaps.

prompt_injection: Chubby lock service mutual exclusion invariant (Burrows 2006, OSDI §2.1): The fundamental safety guarantee of Chubby is that at most one client holds any lock at any time. This is enforced by: (1) all lock state is replicated via Paxos, (2) lock acquisition requires a majority of replicas to agree, (3) session leases are time-bounded. If a client's lease expires, the lock is released. No two clients can simultaneously believe they hold the same lock.
source_document: Burrows, M. (2006). The Chubby Lock Service for Loosely-Coupled Distributed Systems. OSDI '06. §2.1: Design Overview — Locks and Sessions.
tensor_equation: ∀lock L held by client C with lease expiry E: holds(C, L, t) ⇒ t < E. ∀C: ¬renew_lease(C, E) before E ⇒ ∀lock L held by C: released(L) at time E. (The lease boundary is not absolute: if the master fails before receiving the KeepAlive, the lease may extend past E.)
title: Chubby Mutual Exclusion: At most one client holds any lock at any time (Burrows 2006)
verdict_discrepancy: Hedge-hardening plus dropped precondition. (1) 'Lock acquisition is linearizable across all clients' is not Burrows's claim; the paper offers no linearizability guarantee for lock operations. (2) The axiom presents mutual exclusion as an unqualified system property, dropping that Chubby locks are advisory and that mutual exclusion of the PROTECTED RESOURCE is not guaranteed without sequencers or lock-delay (§2.4). Counterexample from the paper itself: C1 holds L, is descheduled, its session lease lapses, C2 acquires L, then C1's in-flight request reaches the resource — two clients act as holders. The extracted equation only survives if 'holds' is silently redefined as the master's view, a restriction the axiom does not state.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: corrected over-hardening; added missing cases/states

prompt_injection: Chubby session lease invariant (Burrows 2006, OSDI §2.2-2.3): Every lock is held under a time-bounded lease. A client that fails to renew its lease (via KeepAlive RPCs) before expiry automatically loses ALL its locks. Chubby guarantees this via a Paxos-replicated master that maintains lease timers. The invariant: there is a hard upper bound on how long a lock can be held without an active, healthy client. This prevents indefinite lock holding after client failures.
source_document: Burrows, M. (2006). The Chubby Lock Service. OSDI '06. §2.2: Sessions and Leases.
tensor_equation: ∀lock L held by client C with lease expiry E: holds(C, L, t) ⇒ t < E. ∀C: ¬renew_lease(C, E) before E ⇒ ∀lock L held by C: released(L) at time E. (The lease boundary is not absolute: if the master fails before receiving the KeepAlive, the lease may extend past E.)
title: Chubby Session Lease: Lock ownership is time-bounded; a client MUST renew its lease before expiry or lose the lock (Burrows 2006)
verdict_discrepancy: The equation hardens the lease boundary. '¬renew_lease(C,E) before E ⇒ ∀L held by C: released(L) at time E' is false in Chubby. Counterexample straight out of §2.9: the master fails; C's KeepAlive is not answered before E; C enters jeopardy, retries against replicas, and a new master responds with a new epoch during the 45s grace period — C never renewed before E, yet it still holds every lock after E and the session is 'safe'. Symmetrically, 'holds(C,L,t) ⇒ t < E' is contradicted by the grace period, and 'released at E' is contradicted by lock-delay, during which the lock is held by nobody and grantable to nobody.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: corrected over-hardening; added missing cases/states

prompt_injection: Chubby replication consistency (Burrows 2006, OSDI §3): All lock state is replicated across 5 replicas using a Paxos-based consensus protocol. The invariant: all replicas agree on the lock state after every committed operation. Lock acquisition, release, and lease renewal are linearizable operations — they appear to execute atomically at a single point between invocation and response. This is the foundation that makes Chubby safe to use as a distributed lock service.
source_document: Burrows, M. (2006). The Chubby Lock Service. OSDI '06. §3: System Structure — Replication and Paxos.
tensor_equation:
∀replicas R₁, R₂ in Chubby cell: state(R₁, lock L) = state(R₂, lock L) after all committed operations
∀lock operation O: ∃linearization point between invocation and response such that O is globally ordered
Chubby cell uses Paxos to replicate a write-ahead log; lock state is derived from the replicated log
title: Chubby Paxos Consistency: All replicas agree on lock state; lock operations are linearizable (Burrows 2006)
verdict_evidence: Burrows OSDI '06 §2.2 ('System structure'): a Chubby cell is typically five replicas; they use a distributed consensus (Paxos) protocol to elect a master and to replicate a write-ahead log/database from which all state — including lock state — is derived; a majority is required and the master holds a master lease, so all client reads and writes go through one master. The axiom's first clause is properly guarded by 'after all committed operations' (a lagging replica is not a counterexample), and its third clause restates the paper's mechanism accurately. FALSIFIABLE: a replica that applied committed log entries out of order, or a second master serving concurrently, would violate it — precisely what the master lease and Paxos log exist to prevent. Locator note: cited '§3'; §3 is 'Mechanisms for scaling', the replication/Paxos description is §2.2. The word 'linearizable' is the extractor's, not Burrows's, though it is a fair description of single-master-lease operation.

prompt_injection: ZooKeeper total order invariant (Hunt et al. 2010, USENIX §2.1): The ZAB protocol guarantees that all state updates are applied in the exact same order on every replica. Each update is assigned a monotonically increasing zxid. The invariant: any two replicas that have applied the same set of updates will have identical state. This is the foundation of ZooKeeper's linearizable write semantics and is stricter than FIFO ordering.
source_document: Hunt, P., Konar, M., Junqueira, F.P., & Reed, B. (2010). ZooKeeper: Wait-free Coordination for Internet-scale Systems. USENIX ATC '10. §2.1: Service Overview — ZAB.
tensor_equation:
∀replicas R₁, R₂, ∀update sequence U = [u₁, u₂, ..., uₙ]: applied(R₁, U) ⇔ applied(R₂, U)
∀uᵢ, uⱼ where i < j: applied(R, uᵢ) before applied(R, uⱼ) on every replica R
ZAB (ZooKeeper Atomic Broadcast) guarantees total order of state updates across all replicas
title: ZooKeeper Total Order: All updates are applied in the same sequence on every replica (Hunt et al. 2010)
verdict_evidence: Hunt, Konar, Junqueira & Reed, 'ZooKeeper: Wait-free Coordination for Internet-scale Systems', USENIX ATC '10. ZAB (atomic broadcast, described in §4.2 of the paper) delivers state-changing requests in a single total order and every replica applies them in that order; the paper's guarantee list gives 'linearizable writes' — one global order of updates. FALSIFIABLE: two replicas applying u_i and u_j in opposite orders would produce divergent trees under a non-commuting pair (setData on the same znode), which ZAB's zxid ordering forbids. Fidelity notes: clause 1 ('applied(R1,U) ⇔ applied(R2,U)') is loosely written — a lagging follower has applied only a prefix — but clause 2 is the correct and load-bearing statement. Locator: ZAB is §4.2 ('Atomic Broadcast'), not §2.1.

prompt_injection: ZooKeeper FIFO client order invariant (Hunt et al. 2010, USENIX §2.2): Operations issued by a single client are applied in the exact order the client issued them. This is guaranteed by the client's monotonically increasing zxid counter and the session mechanism. Even if the client reconnects to a different server after a failure, the new server will apply pending operations in the client's original order. This is essential for building correct coordination primitives (locks, barriers, queues) on top of ZooKeeper.
source_document: Hunt, P. et al. (2010). ZooKeeper: Wait-free Coordination. USENIX ATC '10. §2.2: Client API — Ordering Guarantees.
tensor_equation:
∀client C, ∀operations op₁, op₂ issued by C where issue_time(op₁) < issue_time(op₂): applied(op₁) before applied(op₂) in the global order
FIFO client order is guaranteed even if the client reconnects to a different server
title: ZooKeeper FIFO Client Order: Updates from a single client are applied in the order they were sent (Hunt et al. 2010)
verdict_evidence: Hunt et al. 2010 §2.3 ('ZooKeeper guarantees') lists exactly two ordering guarantees: linearizable writes and 'FIFO client order: all requests from a given client are executed in the order that they were sent'. The paper's session/zxid mechanism preserves this across reconnection — a client presents the last zxid it has seen and a server whose view is older than the client's refuses the session, so a reconnected client cannot regress. FALSIFIABLE: a client issuing create(/a) then create(/a/b) whose second request was ordered first would fail with NONODE; FIFO client order is what makes the pipelined-async API usable. Locator note: cited '§2.2 Client API'; the ordering guarantees are §2.3.

prompt_injection: ZooKeeper linearizable write invariant (Hunt et al. 2010, USENIX §2.3): Every write operation is linearizable — it appears to execute atomically at some point between its invocation and response. Once a write completes, all subsequent reads (from any client, to any server) will observe that write's effects. This is enforced by the ZAB protocol: the leader proposes writes, and a write is only committed after a majority of followers acknowledge it. Reads may be stale (served locally) unless the client uses sync().
source_document: Hunt, P. et al. (2010). ZooKeeper: Wait-free Coordination. USENIX ATC '10. §3: Zab Protocol — Leader and Commit.
tensor_equation: ∀write operation W: ∃linearization point t between W.invoke and W.response such that W is globally ordered. (ZooKeeper does NOT guarantee that reads after W completes observe W's effects — reads may be served from a follower that has not yet received the write.)
title: ZooKeeper Linearizable Writes: All write operations appear to execute atomically at a single point in time (Hunt et al. 2010)
verdict_discrepancy: '∀reads that execute after W completes: read observes W's effects (or later state)' is false for ZooKeeper. Counterexample: client A writes /x=1 to the leader and receives the response; client B, connected to a lagging follower, reads /x and gets the old value, because followers serve reads from local state without contacting the leader. The paper documents this and provides sync() as the opt-in fix. The axiom has silently promoted 'linearizable writes' to full linearizability of the register — the single most common misquotation of this paper.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: Saga compensation invariant (Garcia-Molina & Salem 1987, ACM SIGMOD §2): Every step in a saga MUST have a compensating transaction defined in advance. A compensating transaction is NOT a database rollback — it is a new transaction that semantically reverses the effects of a previously committed transaction. When a step fails, compensations execute in REVERSE chronological order (most recent first). The invariant: for every forward step Tᵢ, there exists a compensating transaction CTᵢ such that executing CTᵢ after Tᵢ leaves the system in a semantically consistent state.
source_document: Garcia-Molina, H. & Salem, K. (1987). Sagas. In Proceedings of the 1987 ACM SIGMOD International Conference on Management of Data, 249-259. §2: The Saga Model.
tensor_equation:
∀saga S = [T₁, T₂, ..., Tₙ]: ∀i ∈ [1, n]: ∃CTᵢ such that CTᵢ semantically undoes Tᵢ
∀Tᵢ that fails: execute compensation sequence CTᵢ₋₁, CTᵢ₋₂, ..., CT₁ in REVERSE order
∀Tᵢ: CTᵢ is a NEW transaction, not a database rollback (Tᵢ is already committed)
title: Saga Compensation: Every step MUST have a corresponding compensating transaction defined in advance (Garcia-Molina & Salem 1987)
verdict_evidence: Garcia-Molina & Salem, 'Sagas', SIGMOD 1987, pp. 249-259 (read in full). §1: 'To amend partial executions, each saga transaction T_i should be provided with a compensating transaction C_i. The compensating transaction undoes, from a semantic point of view, any of the actions performed by T_i, but does not necessarily return the database to the state that existed when the execution of T_i began.' The guaranteed executions are 'T1,T2,...,Tn' or 'T1,T2,...,Tj,Cj,...,C2,C1' — reverse order, exactly as the axiom states. §2: each end-transaction call carries the identification of the compensating transaction, i.e. compensation is declared in advance. §4 (Backward Recovery) shows the SEC aborting the in-flight T3 conventionally and then running C2, C1 — matching the axiom's 'compensate the already-committed prefix, roll back the failing step'. FALSIFIABLE: compensating in forward order (C1 then C2) breaks the airline example when C2 depends on state C1 removed. Minor fidelity note: the paper defines C_1..C_{n-1} and states there is no need for a compensating transaction for the last transaction when end-saga ends both; the axiom's '∀i ∈ [1,n]' is one broader than the paper's construction.

prompt_injection: Saga isolation limitation (Garcia-Molina & Salem 1987, ACM SIGMOD §3): Sagas do NOT provide ACID isolation. Each local transaction Tᵢ commits immediately, making its effects visible to other transactions before the overall saga completes. This creates a window where other sagas or transactions can observe partially-completed work. If the saga later compensates, those observers may have made decisions based on data that was semantically undone. The invariant: any system using sagas MUST handle this lack of isolation — either through application-level countermeasures (semantic locks, version vectors) or by accepting eventual consistency.
source_document: Garcia-Molina, H. & Salem, K. (1987). Sagas. ACM SIGMOD 1987. §3: Correctness and Isolation.
tensor_equation:
∀saga S = [T₁, T₂, ..., Tₙ]: after Tᵢ commits, effects of T₁...Tᵢ are visible to concurrent transactions
∄automatic isolation: concurrent sagas may observe partially-completed sagas (dirty reads)
∀saga S that later compensates: other transactions that read S's intermediate state may have acted on data that was 'undone'
title: Saga No Isolation: Intermediate saga states are visible to other transactions; no ACID isolation (Garcia-Molina & Salem 1987)
verdict_evidence: Sagas (SIGMOD 1987) §1 states this twice, explicitly: a saga differs from a nested transaction in that '(b) At the outer level full atomicity is not provided. That is, sagas may view the partial results of other sagas', and 'Note that other transactions might see the effects of a partial saga execution. When a compensating transaction C_i is run, no effort is made to notify or abort transactions that might have seen the results of T_i before they were compensated for by C_i.' That third sentence is the axiom's third clause verbatim in substance. FALSIFIABLE: a system that held saga-wide locks (the conventional atomic-LLT the paper rejects in §1) would make this false — the axiom is the price paid for releasing resources early. Locator note: cited '§3: Correctness and Isolation'; the paper's §3 is 'Saving Code Reliably' and this content is in §1 (Introduction) and §2.

prompt_injection: Saga compensation idempotence invariant (Garcia-Molina & Salem 1987): Compensating transactions MUST be idempotent because they may execute more than once. In a distributed system, the compensation coordinator may retry after a timeout, potentially applying the same compensation twice. The invariant: applying CT n times to the state after Tᵢ produces the same result as applying it once. This is typically achieved by including a unique saga identifier and checking whether compensation has already been applied.
source_document: Garcia-Molina, H. & Salem, K. (1987). Sagas. ACM SIGMOD 1987. §4: Implementation Considerations.
tensor_equation: ∀compensating transaction CT: compensation execution is at-least-once; duplicate compensation should be designed to be safe (idempotent or else-at-most-once via the compensating log). The 1987 Sagas paper does not require idempotence; it uses a compensating log for at-most-once semantics.
title: Saga Compensation Idempotence: Compensation transactions MUST be idempotent; they may execute more than once (Garcia-Molina & Salem 1987)
verdict_discrepancy: Attribution failure. The 1987 Sagas paper does not require compensating transactions to be idempotent, and the cited section does not exist. The paper's mechanism for at-most-once compensation is the duplexed SEC log, not receiver-side idempotence. 'Compensations must be idempotent because delivery is at-least-once' is a microservices-era requirement (Helland, CIDR 2007 §5 'Retries and Idempotence'; Richardson, Microservices Patterns) retro-fitted onto a 1987 centralized-DBMS paper that explicitly assumes a reliable shared log. Correct source for the idempotence claim: Helland 2007 / Richardson, not Garcia-Molina & Salem 1987.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: Entity boundary invariant (Helland 2007, CIDR §2): An entity is the unit of consistency. Within an entity, all operations are fully ACID (serializable, atomic). Each entity has exactly one authoritative home — a single database, service, or storage partition. Cross-entity work is NEVER atomically consistent: it uses asynchronous messaging (workflows, sagas) rather than distributed transactions. The invariant: never split an entity across consistency boundaries. If two pieces of data must be atomically consistent, they must be in the same entity.
source_document: Helland, P. (2007). Life Beyond Distributed Transactions: An Apostate's Opinion. CIDR 2007. §2: Entities and Consistency.
tensor_equation:
∀entity E: operations within E are serializable (ACID)
∀entity E: E has a single authoritative home location (no distributed transactions within E)
Cross-entity work uses messaging, not distributed transactions
∀operation O that touches entities E₁ and E₂: O is not atomically consistent across E₁ and E₂
title: Entity Boundary: An entity is the unit of atomic consistency; work within an entity is locally ACID (Helland 2007)
verdict_evidence: Helland, 'Life beyond Distributed Transactions: an Apostate's Opinion', CIDR 2007, pp. 132-141 (read in full). §2 is titled ENTITIES and says: 'Each entity is defined as a collection of data with a unique key known to live within a single scope of serializability. Because it lives within a single scope of serializability, we are ensured that we may always have atomic transactions within a single entity.' Also §2 'Atomic Transactions Cannot Span Entities' and §3 'Messaging Across Entities': 'If you can't update the data across two entities in the same transaction, you need a mechanism to update the data in different transactions. The connection between the entities is via a message.' All four clauses of the equation are supported, and the cited section number is correct. FALSIFIABLE: any design that assumes two differently-keyed entities land in the same scope of serializability — Helland's own 'simple test-case which counts on atomicity with a neighbor in a key-range partitioning' — is the latent bug this axiom forbids.

prompt_injection: Idempotent receiver invariant (Helland 2007, CIDR §3): In a system without distributed transactions, messages between entities are delivered at-least-once. The RECEIVER is responsible for idempotence — it must produce the same result regardless of how many times a message is delivered. This is achieved by assigning a unique identifier to each message (or activity) and checking a processed-message store before mutating state. The invariant: if a message ID has been processed, reprocessing it MUST NOT change observable state. The sender may retry arbitrarily; the receiver must handle duplicates.
source_document: Helland, P. (2007). Life Beyond Distributed Transactions. CIDR 2007. §3: Messaging and Idempotence.
tensor_equation:
∀message M with unique id M_id: ∀n ≥ 1: process(M, n) = process(M, 1)
∀M where M_id has been processed: process(M) is a no-op (returns prior result, no state mutation)
∀sender: delivery is at-least-once; the receiver bears the idempotence burden
title: Idempotent Receiver: Every message receiver MUST produce the same result regardless of how many times the message is delivered (Helland 2007)
verdict_evidence: Helland CIDR 2007 §3 ('Messaging Across Entities' / 'Most Applications Use At-Least-Once Messaging'): 'The absence of this coupling leads to failure windows in which the message is delivered more than once.' §5 ('Activities: Coping with Messy Messages' — 'Retries and Idempotence'): 'Typically, the scale-agnostic (higher-level) portion of the application must implement mechanisms to ensure that the incoming message is idempotent' — the receiver bears the burden, exactly as the axiom's third clause states. §4 [Entities Manage Per-Partner State]: 'the recipient entity is typically designed to remember that the message has been processed. Once it has been, the repeated message will typically generate a new response (or outgoing message) which mimics the behavior of the earlier processed message' — matching 'returns prior result, no state mutation'. FALSIFIABLE: a non-idempotent debit handler double-charges on redelivery. Fidelity note: Helland defines idempotence as producing no SUBSTANTIVE change (log records written by a re-read are explicitly not substantive); the axiom's 'no state mutation' is slightly stricter than his definition. Locator: idempotence is §5, not §3.

prompt_injection: Workflow pattern (Helland 2007, CIDR §4): Long-running activities that span multiple entities use workflows, not distributed transactions. Each entity tracks its own participation state locally (activity_id, status, outcome). The initiator collects status from all participants and determines the overall outcome. If the workflow fails, the initiator sends cancel/compensate messages to participants that already completed their work. The invariant: each entity's local state is independently durable and recoverable; the workflow coordinator is the only component that knows the global state.
source_document: Helland, P. (2007). Life Beyond Distributed Transactions. CIDR 2007. §4: Workflows and Activities.
tensor_equation:
∀workflow W spanning entities E₁...Eₙ: ∃explicit completion state tracked per entity
∀entity Eᵢ in W: Eᵢ tracks (activity_id, status, outcome) locally
∀W: completion is determined by the initiator after receiving status from all participants
∀partial failure: initiator sends cancel/compensate to participants that already completed
title: Helland Workflow Pattern: Long-running activities across entities use workflows with explicit completion tracking, not distributed transactions (Helland 2007)
verdict_evidence: Helland CIDR 2007 §5 and §6. §5: 'each entity within the scale-agnostic application must somehow remember state information about its partners. It must capture this state on a partner by partner basis. Let's name this state an activity' — the axiom's per-entity (activity_id, status, outcome) tracking. §6 ('Activities: Coping without Atomicity'): 'The uncertainty of the outcome is held in the business semantics rather than in the record lock... This is simply workflow', and 'Performing Tentative Business Operations' / 'Tentative Operations, Confirmation, and Cancellation': 'the entity that requested the tentative operation decides it is not going to proceed forward. That is a cancelling operation. When the right to cancel is released, that is a confirming operation. Every tentative operation eventually confirms or cancels.' That is the axiom's 'initiator sends cancel/compensate' clause. FALSIFIABLE: a workflow with no per-partner activity state cannot answer 'did my partner already do this?' after a retry or partial failure. Locator error: cited '§4: Workflows and Activities'; §4 is 'Entities, SOA, and Objects' — the workflow material is §5-§6. Fidelity caveat: Helland frames agreement as a web of two-party relationships (his escrow example is hub-and-spoke), which is looser than the axiom's single 'initiator receiving status from all participants'.

prompt_injection: Serializability invariant (Bernstein, Hadzilacos, Goodman 1987, Chapter 2): The fundamental correctness criterion for concurrent transactions is serializability. A concurrent execution is correct if and only if it is equivalent to some serial execution — meaning every transaction sees the same database state it would see if transactions ran one after another. Conflict serializability is the practical implementation: a schedule is conflict-serializable if its conflict graph is acyclic. The invariant: any transaction schedule that violates serializability is incorrect and may produce corrupted data.
source_document: Bernstein, P.A., Hadzilacos, V., & Goodman, N. (1987). Concurrency Control and Recovery in Database Systems. Addison-Wesley. Chapter 2: Serializability Theory.
tensor_equation:
∀schedule H (history of concurrent operations): H is serializable ⇔ ∃serial schedule S such that H ≡ S
where H ≡ S means: ∀transaction T: T observes the same database state in H as in S
Conflict serializability: H is conflict-equivalent to some serial schedule (sufficient, efficiently checkable)
title: Serializability: Concurrent execution MUST be equivalent to some serial execution (Bernstein, Hadzilacos, Goodman 1987)
verdict_evidence: Bernstein, Hadzilacos & Goodman, 'Concurrency Control and Recovery in Database Systems' (Addison-Wesley 1987), Chapter 2 'Serializability Theory' — the cited chapter is correct, and it covers histories, serializable histories, the Serializability Theorem, view equivalence, and recoverability. The axiom's third clause is exactly BHG's position: conflict serializability is the efficiently checkable (via acyclicity of the serialization graph) sufficient condition, strictly contained in view serializability. FALSIFIABLE: the classic non-SR history r1(x) w2(x) w2(y) c2 w1(y) c1 has a cyclic SG and is equivalent to no serial order. Fidelity caveat, noted not disputed: BHG define serializability over the COMMITTED PROJECTION C(H), not over H itself; as literally written, a history containing an active or aborted transaction would be judged non-serializable, which is not BHG's definition. The equivalence relation '≡' is also stated informally (BHG give final-state and view equivalence precisely).

prompt_injection: Recoverability invariant (Bernstein, Hadzilacos, Goodman 1987, Chapter 3): Recoverability is non-negotiable. A committed transaction must never have read data written by an uncommitted or aborted transaction. If T₁ reads x from T₂, then T₁ can only commit after T₂ commits. This invariant is absolute — unlike serializability, which can be relaxed (e.g., snapshot isolation), recoverability violations cause immediate database corruption when a transaction aborts. The invariant: any committed transaction's reads were from committed data only.
source_document: Bernstein, P.A., Hadzilacos, V., & Goodman, N. (1987). Concurrency Control and Recovery. Chapter 3: Recoverability.
tensor_equation:
∀transaction T₁ that reads data item x written by T₂: commit(T₁) ⇒ commit(T₂) before commit(T₁)
∀schedule H: H is recoverable ⇔ ∀T₁ reads x from T₂: T₁ commits only after T₂ commits
Recoverability CANNOT be relaxed — violating it leads to immediate database corruption on abort
title: Recoverability: No committed transaction may read data written by an aborted transaction (Bernstein, Hadzilacos, Goodman 1987)
verdict_evidence: BHG 1987 define recoverable (RC), avoiding-cascading-aborts (ACA) and strict (ST) histories in Chapter 2 (Serializability Theory), which per the book's own structure covers 'Recoverability and its variants'. The definition matches: H is recoverable iff whenever T1 reads x from T2, c2 precedes c1. FALSIFIABLE and consequential: the history w2(x) r1(x) c1 a2 is non-recoverable — T1 has committed on a value that never existed, and no undo of T2 can repair it without unilaterally un-committing T1. That concrete execution is what makes 'cannot be relaxed' true rather than rhetorical: RC is the minimum condition under which abort is implementable at all. Locator error: the citation says 'Chapter 3: Recoverability'; Chapter 3 of BHG is Two-Phase Locking — recoverability is Chapter 2. Precision note: recoverability does not forbid dirty reads; it forbids COMMITTING on one, which is why the title's phrasing ('no committed transaction may read from an aborted transaction') is a consequence rather than the definition.

prompt_injection: Strict Two-Phase Locking invariant (Bernstein, Hadzilacos, Goodman 1987, Chapter 4): In SS2PL, a transaction holds ALL its locks until it commits or aborts. No lock is released before the transaction ends. This is the most common concurrency control mechanism in production databases. SS2PL guarantees both conflict serializability AND recoverability. The invariant: the lock release point is always after the commit/abort decision. Any lock released before transaction end creates a window for recoverability violations.
source_document: Bernstein, P.A., Hadzilacos, V., & Goodman, N. (1987). Concurrency Control and Recovery. Chapter 4: Two-Phase Locking.
tensor_equation: ∀transaction T, ∀lock L acquired by T: release(L) occurs after commit(T) ∨ abort(T). (The lock point does not guarantee commit — T may abort after acquiring its last lock due to deadlock, validation failure, or other reasons.)
title: Strict Two-Phase Locking: Release all locks only after the transaction ends (Bernstein, Hadzilacos, Goodman 1987)
verdict_discrepancy: Two fidelity failures. (1) The second clause is FALSE: 'after lock point, T is guaranteed to commit (barring crash)' does not follow from 2PL and BHG do not claim it. Counterexample: T acquires its last lock, then the application issues ROLLBACK, or a deferred integrity constraint fails at commit time, or the operator kills the session — T aborts after its lock point with no crash involved. The lock point guarantees only a position in the equivalent serial order, not commitment. (2) Level confusion: what the equation defines ('release ALL locks only after the transaction ends') is Strong Strict 2PL / rigorousness, whereas BHG's Strict 2PL requires only that WRITE locks be held to termination; read locks may be released in the shrinking phase. The axiom's title says 'Strict Two-Phase Locking' while its body states SS2PL, then asserts the two are the same in the third clause.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: Atomicity invariant (Bernstein, Hadzilacos, Goodman 1987, Chapter 1): A transaction is atomic — either all of its operations take effect, or none do. There is no intermediate state visible to any other transaction. This is the 'A' in ACID. The invariant: after a transaction completes, the database state is either the state after all operations (commit) or the state before any operation (abort). No client ever observes a partially-executed transaction.
source_document: Bernstein, P.A., Hadzilacos, V., & Goodman, N. (1987). Concurrency Control and Recovery. Chapter 1: Transaction Model — Atomicity.
tensor_equation: ∀transaction T = [op₁, op₂, ..., opₙ]: state_after(T) = state_after(commit(T)) ∨ state_after(T) = state_before(T). commit(T) is atomic: all effects take effect or none do. (The absence of partial execution visibility is ISOLATION, not atomicity.)
title: Atomicity: All-or-nothing — either all operations of a transaction persist or none do (Bernstein, Hadzilacos, Goodman 1987)
verdict_discrepancy: Clause 2 — '∀T: ¬∃partial execution of T visible to any other transaction' — is ISOLATION, not atomicity, and is not entailed by the atomicity BHG define. Counterexample: history w1(x) r2(x) c2 ... c1 under a non-strict scheduler. T1 executes atomically (it commits as a unit, and had it aborted every one of its writes would have been undone), yet T2 observed a partial execution of T1. Atomicity is preserved; the axiom's clause 2 is violated. Real systems make this concrete: READ UNCOMMITTED exposes exactly this while remaining fully atomic. Additionally, clause 3 ('effects become durable') imports Durability into an axiom titled Atomicity. The axiom conflates three of the four ACID letters, which is the standard folklore misquotation the fidelity test is meant to catch.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: ZooKeeper session guarantee (Hunt et al. 2010, USENIX §2.4): A client session is maintained by periodic heartbeats. If a client fails to heartbeat within its session timeout, the session expires and all ephemeral znodes created under that session are automatically deleted. This is ZooKeeper's mechanism for detecting client failures and releasing resources. The invariant: ephemeral znodes exist only while the creating client's session is active. This is the foundation for implementing distributed locks, group membership, and leader election on ZooKeeper.
source_document: Hunt, P. et al. (2010). ZooKeeper: Wait-free Coordination. USENIX ATC '10. §2.4: Sessions and Watches.
tensor_equation:
∀client C with session timeout T: C must send heartbeat within T to maintain session
∀session S that expires: all ephemeral znodes created under S are automatically deleted
∀session S: session ID is unique and monotonically increases across client reconnects
Session state (watches, ephemeral nodes) is maintained across server failover within the session timeout
title: ZooKeeper Session Guarantee: Client session is maintained by heartbeat; session expiry triggers cleanup of ephemeral nodes (Hunt et al. 2010)
verdict_discrepancy: Clause 3 — 'session ID is unique and monotonically increases across client reconnects' — is wrong and internally contradicts clause 4. A ZooKeeper session ID is assigned once at session creation and is CONSTANT across reconnects; that constancy is precisely what lets ephemeral nodes and watches survive a move to another server (clause 4). Counterexample: client disconnects from server A and reconnects to server B within the timeout; the client presents the same session ID and last-seen zxid and continues — the id does not increase, it is deliberately unchanged. A new (larger) id is issued only when a NEW session is created, at which point the old session's ephemerals are destroyed. The paper makes no monotonicity claim about session identifiers.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: Chubby master election invariant (Burrows 2006, OSDI §5): Chubby's primary use case is master election. Clients compete for a lock file; the client that holds the lock is the master. Because Chubby guarantees mutual exclusion (at most one holder per lock), exactly one client is the master at any time. The master stores its identity and serving metadata in Chubby files so that clients can discover the current master. The invariant: Chubby's lock guarantees directly translate to election guarantees — no split-brain.
source_document: Burrows, M. (2006). The Chubby Lock Service. OSDI '06. §5: Use as a Name Service and Master Election.
tensor_equation: ∀election for master role M: |{client C | holds_lock(C, master_lock_for(M))}| ≤ 1. (Not = 1 — the lock may be free. Chubby cannot provide exactly one due to the possibility of a stale lock handle.)
title: Chubby Master Election: Use lock acquisition for master election; exactly one client holds the master lock at any time (Burrows 2006)
verdict_discrepancy: Hedge-hardening: the title asserts 'exactly one client holds the master lock at any time', which the equation itself contradicts (≤ 1, not = 1) and which Chubby cannot provide. Counterexample from the paper: when a master's session lapses, §2.4's lock-delay (typically one minute) blocks ANY client from acquiring the lock, so for that interval zero clients hold it and the service has no master — 'exactly one at any time' is false. A second counterexample: during a Chubby cell fail-over no acquisition succeeds at all. The axiom also drops the sequencer/lock-delay precondition Burrows requires before a lock holder's writes to an external resource may be trusted, so 'the client holding the master lock is the master' is safe only for participants that validate sequencers.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: corrected over-hardening

prompt_injection: Atomic publish-and-update invariant (derived from Garcia-Molina & Salem 1987 Saga model and Richardson's microservices patterns): Each saga step must atomically update its local database and publish the event that triggers the next step. The invariant: it must never be possible for the database to be updated without the event being published (the saga stalls), or for the event to be published without the database being updated (the next step sees stale data). This is achieved via the Transactional Outbox pattern (event row in same DB transaction) or Event Sourcing (the event IS the state change).
source_document: Garcia-Molina, H. & Salem, K. (1987). Sagas. ACM SIGMOD 1987. / Richardson, C. Microservices Patterns: Saga.
tensor_equation: ∀saga step Tᵢ: update_db(Tᵢ) and publish_event(Tᵢ) SHOULD be atomic. Achieved via Transactional Outbox pattern: event row in same database transaction. (The Transactional Outbox and Event Sourcing patterns are from Richardson 2018, not the 1987 Sagas paper.)
title: Saga Atomic Publish-and-Update: Each local transaction MUST atomically update its database AND publish the next event/message (Garcia-Molina & Salem 1987 / Richardson)
verdict_discrepancy: Anachronistic attribution. The Transactional Outbox and Event Sourcing patterns, and the framing 'each local transaction must atomically update its database AND publish the next event', are Richardson's microservices-era formulation, not Garcia-Molina & Salem 1987 — the 1987 model has no messaging layer at all, it is a centralized DBMS with a shared log and a saga daemon. Citing both sources for one equation launders a modern pattern into a 1987 primary source. The claim should be attributed to Richardson (Microservices Patterns) alone, and the 1987 citation dropped. Independently, the equation is stated without its failure model: without an outbox or event store, '¬(db_updated ∧ event_not_published)' is unachievable across a database and a broker — that impossibility is the pattern's motivation, but the axiom asserts the guarantee as if it held unconditionally.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: Presumed Commit optimization (Mohan, Lindsay, Obermarck 1986): The coordinator can forget about a committed transaction after receiving acknowledgment from ALL participants. This eliminates the need for the coordinator to retain log records indefinitely for committed transactions. The invariant: if ANY participant has not yet acknowledged the COMMIT, the coordinator MUST retain the log record to respond to status inquiries. Once all ACKs are received, the coordinator can safely discard the record — any participant inquiry will be met with 'no record, presumed abort, but here's my force-logged COMMIT record.'
source_document: Mohan, C., Lindsay, B., & Obermarck, R. (1986). Transaction Management in R*. ACM TODS 11(4). §4: Presumed Abort and Presumed Commit.
tensor_equation:
∀transaction T: after coordinator has received COMMIT_ACK from all participants: coordinator can discard T's log records
∀participant P that has not yet acknowledged COMMIT: coordinator must retain T's log record to respond to P's inquiry
∀participant P with no log record for T: P assumes ABORT (Presumed Abort) and queries coordinator
title: R* Presumed Commit: Coordinator can forget COMMIT decisions after all participants acknowledge; ACK from all participants removes need for coordinator log retention (Mohan et al. 1986)
verdict_discrepancy: The axiom is titled 'R* Presumed Commit' but its body describes plain 2PC/Presumed-Abort behaviour, and its third clause states the Presumed ABORT rule outright ('∀participant P with no log record for T: P assumes ABORT'). That is the exact opposite of what Presumed Commit means. Counterexample against the axiom as a description of PC: under PC the coordinator does NOT wait for COMMIT_ACKs before discarding — commits are unacknowledged, and it is the ABORT case that requires ACKs before the coordinator may forget; a recovering participant with no record presumes COMMIT, not ABORT. The axiom also omits PC's defining cost, the forced collecting record written before the PREPARE broadcast. As written this is either a duplicate of AX-ORACLE-DTXN-004 (Presumed Abort) under a wrong title, or a false statement of PC.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

title: DFA Determinism
tensor_equation:
∀ regex R: ∃ DFA D such that L(R) = L(D)
∀ state q of D, ∀ character c: |δ(q, c)| ≤ 1 (determinism)
source: Dragon Book, Chapters 3.6-3.7 (Thompson construction + subset construction); Cooper & Torczon, Chapter 2.4
counterexample: A regex-to-DFA compiler that produces a DFA with a state q and character c where δ(q, c) has two or more transitions (nondeterminism). For example, the regex `a|b` compiled to a DFA where state 0 has two transitions on 'a' — one to accept and one to a dead state — would violate the invariant because the DFA would have ambiguous behavior on input 'a'.
verdict_evidence: Extracted from cached source text at axioms/source-cache/compiler-invariants-oracle.md — source verified on disk

title: Longest-Match (Maximal Munch)
tensor_equation:
∀ input string w, ∀ token patterns {p₁, ..., pₙ}:
  lex(w) = the token t such that:
    (1) w = prefix·w' and prefix ∈ L(p_t)
    (2) ¬∃ longer prefix': |prefix'| > |prefix| ∧ prefix' ∈ L(p_j) for some j
source: Dragon Book, Chapter 3.8; Cooper & Torczon, Chapter 2.5
counterexample: In a language with keywords `for` and identifier pattern `[a-z]+`, the input "for" should yield a FOR keyword token, not three identifier tokens "f", "o", "r". A lexer that stops at the first match (matching "f" as identifier, then "o", then "r") violates the longest-match invariant because "for" is a longer valid prefix matching the keyword pattern.
verdict_evidence: Extracted from cached source text at axioms/source-cache/compiler-invariants-oracle.md — source verified on disk

title: Lexer Halt
tensor_equation:
∀ input string w of length n: lex(w) halts in O(n) character reads
∀ position i in w: the DFA advances at most once per character consumed
source: Dragon Book, Chapter 3.4 (DFA simulation); Cooper & Torczon, Chapter 2.6
counterexample: A lexer that backtracks on each character, scanning to the end of a long identifier multiple times, would process O(n²) characters instead of O(n). For example, a 10MB input of continuous letters ('a' repeated 10M times) causing repeated re-scans from the start of each failed token attempt would consume >10M character reads, violating the linear-time invariant.
verdict_evidence: Extracted from cached source text at axioms/source-cache/compiler-invariants-oracle.md — source verified on disk

title: Language Recognition Correctness
tensor_equation:
∀ input string w: w ∈ L(G) ⇔ parse(w) returns a valid parse tree / AST
∀ input string w: w ∉ L(G) ⇔ parse(w) returns an error (no false positives, no false negatives)
source: Dragon Book, Chapter 4.2 (CFG definition, ambiguity, parse trees); Cooper & Torczon, Chapter 3.2
counterexample: An LR parser with a corrupted parsing table entry where action[state, lookahead] incorrectly shifts for an input that is not in the grammar's language, producing a false positive (accepting an invalid program). For example, a parser for a balanced-parentheses grammar that accepts the string "(()" because a table entry erroneously reduces instead of reporting an error.
verdict_evidence: Extracted from cached source text at axioms/source-cache/compiler-invariants-oracle.md — source verified on disk

title: Parse Tree Uniqueness for Unambiguous Grammars
tensor_equation:
∀ unambiguous grammar G, ∀ w ∈ L(G):
  ∃! parse tree T such that yield(T) = w ∧ root(T) = S
source: Dragon Book, Chapter 4.2-4.3; Cooper & Torczon, Chapter 3.3
counterexample: The dangling-else problem in C: `if (a) if (b) c(); else d();` can be parsed as `if (a) { if (b) c(); } else d();` or `if (a) { if (b) c(); else d(); }` — two parse trees for the same string. This is a classic ambiguity in grammars without explicit disambiguation rules for the else clause.
verdict_evidence: Extracted from cached source text at axioms/source-cache/compiler-invariants-oracle.md — source verified on disk

title: LR Shift/Reduce Determinism
tensor_equation:
∀ LR(1) parsing table action[state, lookahead]:
  |action[state, lookahead]| ≤ 1  (at most one shift or reduce, never both)
∀ state with a reduce item A → α· {x}: no other action for that state and lookahead x
source: Dragon Book, Chapter 4.5-4.7 (LR parsing algorithms); Cooper & Torczon, Chapter 3.5
counterexample: An LALR(1) table merger that introduces a reduce/reduce conflict: state S has items A → α· {a} and B → β· {a} from two different LR(1) states that were merged, producing two reduce actions for the same lookahead 'a'. The parser would not know which reduction to perform, causing nondeterministic behavior.
verdict_evidence: Extracted from cached source text at axioms/source-cache/compiler-invariants-oracle.md — source verified on disk

title: FIRST/FOLLOW Fixed Point
tensor_equation:
∀ grammar symbol X:
  FIRST(X) = {a | X ⇒* aα} ∪ {ε if X ⇒* ε}   (least fixed point)
  FOLLOW(A) = {a | S ⇒* αAaβ} ∪ {$ if S ⇒* αA} (least fixed point)
  Nullable(X) ⇔ X ⇒* ε                         (least fixed point)
source: Dragon Book, Chapter 4.4; Cooper & Torczon, Chapter 3.4
counterexample: A grammar with left recursion A → A a | b. If FIRST iteration is not monotone (shrinks instead of growing), the algorithm may claim FIRST(A) = ∅ when in fact FIRST(A) = {b}. The iteration would diverge from the least fixed point because the transfer function is not monotone in the lattice of terminal sets.
verdict_evidence: Extracted from cached source text at axioms/source-cache/compiler-invariants-oracle.md — source verified on disk

title: Type Preservation Under Evaluation
tensor_equation:
∀ expression e, ∀ typing environment Γ:
  Γ ⊢ e : T  ⇒  eval(e) produces a value of type T ∨ diverges ∨ raises a defined exception
  (No "method not found," "cannot add int and string," or nil-deref from well-typed code)
source: Appel, Chapter 5 (Semantic Analysis, type-checking visitor); Dragon Book, Chapter 6.3 (type checking)
counterexample: A program that passes the type checker but at runtime calls `"hello".unknownMethod()` and crashes with "method not found" — the type system failed to reject a method call on a type that doesn't support that method. This violates the preservation guarantee that well-typed programs do not produce type errors at runtime.
verdict_evidence: Extracted from cached source text at axioms/source-cache/compiler-invariants-oracle.md — source verified on disk

title: Symbol Resolution (Declaration Before Use)
tensor_equation:
∀ identifier use at program point p in scope S:
  ∃! declaration D such that D is the innermost declaration of that identifier visible at p
  ∧ D is lexically before p (or in a mutually recursive group with p)
source: Dragon Book, Chapter 6.2 (symbol tables); Appel, Chapter 5; Cooper & Torczon, Chapter 4
counterexample: A program `{ int x = 1; { int x = 2; print(x); } }` where the inner `x` use resolves to the outer declaration (value 1) instead of the inner one (value 2), violating lexical scoping. The most-closely-nested scope rule requires that the inner declaration shadow the outer one.
verdict_evidence: Extracted from cached source text at axioms/source-cache/compiler-invariants-oracle.md — source verified on disk

title: Type Unification (Structural Type Equality)
tensor_equation:
∀ types T₁, T₂:
  compatible(T₁, T₂) ⇔
    T₁ = T₂ (base types equal)
    ∨ T₁ = [N]T₁' ∧ T₂ = [M]T₂' ∧ compatible(T₁', T₂') (array structural equality)
    ∨ T₁ = record{fᵢ: Sᵢ} ∧ T₂ = record{gⱼ: Tⱼ} ∧ ∀fᵢ: ∃!gⱼ with same name ∧ compatible(Sᵢ, Tⱼ)
    ∨ T₁ = T₁' → S₁ ∧ T₂ = T₂' → S₂ ∧ compatible(T₁', T₂') ∧ compatible(S₁, S₂)
    ∨ T₁ <: T₂ ∨ T₂ <: T₁ (subtype polymorphic unification)
source: Appel, Chapter 5; Dragon Book, Chapter 6.3; Pierce TAPL (2002) for subtyping
counterexample: A recursive type unification `X = X → int` without an occurs check would loop infinitely. The unification algorithm must detect this and report a type error rather than diverging. A naive unification that does not implement the occurs check would enter an infinite recursion when attempting to unify X with X → int.
verdict_evidence: Extracted from cached source text at axioms/source-cache/compiler-invariants-oracle.md — source verified on disk

title: Expression Type Inference (Bottom-Up)
source: Dragon Book, Chapter 6.3-6.4 (type expressions, type checking of expressions); Appel, Chapter 5
counterexample: A program `1 + "hello"` in a language without implicit coercion from string to int. If the type checker incorrectly infers int for the result (instead of rejecting with a type error), the expression would violate the invariant. The type checker must reject the addition because the operand type (string) is incompatible with the operator's expected type (int).
verdict_evidence: Extracted from cached source text at axioms/source-cache/compiler-invariants-oracle.md — source verified on disk

title: AST-to-IR Preservation of Semantics
tensor_equation:
∀ program P, ∀ input i:
  eval_AST(P, i) = eval_IR(translate(P), i)
  (The IR faithfully represents the original program's semantics)
source: Dragon Book, Chapter 6.4-6.6 (three-address code); Appel, Chapter 7 (translation to IR trees); Cooper & Torczon, Chapter 5
counterexample: An AST-to-IR translator that reorders evaluation of operands in `a + b` such that `b` is evaluated before `a`, but the AST specifies left-to-right evaluation order. If `a` has side effects (e.g., printing a value), the IR translation would produce different output. For example, `print(1) + print(2)` would print "12" in the AST but "21" in the IR.
verdict_evidence: Extracted from cached source text at axioms/source-cache/compiler-invariants-oracle.md — source verified on disk

title: Basic Block Partition
tensor_equation:
∀ CFG (V, E, entry, exit):
  V is partitioned into basic blocks B₁, ..., Bₖ such that:
    ∀ block B: B = [i₁, i₂, ..., iₙ] where:
      (1) Only iₙ may be a branch/jump (last instruction)
      (2) Only i₁ may be a branch target (first instruction, reached only via label)
      (3) No other instruction in B is a branch or branch target
      (4) ∀ edge (Bᵢ, Bⱼ) ∈ E: the last instruction of Bᵢ can transfer to the first instruction of Bⱼ
source: Dragon Book, Chapter 8.4 (basic blocks and flow graphs); Appel, Chapter 8 (basic blocks and traces); Muchnick, Chapter 7
counterexample: A basic block containing a branch instruction in the middle (not at the end), such as: `add r1, r2, r3; beq r1, r0, L1; add r4, r5, r6` — the branch at instruction 2 means instructions after it are unreachable from the block entry, violating the maximal straight-line property. The instructions after the branch should be in a new basic block.
verdict_evidence: Extracted from cached source text at axioms/source-cache/compiler-invariants-oracle.md — source verified on disk

title: Dominator Tree Correctness
tensor_equation:
∀ CFG with entry node r:
  DOM(n) = {m | every path from r to n includes m}
  ∀ n ≠ r: r ∈ DOM(n)  (entry dominates every node)
  ∀ n: n ∈ DOM(n)      (reflexivity)
  ∀ n ≠ r: |IDOM(n)| = 1  (every non-entry node has exactly one immediate dominator)
  The dominator relation forms a rooted tree with r as root
source: Cooper & Torczon, Chapter 9.2 (dominance); Muchnick, Chapter 7; Dragon Book, Chapter 8.4
counterexample: A CFG where node n has two immediate dominators (|IDOM(n)| = 2) because the dominator computation incorrectly merged two paths. For example, in a CFG with entry → A → C and entry → B → C, if the algorithm claims both A and B are immediate dominators of C, the IDOM relation is not a tree (each node must have exactly one immediate dominator).
verdict_evidence: Extracted from cached source text at axioms/source-cache/compiler-invariants-oracle.md — source verified on disk

title: Single Definition, Dominating Definition
tensor_equation:
∀ SSA-form program:
  ∀ variable v, ∀ use of v at instruction i:
    ∃! definition d of v such that d dominates i
    (d is the unique reaching definition; no ambiguity)
source: Cytron, Ferrante, Rosen, Wegman, Zadeck "Efficiently Computing Static Single Assignment Form and the Control Dependence Graph" (ACM TOPLAS 1991); Cooper & Torczon, Chapter 9.3; Muchnick, Chapter 7
counterexample: A program where variable `x` is defined twice (two different SSA names accidentally assigned the same name) and a use of `x` has two reaching definitions, neither dominating the other. For example, after an if-else merge, the φ-function is missing, so the use after the merge can reach both definitions in the two branches without a unique dominating definition.
verdict_evidence: Extracted from cached source text at axioms/source-cache/compiler-invariants-oracle.md — source verified on disk

title: Dominance Frontier and φ-Placement
tensor_equation:
∀ variable v with definitions at nodes D = {d₁, ..., dₖ}:
  A φ-function for v is placed at node n ⇔ n ∈ DF⁺(D)
  (the iterated dominance frontier of all definition sites)

Where DF⁺(D) = the fixed point of:
  DF₁ = DF(D)
  DF_{i+1} = DF(D ∪ DF_i) ∪ DF_i
source: Cytron et al. (1991); Cooper & Torczon, Chapter 9.3; Muchnick, Chapter 7.4
counterexample: A variable `x` defined in two different loop bodies that merge at a loop exit. If the φ-function is placed at the loop exit block but the iterated dominance frontier also requires a φ at an inner block, the missing φ at the inner block would cause a use of `x` after the loop to have no dominating definition, violating the SSA invariant.
verdict_evidence: Extracted from cached source text at axioms/source-cache/compiler-invariants-oracle.md — source verified on disk

title: Chordal Interference Graph (SSA Property)
source: Hack, Grund, Goos "Register Allocation for Programs in SSA Form" (CC 2006); Pereira & Palsberg "Register Allocation via Coloring of Chordal Graphs" (APLAS 2005); Cooper & Torczon, Chapter 13
counterexample: A program NOT in SSA form (with multiple assignments to the same variable) produces an interference graph with a chordless 4-cycle: variables a, b, c, d where a interferes with b, b with c, c with d, d with a, but no other interferences. The graph is not chordal, making optimal coloring NP-hard instead of O(|V|+|E|).
verdict_evidence: Extracted from cached source text at axioms/source-cache/compiler-invariants-oracle.md — source verified on disk

title: Monotone Framework Convergence
tensor_equation:
∀ dataflow analysis (L, ⊓, F) where:
  L is a finite-height semilattice with top ⊤ and bottom ⊥
  F = {f₁, ..., fₙ} are monotone transfer functions: x ⊑ y ⇒ f(x) ⊑ f(y)

Then the iterative algorithm:
  IN[n] = ⊓_{p ∈ preds(n)} OUT[p]
  OUT[n] = fₙ(IN[n])
converges to the MFP (maximal fixed point) solution in O(|V| × |L|) iterations.
source: Dragon Book, Chapter 9.3 (iterative dataflow analysis); Muchnick, Chapter 11; Cooper & Torczon, Chapter 9
counterexample: A dataflow analysis with a non-monotone transfer function where f(x) does not preserve the lattice ordering. For example, a transfer function that maps ⊤ (unknown) to a concrete value but maps a more-precise lattice element to ⊤, violating monotonicity and causing the solver to oscillate between two states without ever converging to a fixed point.
verdict_evidence: Extracted from cached source text at axioms/source-cache/compiler-invariants-oracle.md — source verified on disk

title: Reaching Definitions Correctness
tensor_equation:
∀ definition d of variable v:
  d ∈ RD_in(p) ⇔ ∃ path from d to p along which v is not redefined
  d ∉ RD_in(p) ⇔ ∀ paths from d to p: v is redefined before reaching p
source: Dragon Book, Chapter 9.2; Muchnick, Chapter 11; Cooper & Torczon, Chapter 9
counterexample: A program with a loop where a variable `x` is defined inside the loop body. The reaching definitions analysis must correctly model that the loop may execute zero times, and a definition of `x` before the loop reaches the loop exit. An incorrect kill/gen that marks `x` as killed by the loop body (even when the loop may not execute) would miss this reaching definition.
verdict_evidence: Extracted from cached source text at axioms/source-cache/compiler-invariants-oracle.md — source verified on disk

title: Liveness is the Dual of Reaching Definitions
tensor_equation:
∀ variable v, ∀ program point p:
  v is live-out at p ⇔ ∃ use of v at q reachable from p along a v-clear path
  v is dead at p ⇔ no use of v is reachable from p (or every path redefines v before any use)
source: Dragon Book, Chapter 9.2; Appel, Chapter 10; Muchnick, Chapter 11
counterexample: A program with a dead store: `x = 1; x = 2; print(x)`. If liveness analysis reports that `x` after `x = 1` is live (i.e., the value of `x` after the first assignment is used in the future), but actually the first assignment to `x` is dead because `x = 2` overwrites it before the only use. A buggy liveness analysis that reports `x` as live at `x = 1` would prevent DCE from removing the dead store.
verdict_evidence: Extracted from cached source text at axioms/source-cache/compiler-invariants-oracle.md — source verified on disk

title: Semantics Preservation (The Prime Directive)
tensor_equation:
∀ optimization pass P, ∀ program π, ∀ input i:
  ⟦P(π)⟧(i) = ⟦π⟧(i)
  (The optimized program produces identical observable output)
source: Muchnick, Chapter 10; Dragon Book, Chapter 9.1; Cooper & Torczon, Chapter 10
counterexample: A constant folding pass that replaces `x = 1 / 0` with `x = 0` (since 1/0 is undefined in integer arithmetic) but the program never executes this branch when the condition is false. On a path where the branch IS taken, the original program would crash with a division-by-zero exception, while the optimized program silently produces x=0 — changing observable behavior. The optimized program crashes instead of producing a value.
verdict_evidence: Extracted from cached source text at axioms/source-cache/compiler-invariants-oracle.md — source verified on disk

title: Constant Propagation Soundness
source: Muchnick, Chapter 12; Dragon Book, Chapter 9.4; Cooper & Torczon, Chapter 10
counterexample: A program `int x = foo(); if (false) { use(x); }` where the constant propagation analysis claims `x = 42` at the false branch (because the branch is unreachable) but a different analysis reports the branch as reachable, causing the optimizer to replace `x` with 42 in code that actually executes. The analysis must be sound: claiming a constant only when it is provably constant on ALL paths.
verdict_evidence: Extracted from cached source text at axioms/source-cache/compiler-invariants-oracle.md — source verified on disk

title: Dead Code Elimination Soundness
tensor_equation:
∀ instruction i removed by DCE:
  The value computed by i is dead: no live use of i's destination exists
  ∧ i has no side effects that are observable by the program
  (Pure dead code: compute but never used. Effectful "dead" code: not really dead.)
source: Cooper & Torczon, Chapter 10; Muchnick, Chapter 10; Dragon Book, Chapter 9.4
counterexample: A store instruction `*p = 42` where `p` points to a global variable that is read later. If DCE incorrectly determines that `p` is dead (because the local variable `p` is not used again), but the store through `p` is observable via global variable `x` (aliased by `p`), removing the store changes the program's output. The store has a side effect that is observable, so it cannot be eliminated.
verdict_evidence: Extracted from cached source text at axioms/source-cache/compiler-invariants-oracle.md — source verified on disk

title: Available Expressions Correctness
tensor_equation:
∀ expression e (e.g., x + y) at program point p:
  e ∈ AE_in(p) ⇔ along EVERY path from entry to p:
    (1) e was computed at some point q before p on that path
    (2) neither x nor y was redefined between q and p on that path
source: Dragon Book, Chapter 9.2; Muchnick, Chapter 11; Cooper & Torczon, Chapter 10
counterexample: A program where `x + y` is computed on one path to p but not on another. The available expressions analysis incorrectly claims `x + y` is available at p (must-analysis). A CSE pass then replaces `x + y` with a previously computed temporary, but on the path where `x + y` was never computed, the temporary holds an incorrect value, producing wrong output.
verdict_evidence: Extracted from cached source text at axioms/source-cache/compiler-invariants-oracle.md — source verified on disk

title: Loop-Invariant Code Motion Correctness
tensor_equation:
∀ expression e computed inside loop L:
  e can be hoisted to L's preheader ⇔
    (1) e is loop-invariant: all operands of e are constant or defined outside L
    (2) e dominates all loop exits where e's value is live
    (3) e is in a block that dominates all exits (or guarantee execution)
source: Muchnick, Chapter 14; Dragon Book, Chapter 10; Cooper & Torczon, Chapter 10
counterexample: A loop `for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) { if (i > 0) { x = 1 / y; } }` where `1 / y` is loop-invariant (y is not modified in the loop). Hoisting `1 / y` to the preheader is incorrect if `y = 0` and the loop body never executes the division (because `i > 0` is false on the first iteration) — the original program would not crash, but the hoisted version would divide by zero at the preheader.
verdict_evidence: Extracted from cached source text at axioms/source-cache/compiler-invariants-oracle.md — source verified on disk

title: Interference Graph Correctness
tensor_equation:
∀ register allocation with k available registers (R0 ... R{k-1}):
  ∀ edge (u, v) in interference graph IG:
    live ranges of virtual registers u and v overlap at some program point
    ⇒ u and v cannot be assigned the same physical register
  ∀ valid coloring col: V → {0, ..., k-1}:
    (u, v) ∈ IG ⇒ col(u) ≠ col(v)
source: Chaitin "Register Allocation and Spilling via Graph Coloring" (PLDI 1982); Chaitin et al. "Register Allocation via Coloring" (1981); Muchnick, Chapter 8; Appel, Chapter 11; Cooper & Torczon, Chapter 13
counterexample: Two virtual registers u and v that are simultaneously live at some program point but are assigned the same physical register R0. When the program writes to u through R0, it corrupts v's live value, causing incorrect output downstream. The allocator must assign distinct physical registers to interfering virtual registers.
verdict_evidence: Extracted from cached source text at axioms/source-cache/compiler-invariants-oracle.md — source verified on disk

title: Spill Correctness (Inserted Loads/Stores)
tensor_equation:
∀ spilled virtual register v with spill slot at [fp + offset]:
  ∀ use of v in original IR:
    in modified IR: load v_tmp ← [fp + offset]; use v_tmp
  ∀ definition of v in original IR:
    in modified IR: ... compute result into v_tmp; store [fp + offset] ← v_tmp
  After spill insertion, the program produces identical results to before
source: Muchnick, Chapter 8; Appel, Chapter 11; Cooper & Torczon, Chapter 13
counterexample: A spilled register v where a use of v is NOT preceded by a reload from the spill slot. The program reads stale data from the register (which now holds a different variable's value after register reassignment) instead of the spilled value, producing incorrect output. Every use of a spilled variable must be preceded by a load from its unique spill slot.
verdict_evidence: Extracted from cached source text at axioms/source-cache/compiler-invariants-oracle.md — source verified on disk

title: Coalescing Correctness
tensor_equation:
∀ copy instruction u ← v (or u = v):
  if u and v do not interfere (no edge in IG):
    u and v can be coalesced: assigned the same physical register
    The copy instruction can be eliminated
  After coalescing, the program produces identical results
source: Chaitin et al. "Register Allocation via Coloring" (1981); Appel, Chapter 11; Cooper & Torczon, Chapter 13.2
counterexample: A copy instruction `t1 = t2` where t1 and t2 DO interfere (their live ranges overlap). Coalescing them into the same register would cause t1's value to be overwritten while t2 is still live, producing incorrect results. For example, if t1 is live after t2 is redefined, assigning them the same register would corrupt t1's value.
verdict_evidence: Extracted from cached source text at axioms/source-cache/compiler-invariants-oracle.md — source verified on disk

title: SSA-based Allocation Optimality
tensor_equation:
∀ SSA-form program with interference graph IG:
  IG is chordal (every cycle of length ≥ 4 has a chord)
  ⇒ color(IG) can be found optimally in O(|V| + |E|) using perfect elimination order
  ⇒ If IG is k-colorable, the optimal coloring uses exactly k registers with no unnecessary spilling
  ⇒ After out-of-SSA translation, register pressure may increase; but within SSA, the allocation is optimal
source: Hack, Grund, Goos (CC 2006); Pereira & Palsberg (APLAS 2005); Cooper & Torczon, Chapter 13.4
counterexample: An SSA-based allocator that claims optimality but uses more colors than ω(IG) (the maximum clique size). For a chordal graph, the chromatic number equals the clique number, so using k+1 colors when the maximum clique is size k indicates a suboptimal allocation, proving the allocator does not find the optimal coloring despite the chordal graph guarantee.
verdict_evidence: Extracted from cached source text at axioms/source-cache/compiler-invariants-oracle.md — source verified on disk

title: Tree-Pattern Coverage Completeness
tensor_equation:
∀ IR expression tree T:
  instruction_selection(T) produces a sequence of machine instructions I₁...Iₙ such that:
    (1) Every IR node in T is covered by exactly one instruction pattern
    (2) The instruction sequence computes the same value as T
    (3) The pattern tiles are valid: each tile corresponds to a single machine instruction
source: Dragon Book, Chapter 8.6 (tree-pattern matching); Appel, Chapter 9 (instruction selection); Muchnick, Chapter 7 (code selection)
counterexample: An IR expression tree `+(*(a, b), c)` where the instruction selection tiling covers the `*` node with a MUL instruction but leaves the `+` node uncovered (no instruction pattern selected for the addition). The generated code would compute `a * b` but not add `c`, producing an incorrect result. Every IR node must be covered by exactly one tiling pattern.
verdict_evidence: Extracted from cached source text at axioms/source-cache/compiler-invariants-oracle.md — source verified on disk

title: Instruction Scheduling Dependence Preservation
tensor_equation:
∀ original IR block B = [i₁, i₂, ..., iₙ]:
  Scheduled block B' = [i'₁, i'₂, ..., i'ₙ] is a permutation of B such that:
    ∀ i, j: if i must execute before j (read-after-write, write-after-write, write-after-read):
      position(i) < position(j) in B' (dependence order preserved)
source: Muchnick, Chapter 9; Cooper & Torczon, Chapter 12; Dragon Book, Chapter 10
counterexample: A basic block `r1 = load [addr]; r2 = r1 + 1; store [addr] ← r2` where the scheduler moves `r2 = r1 + 1` before `r1 = load [addr]`, violating the read-after-write (RAW) dependence. The add instruction reads a stale value from r1 (the value before the load), producing incorrect output. The scheduler must preserve all RAW, WAW, and WAR dependences.
verdict_evidence: Extracted from cached source text at axioms/source-cache/compiler-invariants-oracle.md — source verified on disk

title: Composition of Correct Passes
tensor_equation:
∀ sequence of correct passes P₁, P₂, ..., Pₖ (each preserving semantics individually):
  ⟦Pₖ(⋯P₂(P₁(π))⋯)⟧ = ⟦π⟧
  (Composition of semantics-preserving passes is semantics-preserving)
source: Muchnick, Chapter 10; Dragon Book, Chapter 9.1; Cooper & Torczon, Chapter 10
counterexample: Two passes P₁ and P₂ that are each semantics-preserving individually, but P₂'s analysis depends on loop structure and P₁'s transformation introduces a new loop header that P₂ misidentifies. For example, P₁ is loop unrolling and P₂ is LICM — after unrolling, P₂ hoists an expression from a newly created loop that was not previously a loop, and the hoisted expression is only executed on some paths, changing semantics.
verdict_evidence: Extracted from cached source text at axioms/source-cache/compiler-invariants-oracle.md — source verified on disk

title: CFG Reducibility and Loop Structure
tensor_equation:
∀ CFG G:
  G is reducible ⇔ G can be reduced to a single node by repeatedly applying T₁ and T₂ transformations
  where T₁ removes a self-loop and T₂ removes a node with a single predecessor (folding it in)
  If G is reducible: all loops are natural loops (single entry, dominated by header)
counterexample: A CFG created by goto spaghetti: `entry → A → B → C → A` and `entry → D → C` where C has two predecessors (B and D) and D does not dominate C. The back edge A → C creates a loop header C that is not dominated by any single loop header, making the CFG irreducible.
